Example record: {"deviceId":"device-001","ts":"2025-09-28T12:00:00Z","value":23.5} Manifest key details: - Purpose: Dummy telemetry for testing ingestion/ETL. - Expected Azure Blob path prefix: raw/telemetry/ - Recommended blob URI: https://.blob.core.windows.net//raw/telemetry/telemetry.jsonl - Local file: /workspace/telemetry_demo/telemetry.jsonl Optional next step (upload example): - az storage blob upload --account-name --container-name --name raw/telemetry/telemetry.jsonl --file /workspace/telemetry_demo/telemetry.jsonl --auth-mode login Let me know if you prefer a JSON array file instead of JSONL, a different filename, or additional fields (e.g., status, unit, location).

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Confirm Azure AI Search field types from official docs so I choose correct types for name, price, brand. Then create two files in /workspace: records (JSON and JSONL) and index_schema.json. Finally validate JSON with jq and summarize paths and inline snippets. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Cognitive Search field data types Edm.String Edm.Double Edm.Int32 Collection(Edm.String) documentation", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Data types in Azure AI Search","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-query-odata-comparison-operators#data-types"},{"title":"Create an index (REST API) for Azure AI Search","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-create-index-rest-api"},{"title":"Index attributes and field definitions in Azure AI Search","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-what-is-an-index#fields"},{"title":"Field mappings and data types reference","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/index-add-scoring-profiles#field-attributes"},{"title":"Supported data types and properties (Azure Cognitive Search)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-lucene-query-architecture#supported-data-types"}],"summary":"Azure AI Search fields use OData Edm types such as Edm.String, Edm.Int32, Edm.Int64, Edm.Double, Edm.Boolean, Edm.DateTimeOffset, Edm.GeographyPoint, and Collection(Edm.String). Typical attributes include searchable, filterable, sortable, facetable, and retrievable. For a product catalog, name and brand are Edm.String (searchable), and price is Edm.Double (filterable/sortable/facetable). A deployable index also requires one key field (type Edm.String, key:true)."}} Now scaffold a small workspace folder and placeholder files for records and index schema so I can fill them next. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog && touch /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/products.json /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/products.jsonl /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/index_schema.json && ls -la /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 12 drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 . drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 .. -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 Sep 28 00:00 index_schema.json -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 Sep 28 00:00 products.json -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 Sep 28 00:00 products.jsonl ","stderr":""} Write five toy product records into both JSON (array) and JSONL (one per line) formats. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/products.json << 'JSON' [ {"name":"Aurora Mug","price":12.99,"brand":"Nimbus"}, {"name":"Trailblazer Backpack","price":59.50,"brand":"Summit"}, {"name":"Pixel Studio Headphones","price":89.99,"brand":"WaveLabs"}, {"name":"Eco Bamboo Toothbrush","price":3.49,"brand":"GreenLeaf"}, {"name":"Stellar LED Desk Lamp","price":29.00,"brand":"Luma"} ] JSON cat > /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/products.jsonl << 'JSONL' {"name":"Aurora Mug","price":12.99,"brand":"Nimbus"} {"name":"Trailblazer Backpack","price":59.50,"brand":"Summit"} {"name":"Pixel Studio Headphones","price":89.99,"brand":"WaveLabs"} {"name":"Eco Bamboo Toothbrush","price":3.49,"brand":"GreenLeaf"} {"name":"Stellar LED Desk Lamp","price":29.00,"brand":"Luma"} JSONL"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Create a minimal Azure AI Search index_schema.json defining only the requested fields and their types/attributes based on the docs (Edm.String, Edm.Double). {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/index_schema.json << 'JSON' { "name": "products-mini", "fields": [ { "name": "name", "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": true, "filterable": false, "facetable": false, "sortable": true }, { "name": "price", "type": "Edm.Double", "searchable": false, "filterable": true, "facetable": true, "sortable": true }, { "name": "brand", "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": true, "filterable": true, "facetable": true, "sortable": true } ] } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Validate that both JSON files are well-formed and show a quick preview for the user. {"type":"code","code_command":"jq . /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/index_schema.json >/dev/null && jq . /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/products.json >/dev/null && echo "Validated JSON OK" && echo "--- index_schema.json ---" && cat /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/index_schema.json && echo "\n--- products.json ---" && cat /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/products.json"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Validated JSON OK --- index_schema.json --- { "name": "products-mini", "fields": [ { "name": "name", "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": true, "filterable": false, "facetable": false, "sortable": true }, { "name": "price", "type": "Edm.Double", "searchable": false, "filterable": true, "facetable": true, "sortable": true }, { "name": "brand", "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": true, "filterable": true, "facetable": true, "sortable": true } ] } --- products.json --- [ { "name": "Aurora Mug", "price": 12.99, "brand": "Nimbus" }, { "name": "Trailblazer Backpack", "price": 59.5, "brand": "Summit" }, { "name": "Pixel Studio Headphones", "price": 89.99, "brand": "WaveLabs" }, { "name": "Eco Bamboo Toothbrush", "price": 3.49, "brand": "GreenLeaf" }, { "name": "Stellar LED Desk Lamp", "price": 29, "brand": "Luma" } ] ","stderr":""} Summarize final deliverables with file paths and inline content. Mention that a real Azure AI Search index also needs a key field, but we followed the user's request to define the three fields. Here are the five toy product records and a minimal Azure AI Search index schema for fields name, price, and brand. I also saved both to the workspace. Files created - /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/products.json (array) - /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/products.jsonl (JSON Lines) - /workspace/azure_ai_search_catalog/index_schema.json (index fields) Five toy records (JSON array) [ {"name":"Aurora Mug","price":12.99,"brand":"Nimbus"}, {"name":"Trailblazer Backpack","price":59.50,"brand":"Summit"}, {"name":"Pixel Studio Headphones","price":89.99,"brand":"WaveLabs"}, {"name":"Eco Bamboo Toothbrush","price":3.49,"brand":"GreenLeaf"}, {"name":"Stellar LED Desk Lamp","price":29.00,"brand":"Luma"} ] Azure AI Search index_schema.json (minimal fields) { "name": "products-mini", "fields": [ { "name": "name", "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": true, "filterable": false, "facetable": false, "sortable": true }, { "name": "price", "type": "Edm.Double", "searchable": false, "filterable": true, "facetable": true, "sortable": true }, { "name": "brand", "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": true, "filterable": true, "facetable": true, "sortable": true } ] } Notes - Field types are based on Azure AI Search (OData Edm types): Edm.String and Edm.Double. - In a deployable index, you must also include a key field (type Edm.String with "key": true), for example an "id" field. I omitted it here to match your request that only specifies name, price, and brand. - Brand is often filterable/facetable for navigation, name is searchable, and price is filterable/facetable/sortable but not searchable.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text:
Plan: - Create a project directory with data and docs. - Generate customers.csv and orders.csv with 5 data rows each (plus headers) using a code tool call. - Verify files exist and show line counts to confirm 5 data rows each. - Create join_plan.md detailing SQL join logic and where outputs would be saved in Azure Data Lake Gen2 (e.g., /curated/sales/). - List the created files to surface to the user. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/data_join/{data,docs} && ls -R /workspace/data_join"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/data_join: data docs /workspace/data_join/data: /workspace/data_join/docs: ","stderr":""} Create customers.csv with a header and 5 data rows (UTF-8), ensuring proper quoting for special characters. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/data_join/data/customers.csv << 'CSV' customer_id,first_name,last_name,email,country,signup_date 1001,Alice,Nguyen,alice.nguyen@example.com,US,2024-11-15 1002,Bob,"O'Neil",bob.oneil@example.co.uk,UK,2023-07-02 1003,Carlos,Santos,carlos.santos@example.com,BR,2022-03-27 1004,Diana,Kapoor,diana.kapoor@example.in,IN,2025-01-09 1005,Ethan,Müller,ethan.mueller@example.de,DE,2024-05-19 CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Create orders.csv with a header and 5 data rows. Include one order with a non-existent customer_id to demonstrate unmatched handling in join. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/data_join/data/orders.csv << 'CSV' order_id,order_date,customer_id,item,quantity,unit_price,currency 20001,2025-09-20,1001,Wireless Mouse,1,24.99,USD 20002,2025-09-21,1002,USB-C Cable,3,9.49,GBP 20003,2025-09-21,1004,Laptop Stand,1,2999.00,INR 20004,2025-09-22,1006,Noise-Canceling Headphones,1,199.00,EUR 20005,2025-09-23,1003,Webcam,2,129.90,BRL CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Verify the CSVs exist and contain 6 lines each (1 header + 5 data rows). Then preview the head of each file. {"type":"code","code_command":"wc -l /workspace/data_join/data/customers.csv /workspace/data_join/data/orders.csv && echo '--- customers.csv ---' && head -n 6 /workspace/data_join/data/customers.csv && echo '--- orders.csv ---' && head -n 6 /workspace/data_join/data/orders.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":" 6 /workspace/data_join/data/customers.csv 6 /workspace/data_join/data/orders.csv --- customers.csv --- customer_id,first_name,last_name,email,country,signup_date 1001,Alice,Nguyen,alice.nguyen@example.com,US,2024-11-15 1002,Bob,"O'Neil",bob.oneil@example.co.uk,UK,2023-07-02 1003,Carlos,Santos,carlos.santos@example.com,BR,2022-03-27 1004,Diana,Kapoor,diana.kapoor@example.in,IN,2025-01-09 1005,Ethan,Müller,ethan.mueller@example.de,DE,2024-05-19 --- orders.csv --- order_id,order_date,customer_id,item,quantity,unit_price,currency 20001,2025-09-20,1001,Wireless Mouse,1,24.99,USD 20002,2025-09-21,1002,USB-C Cable,3,9.49,GBP 20003,2025-09-21,1004,Laptop Stand,1,2999.00,INR 20004,2025-09-22,1006,Noise-Canceling Headphones,1,199.00,EUR 20005,2025-09-23,1003,Webcam,2,129.90,BRL ","stderr":""} Write join_plan.md describing SQL join logic, including inner, left, and anti joins, partitioning strategy, and ADLS Gen2 output locations under /curated/sales/. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/data_join/docs/join_plan.md << 'MD' Join Plan: Orders ↔ Customers Overview This plan describes how to join orders (fact) with customers (dimension), the primary join keys, data type assumptions, and where the curated outputs will be written in Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2. Sources (landing/raw) - orders.csv (fact): /raw/sales/orders/ (example source file: /workspace/data_join/data/orders.csv) - customers.csv (dim): /raw/sales/customers/ (example source file: /workspace/data_join/data/customers.csv) Keys and Constraints - customers: primary key (customer_id) - orders: primary key (order_id); foreign key orders.customer_id → customers.customer_id (may be missing) Schema Assumptions - customers: (customer_id INT, first_name STRING, last_name STRING, email STRING, country STRING, signup_date DATE) - orders: (order_id INT, order_date DATE, customer_id INT, item STRING, quantity INT, unit_price DECIMAL(18,2), currency STRING) Join Strategy 1) Stage and cast types WITH cust AS ( SELECT CAST(customer_id AS INT) AS customer_id, first_name, last_name, email, country, TO_DATE(signup_date) AS signup_date FROM customers_source ), ord AS ( SELECT CAST(order_id AS INT) AS order_id, TO_DATE(order_date) AS order_date, CAST(customer_id AS INT) AS customer_id, item, CAST(quantity AS INT) AS quantity, CAST(unit_price AS DECIMAL(18,2)) AS unit_price, currency FROM orders_source ) 2) Inner join (enriched orders; only matched rows) CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS curated.sales_orders_enriched USING DELTA PARTITIONED BY (year, month, day) LOCATION 'abfss://curated@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/orders_enriched' AS SELECT o.order_id, o.order_date, YEAR(o.order_date) AS year, MONTH(o.order_date) AS month, DAY(o.order_date) AS day, o.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name, c.email, c.country, o.item, o.quantity, o.unit_price, o.currency FROM ord o JOIN cust c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id; 3) Left join + anti join (data quality and exception handling) - Unmatched orders (customer not found): write for remediation CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS curated.sales_orders_unmatched USING DELTA LOCATION 'abfss://curated@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/orders_unmatched' AS SELECT o.* FROM ord o LEFT ANTI JOIN cust c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id; - Customers without orders (for lifecycle analysis) CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS curated.customers_without_orders USING DELTA LOCATION 'abfss://curated@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/customers_without_orders' AS SELECT c.* FROM cust c LEFT ANTI JOIN ord o ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id; Expected Results with Provided Sample Data - orders.csv has 5 rows; customers.csv has 5 rows. - There is one unmatched order for customer_id = 1006. • curated.sales_orders_enriched (inner join) → 4 rows. • curated.sales_orders_unmatched (anti join) → 1 row (order_id 20004). Data Lake Gen2 Output Layout - Curated, partitioned, Delta-format outputs under the curated container: • abfss://curated@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/orders_enriched/year=/month=/day=
/ • abfss://curated@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/orders_unmatched/ • abfss://curated@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/customers_without_orders/ Notes - Replace and optionally specify // via your compute environment. - If using CSV outputs instead of Delta, substitute USING DELTA with USING CSV and include appropriate OPTIONS (header=true, delimiter=','). - Consider standardizing currency (e.g., to USD) as a downstream step; not required for the join itself. - Ensure customer_id uniqueness in customers; if duplicates exist, deduplicate by latest signup_date or a business rule. - Apply PII handling for email per governance; masks or hashing may be required before publishing to curated. Validation Checks - Row counts: matched + unmatched = orders total. - Null checks: order_id, customer_id must be non-null in curated outputs. - Referential integrity exception count trend (should remain stable or decrease). Appendix: Spark SQL Snippet (reading from data lake) -- Example loading staged data before the join CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS staged.customers USING CSV OPTIONS (header=true) LOCATION 'abfss://raw@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/customers/'; CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS staged.orders USING CSV OPTIONS (header=true) LOCATION 'abfss://raw@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/orders/'; -- Then apply the join statements above to produce curated tables MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} List final artifacts for the user. {"type":"code","code_command":"ls -l /workspace/data_join/data && echo '---' && ls -l /workspace/data_join/docs"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 8 -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 347 Sep 28 00:00 customers.csv -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 330 Sep 28 00:00 orders.csv --- total 4 -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 3662 Sep 28 00:00 join_plan.md ","stderr":""} Created files - /workspace/data_join/data/customers.csv — 5 data rows (plus header) - /workspace/data_join/data/orders.csv — 5 data rows (plus header) - /workspace/data_join/docs/join_plan.md — SQL join logic and ADLS Gen2 output plan Quick previews - customers.csv customer_id,first_name,last_name,email,country,signup_date 1001,Alice,Nguyen,alice.nguyen@example.com,US,2024-11-15 1002,Bob,"O'Neil",bob.oneil@example.co.uk,UK,2023-07-02 1003,Carlos,Santos,carlos.santos@example.com,BR,2022-03-27 1004,Diana,Kapoor,diana.kapoor@example.in,IN,2025-01-09 1005,Ethan,Müller,ethan.mueller@example.de,DE,2024-05-19 - orders.csv order_id,order_date,customer_id,item,quantity,unit_price,currency 20001,2025-09-20,1001,Wireless Mouse,1,24.99,USD 20002,2025-09-21,1002,USB-C Cable,3,9.49,GBP 20003,2025-09-21,1004,Laptop Stand,1,2999.00,INR 20004,2025-09-22,1006,Noise-Canceling Headphones,1,199.00,EUR 20005,2025-09-23,1003,Webcam,2,129.90,BRL Join plan summary (see docs/join_plan.md) - Join key: orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id - Default output (inner join): curated.sales_orders_enriched - Exception outputs: - Unmatched orders (left anti join): curated.sales_orders_unmatched - Customers without orders (left anti join): curated.customers_without_orders - Partitioning: year/month/day from order_date - ADLS Gen2 locations (examples): - abfss://curated@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/orders_enriched/year=/month=/day=
/ - abfss://curated@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/orders_unmatched/ - abfss://curated@.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/customers_without_orders/ Notes - Files are UTF-8 CSV with headers. - One intentional unmatched order (customer_id 1006) showcases exception flow. - Replace with your storage account name, and adjust containers if needed. If you want, I can also generate Spark SQL or Synapse scripts to read these CSVs, perform the join, and write Delta tables to the curated paths.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Plan: Create a new workspace folder with tiny event data (JSONL + CSV) including ISO timestamps. Create ADLS-style partitioned directories under data/events/day=YYYY-MM-DD for two days and put per-day JSONL files. Write partition_strategy.md explaining the day partitioning, list two sample folder paths, and include best practices. Then verify by listing the tree and showing file contents. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e mkdir -p /workspace/adls_partitioning/{data/events,docs} # Create a tiny JSONL of mixed-day events cat > /workspace/adls_partitioning/data/events.jsonl << 'JSONL' {"event_id":"e1","user_id":"u100","event_type":"login","timestamp":"2025-01-15T08:15:30Z","amount":0.0} {"event_id":"e2","user_id":"u101","event_type":"purchase","timestamp":"2025-01-15T12:00:05Z","amount":19.99,"currency":"USD"} {"event_id":"e3","user_id":"u100","event_type":"logout","timestamp":"2025-01-16T01:45:10Z","amount":0.0} {"event_id":"e4","user_id":"u102","event_type":"purchase","timestamp":"2025-01-16T23:59:59Z","amount":3.5,"currency":"USD"} JSONL # CSV equivalent for convenience cat > /workspace/adls_partitioning/data/events.csv << 'CSV' event_id,user_id,event_type,timestamp,amount,currency e1,u100,login,2025-01-15T08:15:30Z,0.0, e2,u101,purchase,2025-01-15T12:00:05Z,19.99,USD e3,u100,logout,2025-01-16T01:45:10Z,0.0, e4,u102,purchase,2025-01-16T23:59:59Z,3.5,USD CSV # Create ADLS-style day partitions and place per-day JSONL shards mkdir -p /workspace/adls_partitioning/data/events/day=2025-01-15 mkdir -p /workspace/adls_partitioning/data/events/day=2025-01-16 cat > /workspace/adls_partitioning/data/events/day=2025-01-15/part-00000.jsonl << 'JSONL' {"event_id":"e1","user_id":"u100","event_type":"login","timestamp":"2025-01-15T08:15:30Z","amount":0.0} {"event_id":"e2","user_id":"u101","event_type":"purchase","timestamp":"2025-01-15T12:00:05Z","amount":19.99,"currency":"USD"} JSONL cat > /workspace/adls_partitioning/data/events/day=2025-01-16/part-00000.jsonl << 'JSONL' {"event_id":"e3","user_id":"u100","event_type":"logout","timestamp":"2025-01-16T01:45:10Z","amount":0.0} {"event_id":"e4","user_id":"u102","event_type":"purchase","timestamp":"2025-01-16T23:59:59Z","amount":3.5,"currency":"USD"} JSONL # Write partitioning strategy note cat > /workspace/adls_partitioning/docs/partition_strategy.md << 'MD' Partitioning Strategy for ADLS (Azure Data Lake Storage) Purpose - Optimize query performance, enable partition pruning, and organize time-series event data. Recommended Layout - Partition by event date using a hive-style key=value folder: day=YYYY-MM-DD. - Example base path: abfss://datalake@.dfs.core.windows.net/raw/events/ Folder Structure - raw/events/ - day=2025-01-15/ - part-00000.jsonl - day=2025-01-16/ - part-00000.jsonl Why day partitioning? - Common analytical granularity, balances number of files per partition, and simplifies backfills and retention. Optional refinement - If data volume per day is very high, add hour=HH under day (two-level partitioning): day=YYYY-MM-DD/hour=HH. File format & size - Prefer columnar (Parquet) for analytics; JSONL acceptable for landing/ingest. - Target 128–1024 MB compressed per file shard in production to avoid small-file issues. - Use compression (snappy for Parquet, gzip for JSONL) when appropriate. Naming conventions - Use hive-style keys: day=YYYY-MM-DD[/hour=HH]. - Use lower-case, hyphen-separated dataset names; avoid spaces & special chars in keys/values. - Shard filenames as part-00000.parquet (or .jsonl) to be compatible with Spark/Hive conventions. Schema & evolution - Keep a registry of schema versions; add fields in a backward-compatible way when possible. - Avoid breaking changes; if needed, write to a new base path (e.g., raw/events_v2). Retention & lifecycle - Configure retention by partition: e.g., keep 90 days in raw, compact to curated tables. Security & governance - Apply ACLs/permissions at the directory level (e.g., per dataset root). Sample partition paths for two different days - abfss://datalake@contoso.dfs.core.windows.net/raw/events/day=2025-01-15/ - abfss://datalake@contoso.dfs.core.windows.net/raw/events/day=2025-01-16/ Local example paths (this repo) - /workspace/adls_partitioning/data/events/day=2025-01-15/ - /workspace/adls_partitioning/data/events/day=2025-01-16/ Querying tips - In Spark: df.where("day = '2025-01-15'") will prune partitions. - In SQL on external table: WHERE day = '2025-01-15' enables pruning. MD # Verify structure printf "\nTree under /workspace/adls_partitioning:\n" && find /workspace/adls_partitioning -maxdepth 4 -type f -o -type d | sed 's#^/workspace##' | sort "}

Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example.
