File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll create a small workspace folder, write a Python script that synthesizes 10 numbers with one clear outlier and flags values > 100, generate outliers.csv by running the script, then add a method_notes.md documenting the approach. After running, I will verify the CSV content. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/outlier_flagger && cat > /workspace/outlier_flagger/flag_outliers.py << 'PY' import csv from pathlib import Path # Synthesize 10 numeric data points (one clear outlier > 100) data = [7, 12, 9, 10, 8, 13, 11, 12, 10, 150] # Fixed threshold for outlier detection THRESHOLD = 100 # Identify outliers: values strictly greater than the threshold outliers = [(idx, val) for idx, val in enumerate(data) if val > THRESHOLD] # Write outliers to CSV (header: index,value) out_path = Path(__file__).with_name('outliers.csv') with out_path.open('w', newline='') as f: writer = csv.writer(f) writer.writerow(["index", "value"]) # header for idx, val in outliers: writer.writerow([idx, val]) print(f"Flagged {len(outliers)} outlier(s) using threshold > {THRESHOLD}.") print(f"Outliers saved to: {out_path}") PY cat > /workspace/outlier_flagger/method_notes.md << 'MD' Title: Simple Threshold-Based Outlier Flagging Data - We synthesized 10 numeric data points: [7, 12, 9, 10, 8, 13, 11, 12, 10, 150] - The value 150 is a clear outlier relative to the others (single value much larger than the typical range ~7–13). Method - Fixed threshold rule: mark any value strictly greater than 100 as an outlier. - Condition: value > 100 - This is a simple, deterministic rule suitable for quick checks; it does not rely on statistical assumptions. Implementation - Script: flag_outliers.py - It evaluates each data point against the threshold and writes the flagged results to outliers.csv with columns: index, value. - Index corresponds to the zero-based position in the list above. Reproducibility - The dataset is hard-coded in the script for deterministic behavior. - Running `python3 flag_outliers.py` will regenerate outliers.csv. Output - outliers.csv contains only the rows that exceeded the threshold. MD python3 /workspace/outlier_flagger/flag_outliers.py && ls -l /workspace/outlier_flagger"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Flagged 1 outlier(s) using threshold > 100. Outliers saved to: /workspace/outlier_flagger/outliers.csv total 16 -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 860 Sep 28 00:00 method_notes.md -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 698 Sep 28 00:00 flag_outliers.py -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 23 Sep 28 00:00 outliers.csv ","stderr":""} Now I'll verify the contents of outliers.csv to ensure it lists exactly one outlier with the correct index and value. {"type":"code","code_command":"echo '--- outliers.csv ---' && cat /workspace/outlier_flagger/outliers.csv && echo '\n--- line count (should be 2: header + 1 outlier) ---' && wc -l /workspace/outlier_flagger/outliers.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"--- outliers.csv --- index,value 9,150 --- line count (should be 2: header + 1 outlier) --- 2 /workspace/outlier_flagger/outliers.csv ","stderr":""} Done. What I created - /workspace/outlier_flagger/flag_outliers.py - Synthesizes 10 numbers with one clear outlier. - Flags outliers using a fixed threshold: value > 100. - Writes outliers to outliers.csv (columns: index,value). - /workspace/outlier_flagger/outliers.csv - Contains the flagged outlier(s). In this dataset: index 9, value 150. - /workspace/outlier_flagger/method_notes.md - Documents the data, threshold rule, implementation, and reproducibility. How to run - cd /workspace/outlier_flagger - python3 flag_outliers.py Method summary - Data: [7, 12, 9, 10, 8, 13, 11, 12, 10, 150] - Rule: outlier if value > 100 - Output: outliers.csv with the indices and values that exceed the threshold If you want the CSV to include all values with a binary flag instead, I can modify the script to output columns value,is_outlier for the full dataset.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I will scaffold a small workspace, write the contract.md specifying allowed status values and validation rules, implement a Python script that creates 10 toy rows and validates them, run it to generate invalid_status_rows.csv, and verify outputs. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/status_validation/{docs,scripts,data,outputs} && printf "Created dirs:\n" && ls -R /workspace/status_validation"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Created dirs: /workspace/status_validation: data docs outputs scripts /workspace/status_validation/data: /workspace/status_validation/docs: /workspace/status_validation/outputs: /workspace/status_validation/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will write the contract.md describing the allowed categorical values for the 'status' column and validation rules (case sensitivity, trimming, null handling, allowed set). {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/status_validation/docs/contract.md << 'MD' Status Column Contract Purpose Defines the allowed categorical values and validation rules for a status column in tabular datasets. Column - Name: status - Type: string (ASCII uppercase, underscores allowed) Allowed Values (enumeration) - PENDING - RUNNING - COMPLETE - FAILED - CANCELED Rules - Exact match only: values must be exactly one of the allowed values. - Case sensitivity: uppercase only; lowercase or mixed case are invalid. - Whitespace: no leading/trailing whitespace; spaces make the value invalid. - Null/empty handling: null, empty string, or only whitespace are invalid. - Encoding: ASCII; avoid non-printable characters. Examples - Valid: PENDING, RUNNING, COMPLETE, FAILED, CANCELED - Invalid: pending, Completed, DONE, IN_PROGRESS, " COMPLETE ", "" Validation Specification (reference) Given a row with field status, the value is valid if and only if: 1) status is a non-empty string, and 2) status == status.strip() (no leading/trailing whitespace), and 3) status is in the allowed set {PENDING, RUNNING, COMPLETE, FAILED, CANCELED}. Expected Outputs - invalid_status_rows.csv: CSV containing all rows from an input set that violate these rules. Change Control - Any change to the allowed set or rules must be versioned and communicated to downstream consumers. MD && echo "Wrote /workspace/status_validation/docs/contract.md" && sed -n '1,200p' /workspace/status_validation/docs/contract.md"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Wrote /workspace/status_validation/docs/contract.md Status Column Contract Purpose Defines the allowed categorical values and validation rules for a status column in tabular datasets. Column - Name: status - Type: string (ASCII uppercase, underscores allowed) Allowed Values (enumeration) - PENDING - RUNNING - COMPLETE - FAILED - CANCELED Rules - Exact match only: values must be exactly one of the allowed values. - Case sensitivity: uppercase only; lowercase or mixed case are invalid. - Whitespace: no leading/trailing whitespace; spaces make the value invalid. - Null/empty handling: null, empty string, or only whitespace are invalid. - Encoding: ASCII; avoid non-printable characters. Examples - Valid: PENDING, RUNNING, COMPLETE, FAILED, CANCELED - Invalid: pending, Completed, DONE, IN_PROGRESS, " COMPLETE ", "" Validation Specification (reference) Given a row with field status, the value is valid if and only if: 1) status is a non-empty string, and 2) status == status.strip() (no leading/trailing whitespace), and 3) status is in the allowed set {PENDING, RUNNING, COMPLETE, FAILED, CANCELED}. Expected Outputs - invalid_status_rows.csv: CSV containing all rows from an input set that violate these rules. Change Control - Any change to the allowed set or rules must be versioned and communicated to downstream consumers. ","stderr":""} I will create a Python script that: (1) generates 10 toy rows, (2) writes them to data/toy_rows.csv, (3) validates against the allowed list, and (4) writes outputs/invalid_status_rows.csv. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/status_validation/scripts/validate_status.py << 'PY' import csv from pathlib import Path # Contract: allowed values ALLOWED = {"PENDING", "RUNNING", "COMPLETE", "FAILED", "CANCELED"} # Prepare directories root = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1] data_dir = root / "data" outputs_dir = root / "outputs" data_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) outputs_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) # 10 toy rows (some valid, some invalid) rows = [ {"id": 1, "status": "PENDING"}, # valid {"id": 2, "status": "RUNNING"}, # valid {"id": 3, "status": "COMPLETE"}, # valid {"id": 4, "status": "FAILED"}, # valid {"id": 5, "status": "CANCELED"}, # valid {"id": 6, "status": "pending"}, # invalid (lowercase) {"id": 7, "status": "DONE"}, # invalid (not in allowed) {"id": 8, "status": "IN_PROGRESS"}, # invalid (not in allowed) {"id": 9, "status": " COMPLETE "}, # invalid (whitespace) {"id": 10, "status": ""}, # invalid (empty) ] # Write toy input input_csv = data_dir / "toy_rows.csv" with input_csv.open("w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f: writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=["id", "status"]) writer.writeheader() writer.writerows(rows) # Validate and collect invalids invalid = [] for r in rows: val = r["status"] if not isinstance(val, str) or val.strip() == "": invalid.append(r) continue if val != val.strip(): invalid.append(r) continue if val not in ALLOWED: invalid.append(r) continue # Write invalid rows invalid_csv = outputs_dir / "invalid_status_rows.csv" with invalid_csv.open("w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f: writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=["id", "status"]) writer.writeheader() writer.writerows(invalid) print(f"Wrote input: {input_csv}") print(f"Wrote invalid rows: {invalid_csv}") print(f"Counts: total={len(rows)}, invalid={len(invalid)}, valid={len(rows)-len(invalid)}") PY && python3 /workspace/status_validation/scripts/validate_status.py"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Wrote input: /workspace/status_validation/data/toy_rows.csv Wrote invalid rows: /workspace/status_validation/outputs/invalid_status_rows.csv Counts: total=10, invalid=5, valid=5 ","stderr":""} List the generated files and preview invalid_status_rows.csv to confirm contents. {"type":"code","code_command":"echo "Workspace tree:" && ls -R /workspace/status_validation && echo "\nPreview invalid_status_rows.csv:" && sed -n '1,200p' /workspace/status_validation/outputs/invalid_status_rows.csv && echo "\nPreview toy_rows.csv:" && sed -n '1,200p' /workspace/status_validation/data/toy_rows.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Workspace tree: /workspace/status_validation: data docs outputs scripts /workspace/status_validation/data: toy_rows.csv /workspace/status_validation/docs: contract.md /workspace/status_validation/outputs: invalid_status_rows.csv /workspace/status_validation/scripts: validate_status.py Preview invalid_status_rows.csv: id,status 6,pending 7,DONE 8,IN_PROGRESS 9, COMPLETE 10, Preview toy_rows.csv: id,status 1,PENDING 2,RUNNING 3,COMPLETE 4,FAILED 5,CANCELED 6,pending 7,DONE 8,IN_PROGRESS 9, COMPLETE 10, ","stderr":""} Everything is in place. I will finish by summarizing the created files and how to run validation. Done. What I created - Contract file: - /workspace/status_validation/docs/contract.md - Allowed status values: PENDING, RUNNING, COMPLETE, FAILED, CANCELED - Rules: exact match, uppercase only, no leading/trailing whitespace, empty/null invalid - Validation script: - /workspace/status_validation/scripts/validate_status.py - Generates 10 toy rows, validates status values against the contract, and writes invalid rows - Data: - /workspace/status_validation/data/toy_rows.csv (10 toy rows) - Output: - /workspace/status_validation/outputs/invalid_status_rows.csv (rows with invalid status values) How the validation works - A value is valid if: - It is a non-empty string - It has no leading/trailing whitespace (value == value.strip()) - It is exactly one of: {PENDING, RUNNING, COMPLETE, FAILED, CANCELED} Toy data summary - Total rows: 10 - Valid: 5 (PENDING, RUNNING, COMPLETE, FAILED, CANCELED) - Invalid: 5 (pending, DONE, IN_PROGRESS, “ COMPLETE ”, empty) Run it again (if needed) - python3 /workspace/status_validation/scripts/validate_status.py Key outputs to check - /workspace/status_validation/outputs/invalid_status_rows.csv - /workspace/status_validation/docs/contract.md If you want me to allow additional statuses (e.g., IN_PROGRESS) or change case-sensitivity/whitespace rules, tell me and I’ll update the contract and validator.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I will generate a 10-row synthetic dataset with mixed types and some nulls for realistic completeness/uniqueness, then compute dq_report.csv and data_dictionary.csv using a Python script, and save all artifacts under /workspace/data_quality/. I’ll then verify by listing the files and previewing their contents. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/data_quality && python3 - << 'PY' import csv, random, datetime, os, sys from collections import OrderedDict import math random.seed(42) out_dir = "/workspace/data_quality" data_rows = [] # Define 10 synthetic rows with intentional nulls and duplicates names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana", "Ethan", "Fiona", "George", "Hana", "Ivan", "Julia"] countries = ["US", "UK", "DE", "FR", "IN", "CN", "BR", "CA", "AU", "ZA"] # Helper to maybe return None maybe_none = lambda v, p=0.2: None if random.random() < p else v rows = [] for i in range(10): row = OrderedDict() row["id"] = i + 1 # 1..10 # Introduce a duplicate name and email for uniqueness testing name = names[i] if i in (3, 7): name = names[1] # duplicate "Bob" row["name"] = maybe_none(name, 0.1) # Ages with some None and variation age = random.choice([23, 29, 31, 35, 41, 47, 52, 29, 35, 41]) if i == 5: age = None row["age"] = age # Emails derived from name with some duplicates and nulls email = None if random.random() < 0.15 else f"{(row['name'] or 'user').lower()}@example.com" if i in (2, 8): email = "bob@example.com" # duplicate email row["email"] = email # signup_date with occasional None base = datetime.date(2024, 1, 1) signup_date = base + datetime.timedelta(days=random.randint(0, 365)) if i in (4,): signup_date = None row["signup_date"] = signup_date.isoformat() if signup_date else None # country with some None and duplicates country = countries[i] if i in (6, 9): country = countries[0] # duplicate "US" country = maybe_none(country, 0.1) row["country"] = country # balance numeric with some None balance = round(random.uniform(-50.0, 500.0), 2) if i in (1, 7): balance = None row["balance"] = balance # is_active boolean with occasional None is_active = random.choice([True, False]) if i in (3,): is_active = None row["is_active"] = is_active # score float with some None score = round(random.uniform(0.0, 1.0), 3) if i in (0, 9): score = None row["score"] = score # comments free text with some None and duplicates comments = random.choice(["ok", "review", "pending", "ok", None]) row["comments"] = comments rows.append(row) # Write synthetic_data.csv synthetic_path = os.path.join(out_dir, "synthetic_data.csv") fieldnames = list(rows[0].keys()) os.makedirs(out_dir, exist_ok=True) with open(synthetic_path, "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f: w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=fieldnames) w.writeheader() for r in rows: w.writerow(r) # Compute DQ metrics: completeness (non-null/total), uniqueness (distinct non-null/total) total_rows = len(rows) def is_null(v): return v is None or v == "" # treat empty string as null if any # Collect columns cols = fieldnames # Prepare dq_report rows dq_rows = [] for c in cols: values = [r[c] for r in rows] non_null_vals = [v for v in values if not is_null(v)] non_null_count = len(non_null_vals) completeness = non_null_count / total_rows if total_rows else 0.0 # Distinct non-null values # Normalize types for consistent set behavior (dates are strings already) distinct_non_null = len(set(non_null_vals)) uniqueness = distinct_non_null / total_rows if total_rows else 0.0 dq_rows.append({ "column": c, "non_null_count": non_null_count, "total_rows": total_rows, "completeness": f"{completeness:.3f}", "distinct_non_null_count": distinct_non_null, "uniqueness": f"{uniqueness:.3f}", }) # Write dq_report.csv dq_path = os.path.join(out_dir, "dq_report.csv") with open(dq_path, "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f: w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=["column","non_null_count","total_rows","completeness","distinct_non_null_count","uniqueness"]) w.writeheader() w.writerows(dq_rows) # Build data dictionary (column types) by simple inference from non-null values def infer_type(values): # Look at first non-null value for v in values: if not is_null(v): if isinstance(v, bool): return "boolean" if isinstance(v, int): return "integer" if isinstance(v, float): return "float" # try parse date ISO if isinstance(v, str): # try ISO date YYYY-MM-DD try: datetime.date.fromisoformat(v) return "date" except Exception: return "string" return type(v).__name__ return "unknown" schema_rows = [] for c in cols: values = [r[c] for r in rows] inferred = infer_type(values) schema_rows.append({"column": c, "inferred_type": inferred}) # Write data_dictionary.csv dict_path = os.path.join(out_dir, "data_dictionary.csv") with open(dict_path, "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f: w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=["column","inferred_type"]) w.writeheader() w.writerows(schema_rows) print("Created:") print(" -", synthetic_path) print(" -", dq_path) print(" -", dict_path) # Preview outputs import pandas as pd pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None) print("\nSynthetic data (first 10 rows):") print(pd.read_csv(synthetic_path).to_string(index=False)) print("\nDQ report:") print(pd.read_csv(dq_path).to_string(index=False)) print("\nData dictionary:") print(pd.read_csv(dict_path).to_string(index=False)) PY"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: First, confirm authoritative CSV quoting rules (RFC 4180, Python csv docs) to ensure the serialization code and notes are correct. I will search the web. {"type":"web","q":"CSV quoting rules RFC 4180 double quotes commas newlines Python csv quoting minimal doublequote", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"RFC 4180: Common Format and MIME Type for Comma-Separated Values (CSV) Files","url":"https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4180"},{"title":"Python csv module — Dialects and Formatting Parameters","url":"https://docs.python.org/3/library/csv.html#csv.Dialect"},{"title":"Wikipedia: Comma-separated values — Quoting and escaping","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comma-separated_values#Standardization"},{"title":"CSV best practices (Microsoft/Excel) — Import/export considerations","url":"https://support.microsoft.com/office/text-import-wizard-and-csv-formatting-best-practices"},{"title":"RFC 7111: URI Fragment Identifiers for the text/csv Media Type (context on CSV format)","url":"https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7111"},{"title":"Pandas to_csv quoting options","url":"https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.DataFrame.to_csv.html"}],"summary":"RFC 4180 states that fields containing commas, double quotes, or CRLFs must be enclosed in double quotes. Embedded double quotes in a field must be represented as two consecutive double quotes. Python's csv module follows these rules by default with QUOTE_MINIMAL and doublequote=True, and requires opening files with newline='' to avoid extra blank lines on Windows. Wikipedia reiterates these quoting/escaping conventions. Excel and Pandas follow the same conventions by default."}} Now create a workspace folder, write a Python script that safely serializes a CSV with commas, quotes, and newlines using csv.writer with quoting=QUOTE_MINIMAL and doublequote=True, run it to produce output.csv, and verify contents. Then write format_notes.md explaining why quoting is necessary with references. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e mkdir -p /workspace/csv_quoting_demo cat > /workspace/csv_quoting_demo/serialize_csv.py << 'PY' #!/usr/bin/env python3 import csv from pathlib import Path out_path = Path(__file__).parent / "output.csv" rows = [ {"id": 1, "label": "Simple", "notes": "No commas or quotes"}, {"id": 2, "label": "Comma", "notes": "A, B, C"}, {"id": 3, "label": "Quote", "notes": "She said \"Hello, world\"."}, {"id": 4, "label": "Mixed", "notes": "Value, with comma and \"quotes\""}, {"id": 5, "label": "Newline", "notes": "First line\nSecond line"}, {"id": 6, "label": "Empty", "notes": ""}, ] # Open with newline='' per Python csv docs to prevent extra blank lines on Windows with out_path.open("w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f: writer = csv.writer( f, delimiter=",", quotechar='"', lineterminator="\r\n", # RFC 4180 recommends CRLF line endings quoting=csv.QUOTE_MINIMAL, # quote only when needed escapechar=None, # not needed when doublequote=True doublequote=True, # double embedded quotes per RFC 4180 ) # Header writer.writerow(["id", "label", "notes"]) # Rows for r in rows: writer.writerow([r["id"], r["label"], r["notes"]]) print(f"Wrote CSV to: {out_path}") PY chmod +x /workspace/csv_quoting_demo/serialize_csv.py python3 /workspace/csv_quoting_demo/serialize_csv.py ls -l /workspace/csv_quoting_demo"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Wrote CSV to: /workspace/csv_quoting_demo/output.csv total 12 -rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 1381 Sep 28 00:00 serialize_csv.py -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 234 Sep 28 00:00 output.csv ","stderr":""} Display the produced CSV to verify correct quoting and doubling of embedded quotes. Use cat to show content. {"type":"code","code_command":"printf '--- Begin output.csv ---\n' && cat /workspace/csv_quoting_demo/output.csv && printf '\n--- End output.csv ---\n'"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"--- Begin output.csv --- id,label,notes 1,Simple,No commas or quotes 2,Comma,"A, B, C" 3,Quote,"She said ""Hello, world""." 4,Mixed,"Value, with comma and ""quotes""" 5,Newline,"First line Second line" 6,Empty, --- End output.csv --- ","stderr":""} Now write format_notes.md explaining quoting necessity, RFC 4180 rules, Python csv settings, examples, and pitfalls (newlines, commas, quotes). Include references found. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/csv_quoting_demo/format_notes.md << 'MD' CSV quoting: what, why, and how Why quoting is necessary - Ambiguity prevention: The comma is the field delimiter in CSV. If a field value contains a comma (e.g., "A, B, C"), the parser would misinterpret it as multiple fields unless the field is quoted as a single unit. - Newlines inside fields: A CSV record may contain line breaks within a field (e.g., multi-line notes). Quoting keeps the embedded newline as part of the field instead of ending the record. - Embedded double quotes: The double quote is the quoting character itself. When a field contains a double quote, it must be represented in a way that does not prematurely terminate the field. Rules (per RFC 4180 and widely adopted practice) - Field enclosure: If a field contains a comma, double quote, or CR/LF, it must be enclosed in double quotes. - Doubling quotes: A double quote appearing inside a quoted field must be represented by two double quotes. - Record terminator: Lines typically end with CRLF (\r\n) in RFC 4180. Many tools are tolerant of LF (\n), but CRLF is most interoperable. - Header row: A header line is recommended and widely used, though not required by the RFC. - Single quotes: Single quotes have no special meaning in standard CSV; they are regular characters. Examples from this repository - Field with commas: The value A, B, C is serialized as "A, B, C". - Field with quotes: The value She said "Hello, world". is serialized as "She said ""Hello, world""." (the inner quotes are doubled). - Field with a newline: A multi-line field like First line\nSecond line is serialized as a quoted field spanning two lines. Python csv module settings used here - quoting=csv.QUOTE_MINIMAL: Only quote fields when required (commas, quotes, newlines). This matches common expectations and keeps files compact. - doublequote=True: Embedded double quotes are represented as "" inside quoted fields, per RFC 4180. - newline='' when opening files: Required by Python's csv module on Windows to prevent extra blank lines. - lineterminator='\r\n': Ensures CRLF records, improving cross-tool compatibility (Excel, legacy parsers). Do not hand-roll CSV - Manual string joins (like ','.join([...])) will break when data contains commas or quotes. - Proper handling also requires correct line endings and escaping rules. Use a CSV library to avoid subtle bugs. References - RFC 4180: Common Format and MIME Type for CSV files — https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4180 - Python csv module documentation — https://docs.python.org/3/library/csv.html - Wikipedia: CSV quoting/escaping conventions — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comma-separated_values#Standardization - Excel CSV import/export notes — https://support.microsoft.com/office/text-import-wizard-and-csv-formatting-best-practices MD ls -l /workspace/csv_quoting_demo && printf '\nPreview format_notes.md:\n' && sed -n '1,80p' /workspace/csv_quoting_demo/format_notes.md"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 20 -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1160 Sep 28 00:00 format_notes.md -rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 1381 Sep 28 00:00 serialize_csv.py -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 234 Sep 28 00:00 output.csv Preview format_notes.md: CSV quoting: what, why, and how Why quoting is necessary - Ambiguity prevention: The comma is the field delimiter in CSV. If a field value contains a comma (e.g., "A, B, C"), the parser would misinterpret it as multiple fields unless the field is quoted as a single unit. - Newlines inside fields: A CSV record may contain line breaks within a field (e.g., multi-line notes). Quoting keeps the embedded newline as part of the field instead of ending the record. - Embedded double quotes: The double quote is the quoting character itself. When a field contains a double quote, it must be represented in a way that does not prematurely terminate the field. Rules (per RFC 4180 and widely adopted practice) - Field enclosure: If a field contains a comma, double quote, or CR/LF, it must be enclosed in double quotes. - Doubling quotes: A double quote appearing inside a quoted field must be represented by two double quotes. - Record terminator: Lines typically end with CRLF (\r\n) in RFC 4180. Many tools are tolerant of LF (\n), but CRLF is most interoperable. - Header row: A header line is recommended and widely used, though not required by the RFC. - Single quotes: Single quotes have no special meaning in standard CSV; they are regular characters. Examples from this repository - Field with commas: The value A, B, C is serialized as "A, B, C". - Field with quotes: The value She said "Hello, world". is serialized as "She said ""Hello, world""." (the inner quotes are doubled). - Field with a newline: A multi-line field like First line\nSecond line is serialized as a quoted field spanning two lines. Python csv module settings used here - quoting=csv.QUOTE_MINIMAL: Only quote fields when required (commas, quotes, newlines). This matches common expectations and keeps files compact. - doublequote=True: Embedded double quotes are represented as "" inside quoted fields, per RFC 4180. - newline='' when opening files: Required by Python's csv module on Windows to prevent extra blank lines. - lineterminator='\r\n': Ensures CRLF records, improving cross-tool compatibility (Excel, legacy parsers). Do not hand-roll CSV - Manual string joins (like ','.join([...])) will break when data contains commas or quotes. - Proper handling also requires correct line endings and escaping rules. Use a CSV library to avoid subtle bugs. References - RFC 4180: Common Format and MIME Type for CSV files — https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4180 - Python csv module documentation — https://docs.python.org/3/library/csv.html - Wikipedia: CSV quoting/escaping conventions — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comma-separated_values#Standardization - Excel CSV import/export notes — https://support.microsoft.com/office/text-import-wizard-and-csv-formatting-best-practices ","stderr":""} Optionally add a quick round-trip verification script to ensure parsing recovers originals, then run it. This validates the quoting works. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/csv_quoting_demo/verify_roundtrip.py << 'PY' #!/usr/bin/env python3 import csv, json from pathlib import Path p = Path(__file__).parent / 'output.csv' rows = [] with p.open('r', encoding='utf-8', newline='') as f: r = csv.DictReader(f) for row in r: rows.append(row) print(json.dumps(rows, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)) PY python3 /workspace/csv_quoting_demo/verify_roundtrip.py"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"[ { "id": "1", "label": "Simple", "notes": "No commas or quotes" }, { "id": "2", "label": "Comma", "notes": "A, B, C" }, { "id": "3", "label": "Quote", "notes": "She said \"Hello, world\"." }, { "id": "4", "label": "Mixed", "notes": "Value, with comma and \"quotes\"" }, { "id": "5", "label": "Newline", "notes": "First line\nSecond line" }, { "id": "6", "label": "Empty", "notes": "" } ] ","stderr":""} Everything is created and verified. I will now summarize the outputs, locations, and how to run.
