content.text: Plan: Create a working directory, generate features_date.csv from 10 toy timestamps using Python (derive day_of_week and month plus readable names), verify contents, then write feature_docs.md explaining the transformation and assumptions. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/date_features && ls -la /workspace/date_features"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 8 drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 . drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 .. ","stderr":""} Now generate features_date.csv by computing features from 10 toy ISO-8601 UTC timestamps using Python's datetime and calendar. Include columns: timestamp, day_of_week (Mon=0..Sun=6), day_of_week_name, month (1..12), month_name. {"type":"code","code_command":"python3 - << 'PY' import csv from datetime import datetime, timezone import calendar # 10 toy timestamps in UTC (Z) timestamps = [ "2025-01-01T08:00:00Z", "2025-02-14T12:30:00Z", "2025-03-15T09:00:00Z", "2025-04-01T00:00:00Z", "2025-05-05T18:45:00Z", "2025-06-21T06:15:00Z", "2025-07-04T20:00:00Z", "2025-08-31T23:59:59Z", "2025-09-28T10:00:00Z", "2025-10-31T22:22:22Z", ] def parse_iso_utc(ts: str) -> datetime: # Example: 2025-01-01T08:00:00Z return datetime.strptime(ts, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ").replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc) rows = [] for ts in timestamps: dt = parse_iso_utc(ts) dow = dt.weekday() # Monday=0, Sunday=6 month = dt.month rows.append({ "timestamp": ts, "day_of_week": dow, "day_of_week_name": calendar.day_name[dow], "month": month, "month_name": calendar.month_name[month], }) out_path = "/workspace/date_features/features_date.csv" with open(out_path, "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f: writer = csv.DictWriter( f, fieldnames=[ "timestamp", "day_of_week", "day_of_week_name", "month", "month_name", ], ) writer.writeheader() writer.writerows(rows) print(f"Wrote {out_path} with {len(rows)} rows") PY"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Wrote /workspace/date_features/features_date.csv with 10 rows ","stderr":""} Verify the CSV contents and row count to ensure 10 data rows plus header. {"type":"code","code_command":"echo "--- features_date.csv ---" && cat /workspace/date_features/features_date.csv && echo "\n--- Line count (should be 11 including header) ---" && wc -l /workspace/date_features/features_date.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"--- features_date.csv --- timestamp,day_of_week,day_of_week_name,month,month_name 2025-01-01T08:00:00Z,2,Wednesday,1,January 2025-02-14T12:30:00Z,4,Friday,2,February 2025-03-15T09:00:00Z,5,Saturday,3,March 2025-04-01T00:00:00Z,1,Tuesday,4,April 2025-05-05T18:45:00Z,0,Monday,5,May 2025-06-21T06:15:00Z,5,Saturday,6,June 2025-07-04T20:00:00Z,4,Friday,7,July 2025-08-31T23:59:59Z,6,Sunday,8,August 2025-09-28T10:00:00Z,6,Sunday,9,September 2025-10-31T22:22:22Z,4,Friday,10,October --- Line count (should be 11 including header) --- 11 /workspace/date_features/features_date.csv ","stderr":""} Create feature_docs.md documenting the transformation: inputs, outputs, logic (Mon=0..Sun=6, month 1..12), assumptions (UTC), reproducibility, and file details. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/date_features/feature_docs.md << 'MD' Title: Date Feature Engineering Documentation Overview This document describes the transformation used to derive date/time features from a list of toy timestamps. The resulting dataset is saved as features_date.csv. Inputs - Source: 10 toy timestamps in ISO-8601 format with UTC designator (Z) - 2025-01-01T08:00:00Z - 2025-02-14T12:30:00Z - 2025-03-15T09:00:00Z - 2025-04-01T00:00:00Z - 2025-05-05T18:45:00Z - 2025-06-21T06:15:00Z - 2025-07-04T20:00:00Z - 2025-08-31T23:59:59Z - 2025-09-28T10:00:00Z - 2025-10-31T22:22:22Z Transformations For each timestamp, the following features are derived using Python's datetime and calendar modules with UTC timezone: - day_of_week (int): Day of week where Monday=0, ..., Sunday=6 - day_of_week_name (string): Full day name (e.g., Monday, Tuesday, ...) - month (int): Month number in [1..12] - month_name (string): Full month name (e.g., January, February, ...) Assumptions - Timezone: All timestamps are parsed as UTC (suffix Z). No timezone conversion is performed. - Format: Timestamps are expected to follow the pattern YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ. - Missing/invalid timestamps: Not applicable in this toy example. In a production setting, invalid/missing timestamps should yield null features and be flagged for downstream handling. Output File - Path: /workspace/date_features/features_date.csv - Format: CSV, UTF-8 encoded, comma-separated, with header - Columns: 1) timestamp (string) 2) day_of_week (integer, Monday=0..Sunday=6) 3) day_of_week_name (string) 4) month (integer, 1..12) 5) month_name (string) - Row count: 10 data rows (+1 header) Reproducibility - The features are deterministic functions of the provided timestamps. - No randomness is used. - Implementation reference: Python 3 datetime + calendar. Example For timestamp 2025-01-01T08:00:00Z → day_of_week=2 (Wednesday), month=1 (January). Change Log - v1.0 (initial): Created features_date.csv with 10 toy timestamps and documented transformation. MD && echo "Wrote /workspace/date_features/feature_docs.md" && sed -n '1,40p' /workspace/date_features/feature_docs.md"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Wrote /workspace/date_features/feature_docs.md Title: Date Feature Engineering Documentation Overview This document describes the transformation used to derive date/time features from a list of toy timestamps. The resulting dataset is saved as features_date.csv. Assumptions - Timezone: All timestamps are parsed as UTC (suffix Z). No timezone conversion is performed. - Format: Timestamps are expected to follow the pattern YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ. - Missing/invalid timestamps: Not applicable in this toy example. In a production setting, invalid/missing timestamps should yield null features and be flagged for downstream handling. Output File - Path: /workspace/date_features/features_date.csv - Format: CSV, UTF-8 encoded, comma-separated, with header - Columns: 1) timestamp (string) 2) day_of_week (integer, Monday=0..Sunday=6) 3) day_of_week_name (string) 4) month (integer, 1..12) 5) month_name (string) - Row count: 10 data rows (+1 header) Reproducibility - The features are deterministic functions of the provided timestamps. - No randomness is used. - Implementation reference: Python 3 datetime + calendar. Example For timestamp 2025-01-01T08:00:00Z → day_of_week=2 (Wednesday), month=1 (January). Change Log - v1.0 (initial): Created features_date.csv with 10 toy timestamps and documented transformation. ","stderr":""} List the directory to show both artifacts exist before finalizing. {"type":"code","code_command":"ls -l /workspace/date_features && echo "\nFile sizes:" && wc -c /workspace/date_features/features_date.csv /workspace/date_features/feature_docs.md"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 8 -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 541 Sep 28 00:00 feature_docs.md -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 482 Sep 28 00:00 features_date.csv File sizes: 482 /workspace/date_features/features_date.csv 541 /workspace/date_features/feature_docs.md ","stderr":""} All set! Created - /workspace/date_features/features_date.csv - /workspace/date_features/feature_docs.md features_date.csv - Columns: timestamp, day_of_week (Mon=0..Sun=6), day_of_week_name, month (1..12), month_name - 10 rows generated from toy ISO-8601 UTC timestamps - Sample rows: - 2025-01-01T08:00:00Z → day_of_week=2 (Wednesday), month=1 (January) - 2025-02-14T12:30:00Z → day_of_week=4 (Friday), month=2 (February) feature_docs.md - Documents the transformation steps, assumptions (UTC, format), output schema, and reproducibility notes. - Includes the exact list of input timestamps and an example mapping. Where to find the files - features_date.csv: /workspace/date_features/features_date.csv - feature_docs.md: /workspace/date_features/feature_docs.md If you want different date parts (e.g., week number, quarter) or a different day-of-week numbering (e.g., Sunday=0), I can regenerate quickly.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: First, check if there is an input CSV in /workspace to bin. If not, create a small example scores.csv to proceed, then implement a robust quantile (tertile) binning script that saves binned.csv and bin_edges.json for reproducibility. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e shopt -s nullglob CSV_FILES=(/workspace/*.csv) if [ ${#CSV_FILES[@]} -gt 0 ]; then echo "Found CSV files:"; printf '%s\n' "${CSV_FILES[@]}" else echo "No CSV found in /workspace. Creating example scores.csv..." cat > /workspace/scores.csv << 'CSV' id,score A,0.12 B,0.55 C,0.32 D,0.91 E,0.73 F,0.67 G,0.44 H,0.23 I,0.88 J,0.35 K,0.04 L,0.61 CSV echo "Created /workspace/scores.csv" fi # Show a preview of the candidate input CANDIDATE=${CSV_FILES[0]:-/workspace/scores.csv} echo "Using input: $CANDIDATE" head -n 10 "$CANDIDATE" || true"}

J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json content.text: I'll create a small synthetic CSV with one categorical column, then write a short Python script to label-encode it, producing mapping.json and transformed.csv. I’ll verify outputs by printing them. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/label_encoding && cat > /workspace/label_encoding/input.csv << 'CSV' color red blue green blue red yellow CSV ls -l /workspace/label_encoding && echo '--- input.csv' && cat /workspace/label_encoding/input.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 4 -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 38 Sep 28 12:00 input.csv --- input.csv color red blue green blue red yellow ","stderr":""} Now I’ll write and run a Python script to perform label encoding on the 'color' column, saving mapping.json and transformed.csv. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/label_encoding/encode.py << 'PY' import argparse, csv, json, os def main(): ap = argparse.ArgumentParser() ap.add_argument('--input', required=True) ap.add_argument('--column', required=True) ap.add_argument('--out_csv', required=True) ap.add_argument('--out_map', required=True) args = ap.parse_args() # Read the categorical column values values = [] with open(args.input, newline='') as f: reader = csv.DictReader(f) if args.column not in reader.fieldnames: raise SystemExit(f"Column '{args.column}' not found in {args.input}. Fields: {reader.fieldnames}") for row in reader: values.append(row[args.column]) # Order-of-appearance mapping mapping = {} next_id = 0 for v in values: if v not in mapping: mapping[v] = next_id next_id += 1 # Write mapping.json with open(args.out_map, 'w') as f: json.dump(mapping, f, separators=(',', ':')) # Write transformed.csv with single column '_id' out_field = f"{args.column}_id" with open(args.out_csv, 'w', newline='') as f: writer = csv.writer(f) writer.writerow([out_field]) for v in values: writer.writerow([mapping[v]]) print(f"Read {len(values)} rows from {args.input}") print(f"Found {len(mapping)} unique categories in order: {list(mapping.keys())}") print(f"Wrote mapping.json to {args.out_map}") print(f"Wrote transformed column to {args.out_csv}") if __name__ == '__main__': main() PY python3 /workspace/label_encoding/encode.py --input /workspace/label_encoding/input.csv --column color --out_csv /workspace/label_encoding/transformed.csv --out_map /workspace/label_encoding/mapping.json ls -l /workspace/label_encoding"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Read 6 rows from /workspace/label_encoding/input.csv Found 4 unique categories in order: ['red', 'blue', 'green', 'yellow'] Wrote mapping.json to /workspace/label_encoding/mapping.json Wrote transformed column to /workspace/label_encoding/transformed.csv total 16 -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 38 Sep 28 12:00 input.csv -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 106 Sep 28 12:00 encode.py -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 41 Sep 28 12:00 mapping.json -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 28 Sep 28 12:00 transformed.csv ","stderr":""} Verify the contents of mapping.json and transformed.csv to ensure correctness. {"type":"code","code_command":"echo '--- mapping.json' && cat /workspace/label_encoding/mapping.json && echo '\n--- transformed.csv' && cat /workspace/label_encoding/transformed.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"--- mapping.json {"red":0,"blue":1,"green":2,"yellow":3} --- transformed.csv color_id 0 1 2 1 0 3 ","stderr":""} Optionally run a quick verification to ensure mapping covers all unique categories and transformed length matches input. {"type":"code","code_command":"python3 - << 'PY' import csv, json from pathlib import Path base = Path('/workspace/label_encoding') # Load input values with open(base/'input.csv', newline='') as f: reader = csv.DictReader(f) vals = [r['color'] for r in reader] # Load mapping with open(base/'mapping.json') as f: mapping = json.load(f) # Load transformed with open(base/'transformed.csv', newline='') as f: reader = csv.DictReader(f) enc_vals = [int(r['color_id']) for r in reader] assert len(set(vals)) == len(mapping), 'Mismatch in unique categories vs mapping size' assert len(vals) == len(enc_vals), 'Row count mismatch in transformed output' assert set(enc_vals).issubset(set(mapping.values())), 'Transformed values not subset of mapping IDs' print('Verification OK:') print(f" rows: input={len(vals)}, transformed={len(enc_vals)}") print(f" unique categories={len(set(vals))}, mapping size={len(mapping)}") print(f" mapping: {mapping}") PY"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Verification OK: rows: input=6, transformed=6 unique categories=4, mapping size=4 mapping: {'red': 0, 'blue': 1, 'green': 2, 'yellow': 3} ","stderr":""} Done. What I created - Synthetic input data: /workspace/label_encoding/input.csv - Column: color - Values: red, blue, green, blue, red, yellow - Label encoder script: /workspace/label_encoding/encode.py - Outputs: - mapping.json (category → ID): /workspace/label_encoding/mapping.json - {"red":0,"blue":1,"green":2,"yellow":3} - transformed.csv (single encoded column): /workspace/label_encoding/transformed.csv - Header: color_id - Rows: 0, 1, 2, 1, 0, 3 How to reproduce - Run: python3 /workspace/label_encoding/encode.py --input /workspace/label_encoding/input.csv --column color --out_csv /workspace/label_encoding/transformed.csv --out_map /workspace/label_encoding/mapping.json Notes - IDs are assigned in order of first appearance starting at 0. - The transformed CSV contains only the encoded column for the requested categorical feature. If you prefer to keep original columns or include an ID column, I can adjust the output format.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll create a small project directory, write a Python script that generates synthetic data, trains a DecisionTreeClassifier with max_depth=2, computes accuracy, and writes feature_importances.csv and metrics.json. Then I'll run it and verify outputs. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e mkdir -p /workspace/toy_dt/outputs cat > /workspace/toy_dt/train_dt.py << 'PY' import argparse import json import os from pathlib import Path import numpy as np from sklearn.datasets import make_classification from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier def main(): parser = argparse.ArgumentParser() parser.add_argument("--outdir", type=str, default="outputs") parser.add_argument("--random_state", type=int, default=42) parser.add_argument("--n_samples", type=int, default=200) parser.add_argument("--n_features", type=int, default=5) parser.add_argument("--n_informative", type=int, default=3) parser.add_argument("--test_size", type=float, default=0.3) parser.add_argument("--max_depth", type=int, default=2) args = parser.parse_args() outdir = Path(args.outdir) outdir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) X, y = make_classification( n_samples=args.n_samples, n_features=args.n_features, n_informative=args.n_informative, n_redundant=0, n_repeated=0, n_clusters_per_class=2, flip_y=0.01, class_sep=1.25, random_state=args.random_state, ) X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split( X, y, test_size=args.test_size, random_state=args.random_state, stratify=y ) clf = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=args.max_depth, random_state=args.random_state) clf.fit(X_train, y_train) acc = clf.score(X_test, y_test) # Save metrics.json metrics = { "accuracy": acc, "params": { "model": "DecisionTreeClassifier", "max_depth": args.max_depth, "random_state": args.random_state, }, "data": { "n_samples": args.n_samples, "n_features": args.n_features, "n_informative": args.n_informative, "test_size": args.test_size, }, } (outdir / "metrics.json").write_text(json.dumps(metrics, indent=2)) # Save feature_importances.csv importances = clf.feature_importances_ feature_names = [f"f{i}" for i in range(args.n_features)] lines = ["feature,importance\n"] + [f"{name},{imp}\n" for name, imp in zip(feature_names, importances)] (outdir / "feature_importances.csv").write_text("".join(lines)) print(f"Accuracy: {acc:.4f}") print(f"Wrote: {outdir / 'metrics.json'}") print(f"Wrote: {outdir / 'feature_importances.csv'}") if __name__ == "__main__": main() PY ls -la /workspace/toy_dt"}

First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll create a small project in /workspace, write a Python script to generate (x, y=2x+noise), fit an OLS linear regression baseline, and save outputs to outputs/coefficients.csv and outputs/rmse.txt. Then I'll run it and verify artifacts. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/linreg_baseline/{outputs,scripts} && ls -R /workspace/linreg_baseline"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/linreg_baseline: outputs scripts ","stderr":""} Now I’ll add a deterministic Python script (seeded RNG) to generate data, fit OLS (closed-form), compute RMSE, and write the two required files. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/linreg_baseline/train.py << 'PY' #!/usr/bin/env python3 import os import sys import argparse import numpy as np def fit_linear_regression(x: np.ndarray, y: np.ndarray): # OLS closed-form for simple linear regression x_mean = x.mean() y_mean = y.mean() cov_xy = np.mean((x - x_mean) * (y - y_mean)) var_x = np.mean((x - x_mean) ** 2) slope = cov_xy / var_x intercept = y_mean - slope * x_mean return intercept, slope def rmse(y_true: np.ndarray, y_pred: np.ndarray) -> float: return float(np.sqrt(np.mean((y_true - y_pred) ** 2))) def main(): parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Fit a linear regression baseline on y=2x+noise and save coefficients and RMSE.") parser.add_argument("--n", type=int, default=1000, help="Number of samples") parser.add_argument("--x_low", type=float, default=-5.0, help="Lower bound for x uniform distribution") parser.add_argument("--x_high", type=float, default=5.0, help="Upper bound for x uniform distribution") parser.add_argument("--noise_sigma", type=float, default=1.0, help="Std dev of Gaussian noise") parser.add_argument("--seed", type=int, default=42, help="Random seed for reproducibility") parser.add_argument("--outdir", type=str, default="outputs", help="Output directory") args = parser.parse_args() np.random.seed(args.seed) # Generate data x = np.random.uniform(args.x_low, args.x_high, size=args.n) noise = np.random.normal(0.0, args.noise_sigma, size=args.n) y = 2.0 * x + noise # Fit OLS baseline intercept, slope = fit_linear_regression(x, y) # Predictions and RMSE y_pred = intercept + slope * x score_rmse = rmse(y, y_pred) # Prepare outputs os.makedirs(args.outdir, exist_ok=True) coeffs_path = os.path.join(args.outdir, "coefficients.csv") rmse_path = os.path.join(args.outdir, "rmse.txt") # Save coefficients.csv with header: intercept,slope with open(coeffs_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f: f.write("intercept,slope\n") f.write(f"{intercept:.6f},{slope:.6f}\n") # Save rmse.txt containing only the numeric value with open(rmse_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f: f.write(f"{score_rmse:.6f}\n") print(f"Fitted intercept={intercept:.6f}, slope={slope:.6f}") print(f"RMSE={score_rmse:.6f}") print(f"Wrote {coeffs_path} and {rmse_path}") if __name__ == "__main__": sys.exit(main()) PY chmod +x /workspace/linreg_baseline/train.py && python3 /workspace/linreg_baseline/train.py --n 1000 --noise_sigma 1.0 --seed 42 --outdir /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Fitted intercept=-0.001807, slope=2.001687 RMSE=1.004227 Wrote /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs/coefficients.csv and /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs/rmse.txt ","stderr":""} Verify the artifacts exist and show their contents. {"type":"code","code_command":"ls -l /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs && echo '--- coefficients.csv ---' && cat /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs/coefficients.csv && echo '--- rmse.txt ---' && cat /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs/rmse.txt"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 8 -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 45 Sep 28 00:00 coefficients.csv -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 10 Sep 28 00:00 rmse.txt --- coefficients.csv --- intercept,slope -0.001807,2.001687 --- rmse.txt --- 1.004227 ","stderr":""} Add a minimal check script to recompute RMSE from saved coefficients and the original generated data parameters (using the same seed) to validate consistency. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/linreg_baseline/scripts/check_rmse.py << 'PY' #!/usr/bin/env python3 import numpy as np import argparse def main(): ap = argparse.ArgumentParser() ap.add_argument('--n', type=int, default=1000) ap.add_argument('--x_low', type=float, default=-5.0) ap.add_argument('--x_high', type=float, default=5.0) ap.add_argument('--noise_sigma', type=float, default=1.0) ap.add_argument('--seed', type=int, default=42) ap.add_argument('--coeffs', type=str, required=True) args = ap.parse_args() np.random.seed(args.seed) x = np.random.uniform(args.x_low, args.x_high, size=args.n) noise = np.random.normal(0.0, args.noise_sigma, size=args.n) y = 2.0 * x + noise # Read coefficients with open(args.coeffs, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f: header = f.readline().strip().split(',') vals = f.readline().strip().split(',') intercept = float(vals[0]) slope = float(vals[1]) y_pred = intercept + slope * x rmse_val = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((y - y_pred) ** 2))) print(f"Recomputed RMSE={rmse_val:.6f}") if __name__ == '__main__': main() PY python3 /workspace/linreg_baseline/scripts/check_rmse.py --coeffs /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs/coefficients.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Recomputed RMSE=1.004227 ","stderr":""} Everything is complete. I'll provide the final summary with artifact paths and how to rerun. Done. What I did - Generated synthetic data (x ~ Uniform[-5,5], y = 2x + Gaussian noise, seed=42). - Fit a simple OLS linear regression baseline. - Saved: - outputs/coefficients.csv with intercept and slope - outputs/rmse.txt with the RMSE on the generated data Artifacts - /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs/coefficients.csv intercept,slope -0.001807,2.001687 - /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs/rmse.txt 1.004227 How to reproduce - Run: python3 /workspace/linreg_baseline/train.py --n 1000 --noise_sigma 1.0 --seed 42 --outdir /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs Optional validation - Recompute RMSE from saved coefficients: python3 /workspace/linreg_baseline/scripts/check_rmse.py --coeffs /workspace/linreg_baseline/outputs/coefficients.csv If you want different sample size or noise level, adjust --n and --noise_sigma flags and rerun.

File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example.
