/workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

First, list /workspace to discover any transform output to target. {"type":"code","code_command":"ls -R /workspace || true"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. content.text: We need to implement a dry-run mode concept in the existing workspace. I'll add: - scripts/dry_run_utils.sh to centralize dry-run checks and logging. - dry_run_log.txt at repo root with example "intended writes". - README.md with instructions on enabling dry-run and sample output. - Update scripts/validate_bundle.sh to respect DRY_RUN=1 and validate intents from the dry_run_log instead of actual files. Then verify validate script both normally and with DRY_RUN=1. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e REPO=/workspace/azureml_eval_contract mkdir -p "$REPO/scripts" # 1) Add a dry-run utility script with helpers cat > "$REPO/scripts/dry_run_utils.sh" << 'SH' #!/usr/bin/env bash # Dry-run utilities: source this in any script that may write artifacts. # Behavior: # - DRY_RUN=1 enables dry-run globally. # - --dry-run flag sets DRY_RUN=1 for the current process. # - DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH overrides the log destination (default: ./dry_run_log.txt) # Ensure safe defaults : "${DRY_RUN:=0}" : "${DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH:=./dry_run_log.txt}" # Detect --dry-run in args, export DRY_RUN=1, and strip the flag from $@ # Usage: parse_dry_run_flag "$@"; set -- "${PARSED_ARGS[@]}" parse_dry_run_flag() { PARSED_ARGS=() for arg in "$@"; do case "$arg" in --dry-run) export DRY_RUN=1 ;; *) PARSED_ARGS+=("$arg") ;; esac done } # Write an entry to the dry-run log (thread/process-safe append) # Usage: dry_log "ACTION" "TARGET_PATH" "DETAILS" dry_log() { local action="$1"; shift || true local target="$1"; shift || true local details="$*" local ts ts="$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")" { printf "[%s] DRY-RUN %-12s %s" "$ts" "$action" "$target" if [[ -n "$details" ]]; then printf " — %s" "$details"; fi printf "\n" } >> "$DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH" } # A helper that either writes content to a file (non-dry) or logs intent (dry) # Usage: maybe_write_file "/path/to/file" "CONTENT..." maybe_write_file() { local dest="$1"; shift || true if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "1" ]]; then dry_log "WOULD-WRITE" "$dest" "skipped actual write" return 0 fi # Normal mode: ensure parent dir exists and write content mkdir -p "$(dirname "$dest")" cat > "$dest" } # A helper that either copies a file (non-dry) or logs intent (dry) # Usage: maybe_copy "/path/from" "/path/to" maybe_copy() { local src="$1"; shift || true local dest="$1"; shift || true if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "1" ]]; then dry_log "WOULD-COPY" "$dest" "from=$src" return 0 fi mkdir -p "$(dirname "$dest")" cp "$src" "$dest" } # A helper that creates a directory (or logs intent in dry-run) # Usage: maybe_mkdir "/path/to/dir" maybe_mkdir() { local d="$1" if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "1" ]]; then dry_log "WOULD-MKDIR" "$d" "" return 0 fi mkdir -p "$d" } # Helper to advertise a planned artifact without actually writing # Usage: plan_artifact "/target/path" "description" plan_artifact() { local dest="$1"; shift || true local desc="$*" dry_log "WOULD-WRITE" "$dest" "$desc" } # Emit a banner line for a dry-run session # Usage: dry_run_banner "ComponentName" "Context details" dry_run_banner() { local component="$1"; shift || true local info="$*" local ts ts="$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")" { printf "[%s] DRY-RUN-BEGIN component=%s %s\n" "$ts" "$component" "$info" } >> "$DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH" } # Emit a closing line for a dry-run session # Usage: dry_run_end dry_run_end() { local ts ts="$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")" printf "[%s] DRY-RUN-END\n" "$ts" >> "$DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH" } SH chmod +x "$REPO/scripts/dry_run_utils.sh" # 2) Create README.md (or append if exists) with Dry-Run Mode instructions if [[ -f "$REPO/README.md" ]]; then cp "$REPO/README.md" "$REPO/README.md.bak" fi cat > "$REPO/README.md" << 'MD' # AzureML Evaluation Contract — Repository This repository contains artifacts and scripts for a standardized Azure ML evaluation contract, including schemas, sample outputs, and CI validation utilities. - docs/eval_contract.md — detailed evaluation contract - bundle_index.csv — machine-readable index of expected files - outputs/ — sample artifacts and schemas - scripts/ — validation and helper utilities ## Dry-Run Mode Dry-run mode allows you to execute pipelines in a non-destructive manner. Instead of writing files, the scripts record the intended write locations in a log file. Key points: - No files are created or modified when dry-run is enabled. - All intended writes are appended to dry_run_log.txt in the repository root by default. - Use this in CI/CD to validate paths and planned artifacts without touching storage. ### How to enable You can enable dry-run in two equivalent ways: 1) Environment variable: export DRY_RUN=1 bash scripts/your_script.sh 2) Command-line flag (for scripts that source scripts/dry_run_utils.sh): bash scripts/your_script.sh --dry-run Optional: override the log path with DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH export DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH=./logs/my_dry_run.txt export DRY_RUN=1 bash scripts/your_script.sh ### What gets logged Each intended write produces a line like: [2025-09-28T12:00:00Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-WRITE outputs/predictions.jsonl — skipped actual write [2025-09-28T12:00:00Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-MKDIR outputs/plots — [2025-09-28T12:00:00Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-WRITE outputs/metrics.json — aggregate metrics A session is marked by DRY-RUN-BEGIN and DRY-RUN-END lines with component/context details. ### Integrating in scripts Source the utility helpers at the top of writer scripts: source scripts/dry_run_utils.sh parse_dry_run_flag "$@"; set -- "${PARSED_ARGS[@]}" dry_run_banner "eval-writer" "run_id=${AZUREML_RUN_ID:-local}" # Plan an artifact plan_artifact "outputs/metrics.json" "aggregate metrics" # Write a file only when not in dry-run maybe_write_file "outputs/metrics.json" <<'JSON' {"accuracy": 0.95} JSON dry_run_end ### Validation in dry-run scripts/validate_bundle.sh supports dry-run. When DRY_RUN=1, validation checks that the dry-run log includes all required artifacts (e.g., outputs/predictions.jsonl, outputs/metrics.json, outputs/run_metadata.json, outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json). ### Clearing the log rm -f dry_run_log.txt MD # 3) Create a sample dry_run_log.txt demonstrating intended writes cat > "$REPO/dry_run_log.txt" << 'LOG' [2025-09-28T12:00:00Z] DRY-RUN-BEGIN component=eval-writer run_id=local-DRY ctx=model=spam-detector data=2025-09-26 [2025-09-28T12:00:01Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-MKDIR outputs — [2025-09-28T12:00:01Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-WRITE outputs/predictions.jsonl — skipped actual write [2025-09-28T12:00:01Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-WRITE outputs/metrics.json — aggregate metrics [2025-09-28T12:00:01Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-WRITE outputs/run_metadata.json — traceability metadata [2025-09-28T12:00:02Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-MKDIR outputs/schemas — [2025-09-28T12:00:02Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-WRITE outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json — schema definition [2025-09-28T12:00:02Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-WRITE outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json — schema definition (conditional) [2025-09-28T12:00:03Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-MKDIR outputs/plots — [2025-09-28T12:00:03Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-WRITE outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png — visualization (recommended) [2025-09-28T12:00:03Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-MKDIR outputs/diagnostics — [2025-09-28T12:00:03Z] DRY-RUN WOULD-WRITE outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json — calibration metrics (optional) [2025-09-28T12:00:04Z] DRY-RUN-END LOG # 4) Update validate_bundle.sh to respect DRY_RUN=1 and validate intents from the log if [[ -f "$REPO/scripts/validate_bundle.sh" ]]; then cp "$REPO/scripts/validate_bundle.sh" "$REPO/scripts/validate_bundle.sh.bak" fi cat > "$REPO/scripts/validate_bundle.sh" << 'SH' #!/usr/bin/env bash set -euo pipefail # Honor dry-run mode : "${DRY_RUN:=0}" : "${DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH:=./dry_run_log.txt}" required=( outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json ) if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "1" ]]; then if [[ ! -s "$DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH" ]]; then echo "DRY-RUN enabled but log not found or empty: $DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH" >&2 exit 1 fi missing=0 for f in "${required[@]}"; do if ! grep -E "WOULD-(WRITE|COPY).*\s$f(\s|$)" "$DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH" >/dev/null; then echo "DRY-RUN: required planned artifact missing in log: $f" >&2 missing=1 fi done if [[ "$missing" -eq 1 ]]; then echo "DRY-RUN validation FAILED: one or more required artifacts not planned." >&2 exit 1 fi echo "DRY-RUN validation passed: all required artifacts are planned in $DRY_RUN_LOG_PATH" exit 0 fi # Normal mode validation (files must exist and be non-empty) files=("${required[@]}") for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f" >&2 exit 1 fi done # Minimal JSON validation for metrics.json if present if command -v jq >/dev/null 2>&1; then jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid" >&2; exit 1; } fi echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x "$REPO/scripts/validate_bundle.sh" # 5) Show tree for confirmation ls -R "$REPO""}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: First, confirm Azure Service Bus message property names (e.g., contentType vs content_type, subject vs label) and JSON Schema draft version best suited. Then implement schema.json and a sample message payload with correct Service Bus envelope. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Service Bus message properties contentType subject applicationProperties JSON body example", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Azure Service Bus message structure (AMQP) and SDK properties","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/service-bus-messaging/service-bus-messages-payloads"},{"title":"Send and receive messages as JSON with Azure Service Bus (JS/TS)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/service-bus-messaging/service-bus-nodejs-how-to-use-queues#send-messages"},{"title":"Service Bus .NET client message properties","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/azure.messaging.servicebus.servicebusmessage"},{"title":"Service Bus REST/AMQP content type and subject/label mapping","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/service-bus-messaging/service-bus-messaging-overview#message-properties"}],"summary":"Azure Service Bus messages carry a body plus system and application properties. SDKs expose: contentType (string), subject (formerly label), correlationId, messageId, sessionId, applicationProperties (custom key/value). JSON bodies should set contentType to application/json. Subject is the recommended property name in Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus; legacy clients may use label. Application properties are useful for routing with rules/subscriptions."}} Now create a workspace directory and write a JSON Schema (Draft 2020-12) for the batch-complete message with fields: batchId (string, non-empty), rowCount (integer, >=0), checksum (string with hex format). Then create a sample payload JSON adhering to schema and a Service Bus message envelope example. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/batch_complete_msg && cat > /workspace/batch_complete_msg/schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "$id": "https://example.org/schemas/batch-complete.schema.json", "title": "BatchCompleteMessage", "description": "Schema for a batch-complete notification message.", "type": "object", "additionalProperties": false, "required": ["batchId", "rowCount", "checksum"], "properties": { "batchId": { "type": "string", "minLength": 1, "description": "Identifier for the completed batch; should be globally unique in the producer domain." }, "rowCount": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 0, "description": "Number of rows/items processed in the batch." }, "checksum": { "type": "string", "pattern": "^[A-Fa-f0-9]{32,128}$", "description": "Hex-encoded checksum (e.g., MD5=32 hex, SHA-256=64 hex, SHA-512=128 hex) of the batch contents." } }, "examples": [ { "batchId": "2025-09-28T12:00:00Z_ingest_042", "rowCount": 125000, "checksum": "9f2a6b5d3a4c7e8f1b0c2d4e6f8a1b3c9d2e4f6a8c0b2d4e6f8a1b3c5d7e9f0a" } ] } JSON # Sample body adhering to schema && cat > /workspace/batch_complete_msg/sample_payload.json << 'JSON' { "batchId": "batch-2025-09-28-00042", "rowCount": 150000, "checksum": "3e7a1f5c9b2d4e6f8a0c1b3d5e7f9a2c4d6e8f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a" } JSON # Example Azure Service Bus message envelope (as you would construct via SDKs) && cat > /workspace/batch_complete_msg/servicebus_message_example.json << 'JSON' { "body": { "batchId": "batch-2025-09-28-00042", "rowCount": 150000, "checksum": "3e7a1f5c9b2d4e6f8a0c1b3d5e7f9a2c4d6e8f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a" }, "contentType": "application/json", "subject": "batch.complete", "messageId": "batch-2025-09-28-00042", "applicationProperties": { "eventType": "batch.complete", "schemaId": "https://example.org/schemas/batch-complete.schema.json", "version": "1.0" } } JSON && ls -R /workspace/batch_complete_msg && echo "\nFiles created under /workspace/batch_complete_msg""} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/batch_complete_msg: sample_payload.json schema.json servicebus_message_example.json Files created under /workspace/batch_complete_msg ","stderr":""} Show contents to verify correctness and provide them in the final answer. Also, run jq to ensure JSON validity. {"type":"code","code_command":"jq . /workspace/batch_complete_msg/schema.json >/dev/null && jq . /workspace/batch_complete_msg/sample_payload.json >/dev/null && jq . /workspace/batch_complete_msg/servicebus_message_example.json >/dev/null && echo "JSON files validated with jq." && echo "\n--- schema.json ---" && cat /workspace/batch_complete_msg/schema.json && echo "\n--- sample_payload.json ---" && cat /workspace/batch_complete_msg/sample_payload.json && echo "\n--- servicebus_message_example.json ---" && cat /workspace/batch_complete_msg/servicebus_message_example.json"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"JSON files validated with jq. --- schema.json --- { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "$id": "https://example.org/schemas/batch-complete.schema.json", "title": "BatchCompleteMessage", "description": "Schema for a batch-complete notification message.", "type": "object", "additionalProperties": false, "required": [ "batchId", "rowCount", "checksum" ], "properties": { "batchId": { "type": "string", "minLength": 1, "description": "Identifier for the completed batch; should be globally unique in the producer domain." }, "rowCount": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 0, "description": "Number of rows/items processed in the batch." }, "checksum": { "type": "string", "pattern": "^[A-Fa-f0-9]{32,128}$", "description": "Hex-encoded checksum (e.g., MD5=32 hex, SHA-256=64 hex, SHA-512=128 hex) of the batch contents." } }, "examples": [ { "batchId": "2025-09-28T12:00:00Z_ingest_042", "rowCount": 125000, "checksum": "9f2a6b5d3a4c7e8f1b0c2d4e6f8a1b3c9d2e4f6a8c0b2d4e6f8a1b3c5d7e9f0a" } ] } --- sample_payload.json --- { "batchId": "batch-2025-09-28-00042", "rowCount": 150000, "checksum": "3e7a1f5c9b2d4e6f8a0c1b3d5e7f9a2c4d6e8f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a" } --- servicebus_message_example.json --- { "body": { "batchId": "batch-2025-09-28-00042", "rowCount": 150000, "checksum": "3e7a1f5c9b2d4e6f8a0c1b3d5e7f9a2c4d6e8f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a" }, "contentType": "application/json", "subject": "batch.complete", "messageId": "batch-2025-09-28-00042", "applicationProperties": { "eventType": "batch.complete", "schemaId": "https://example.org/schemas/batch-complete.schema.json", "version": "1.0" } } ","stderr":""} Provide final summary with file paths and include the sample payload and Service Bus message structure. Keep it concise. Done. Files created - /workspace/batch_complete_msg/schema.json — JSON Schema (Draft 2020-12) for the batch-complete message. - /workspace/batch_complete_msg/sample_payload.json — A sample payload conforming to the schema. - /workspace/batch_complete_msg/servicebus_message_example.json — Example Azure Service Bus topic message envelope. Schema (summary) - Fields: • batchId: string, required, non-empty. • rowCount: integer, required, >= 0. • checksum: string, required, hex-encoded (32–128 hex chars; supports MD5/SHA-256/SHA-512). - additionalProperties: false Sample payload (body) { "batchId": "batch-2025-09-28-00042", "rowCount": 150000, "checksum": "3e7a1f5c9b2d4e6f8a0c1b3d5e7f9a2c4d6e8f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a" } Azure Service Bus message example (for topic publish) { "body": { "batchId": "batch-2025-09-28-00042", "rowCount": 150000, "checksum": "3e7a1f5c9b2d4e6f8a0c1b3d5e7f9a2c4d6e8f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a" }, "contentType": "application/json", "subject": "batch.complete", "messageId": "batch-2025-09-28-00042", "applicationProperties": { "eventType": "batch.complete", "schemaId": "https://example.org/schemas/batch-complete.schema.json", "version": "1.0" } } Notes - contentType should be application/json for JSON bodies. - subject is the recommended property (legacy clients may call it label). - Use applicationProperties for routing and schema/version metadata. You can now publish the sample payload to your Azure Service Bus topic using your preferred SDK, setting the body to the JSON object above and the properties as shown.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Plan: Create a workspace directory, generate a 20-row dataset in Python, split into 10 chunks (2 rows each), write chunk_sizes.csv, and produce two sample JSON files (message_batch_examples_1.json, message_batch_examples_2.json) that show how a single chunk would be packaged as a message. Then verify by listing and showing file snippets. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -euo pipefail mkdir -p /workspace/message_chunking python - << 'PY' import json, csv, os out_dir = "/workspace/message_chunking" os.makedirs(out_dir, exist_ok=True) # Create a 20-row "DataFrame" equivalent as list-of-dicts rows = [ { "id": i, "title": f"Record {i}", "text": f"This is an example text for row {i}.", "category": ["alpha","beta","gamma","delta"][ (i-1) % 4 ] } for i in range(1, 21) ] # Split into 10 message-sized chunks (2 rows each) num_chunks = 10 n = len(rows) base = n // num_chunks # 2 rem = n % num_chunks # 0 sizes = [base + (1 if i < rem else 0) for i in range(num_chunks)] chunks = [] start = 0 for cid, sz in enumerate(sizes, start=1): end = start + sz chunk_rows = rows[start:end] chunks.append({ "chunk_id": cid, "start_index": start, # 0-based "end_index_inclusive": end - 1 if sz > 0 else start - 1, "size": sz, "rows": chunk_rows, }) start = end # Write chunk_sizes.csv csv_path = os.path.join(out_dir, "chunk_sizes.csv") with open(csv_path, "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f: w = csv.writer(f) w.writerow(["chunk_id","start_index","end_index_inclusive","size"]) for c in chunks: w.writerow([c["chunk_id"], c["start_index"], c["end_index_inclusive"], c["size"]]) # Produce two sample message batch examples for chunk 1 and 2 for example_idx in [1, 2]: c = chunks[example_idx - 1] # Build a chat-like message payload with system + user messages # The user message contains a compact JSONL of the rows in the chunk. jsonl = "\n".join(json.dumps(r, ensure_ascii=False) for r in c["rows"]) payload = { "chunk_id": c["chunk_id"], "messages": [ { "role": "system", "content": "You are given a chunk of rows from a dataset. Process them as needed." }, { "role": "user", "content": ( f"Chunk {c['chunk_id']} of {num_chunks}.\n" f"Rows (JSONL with {c['size']} records):\n{jsonl}" ) } ], "metadata": { "total_chunks": num_chunks, "num_rows": c["size"], "row_ids": [r["id"] for r in c["rows"]], "columns": list(c["rows"][0].keys()) if c["rows"] else [] } } out_json = os.path.join(out_dir, f"message_batch_examples_{example_idx}.json") with open(out_json, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f: json.dump(payload, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2) print("Wrote:") print(csv_path) print(os.path.join(out_dir, "message_batch_examples_1.json")) print(os.path.join(out_dir, "message_batch_examples_2.json")) PY ls -l /workspace/message_chunking printf "\n--- chunk_sizes.csv (first 12 lines) ---\n" && head -n 12 /workspace/message_chunking/chunk_sizes.csv printf "\n--- message_batch_examples_1.json (snippet) ---\n" && sed -n '1,40p' /workspace/message_chunking/message_batch_examples_1.json printf "\n--- message_batch_examples_2.json (snippet) ---\n" && sed -n '1,40p' /workspace/message_chunking/message_batch_examples_2.json"}
