Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll create a small workspace under /workspace/deadletter_policy, then add a comprehensive deadletter_policy.md and a mock quarantine.csv with one bad message that hit failures > 2. I'll also add a simple validator script to verify the CSV aligns with the policy. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/deadletter_policy/{docs,data,scripts} && ls -R /workspace/deadletter_policy"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/deadletter_policy: data docs scripts /workspace/deadletter_policy/data: /workspace/deadletter_policy/docs: /workspace/deadletter_policy/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I'll write the dead-letter and quarantine policy document capturing the rule (failures > 2 → quarantine), state transitions, retry/backoff, observability, data handling, runbook, and the CSV schema. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/deadletter_policy/docs/deadletter_policy.md << 'MD' Dead-Letter and Quarantine Policy Version: 1.0.0 Date: 2025-09-28 1) Purpose and Scope - This policy governs handling of message-processing failures across streaming/queue consumers (system-agnostic: applies to Kafka, Service Bus, SQS, Pub/Sub, etc.). - Objective: minimize reprocessing loops, protect downstream systems, and provide deterministic triage for poison or malformed messages. 2) Core Rule (Threshold) - If a message accumulates failures > 2 (i.e., the third failure), it must be quarantined immediately and excluded from the normal retry path. - Equivalently, max_failures_allowed = 2. After the 3rd failed attempt, quarantine. 3) Definitions - Attempt: One execution of the message handler. The initial processing counts as attempt 1; each retry increments attempts. - Failure count: The number of failed attempts for the specific message_id. Includes initial failure and any retries that failed. - Retryable (transient) error: Likely resolvable with time (e.g., network timeout, 5xx, rate limit). Eligible for retry up to threshold. - Non-retryable (permanent) error: Unlikely to succeed on retry (e.g., schema violation, missing required field, validation failure). Quarantine immediately without consuming the retry budget. - Quarantine: Durable isolation of the failed message and metadata for human triage and optional reprocessing. Quarantine is distinct from a DLQ queue; it may be a data store (CSV, blob, DB) plus an ops workflow. 4) State Transitions - Received → Processing - Processing → Succeeded (ack) - Processing → Failed (failure_count += 1) - Failed (transient) and failure_count ≤ 2 → Retry with backoff - Failed (transient) and failure_count > 2 → Quarantine - Failed (non-retryable) → Quarantine (immediate) 5) Backoff and Jitter (for transient failures) - Exponential backoff with full jitter to avoid thundering herds. - Example schedule (seconds): 1s, 5s, 25s for attempts 2 and 3 (two retries maximum). - Formula (example): delay = min(base * 2^(attempt-2), max_backoff) * random(0.5, 1.5) - Recommended defaults: base=1s, max_backoff=30s. 6) Idempotency and Ordering - All handlers must be idempotent by key (message_id) to allow safe retries. - Maintain at-least-once delivery semantics; avoid in-process dedup except for idempotency checks. - Where strict ordering is required, ensure order keys (e.g., partition key) align with handler capabilities. 7) Observability and Alerting - Emit metrics: - handler_attempts_total{status=[success|failure]} - handler_failures_total{error_type} - quarantine_events_total{reason} - retry_attempts_total - processing_latency_ms (p50, p95, p99) - Logs must include: message_id, attempt, error class, stack trace, decision (retry/quarantine), and correlation_id if present. - Page-on-call if quarantine rate exceeds a threshold (e.g., > 10 per 5 minutes) or if any single message triggers immediate quarantine due to non-retryable error. 8) Data Handling in Quarantine - Payloads may contain PII/PHI. Apply the following: - Prefer base64 encoding for raw payloads to preserve fidelity and avoid CSV escaping issues. - Redact known PII fields if present (e.g., email, phone) in a separate redacted view. Store full payloads in a secure location with restricted access. - Encrypt at rest; restrict ACLs to on-call engineers and data stewards. - Retention: 30 days (configurable) or until remediation is complete, whichever is later. 9) Quarantine Record Schema (CSV) - File: data/quarantine.csv - Delimiter: comma - Header columns (names and notes): - quarantined_at: ISO 8601 UTC timestamp when quarantined - message_id: globally unique identifier for the message - source: source system or topic/queue name - partition: partition/shard name or ID (optional; use "" if n/a) - offset_or_sequence: source offset/sequence number for traceability - handler: logical name of the consumer/handler that failed - failure_count: integer total failures for this message (must be > 2 for threshold-based quarantine; may be 1 if non-retryable) - last_error: concise error summary (class:message) - quarantine_reason: one of [threshold_exceeded|non_retryable] - content_type: MIME type of payload (e.g., application/json) - raw_payload_base64: base64-encoded original payload bytes - schema_version: version of message schema expected by the handler (if applicable) - pii_redacted: true|false indicating whether PII was redacted in this CSV artifact - checksum_sha256: SHA-256 of the original payload bytes for integrity 10) Handler Pseudocode - Language-agnostic pseudocode: on_message(msg): id = msg.message_id attempts = load_failure_count(id) // defaults to 0 if unseen try: validate_schema(msg) process(msg) ack(msg) record_metric(success) clear_failure_count(id) except NonRetryableError as e: log_error(id, attempts+1, e, decision="quarantine") quarantine(msg, attempts+1, reason="non_retryable", last_error=e) nack_without_retry(msg) except TransientError as e: attempts += 1 record_metric(failure, error_type="transient") if attempts <= 2: schedule_retry_with_backoff(msg, attempts) save_failure_count(id, attempts) else: log_error(id, attempts, e, decision="quarantine") quarantine(msg, attempts, reason="threshold_exceeded", last_error=e) nack_without_retry(msg) 11) Operational Runbook - Triage - Review quarantine.csv entries; pivot by handler and error_type. - Inspect last_error and sample payload (decode base64 in a secure environment). - Determine if the issue is schema drift, data quality, dependency outage, or code bug. - Remediation - For schema/data issues: update schema mapping or sanitize upstream. - For code bugs: fix, deploy, and test against quarantined samples. - Replay - After fix, re-queue quarantined messages through a controlled replay tool that enforces idempotency and limits throughput. - Upon successful processing, archive or remove entries from quarantine.csv. 12) Compliance and Retention - Retain quarantine logs and artifacts for 30 days or per policy . - Ensure access auditing and encryption at rest in the storage backing quarantine artifacts. 13) Quality Gates - Any quarantined record with failure_count ≤ 2 must have quarantine_reason=non_retryable; otherwise it violates the threshold rule. - All quarantined records must include checksum_sha256 and content_type. - CSV must include a header row exactly matching the schema in section 9. 14) Examples - Threshold exceeded: a message timed out three times when calling a dependency. - Non-retryable: invalid JSON or missing required key (e.g., user_id). 15) Change Management - Changes to threshold or backoff require review and version bump. - Document in changelog; update runbooks and validators accordingly. MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next I'll create the mock quarantine.csv with a single quarantined record that hit the threshold (failure_count=3) due to an invalid JSON payload. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/deadletter_policy/data/quarantine.csv << 'CSV' quarantined_at,message_id,source,partition,offset_or_sequence,handler,failure_count,last_error,quarantine_reason,content_type,raw_payload_base64,schema_version,pii_redacted,checksum_sha256 2025-09-28T12:45:03Z,5f2a3c1e-8d4b-4d3b-9e9a-7b1c5f8e2c11,orders-events,2,1048576,orders_enricher,3,JSONDecodeError: Expecting ',' delimiter at pos 42,threshold_exceeded,application/json,eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxMjMsImVtYWlsIjoiYWxpY2VAZXhhbXBsZS5jb20iICJldmVudCI6InNpZ251cCIsInRzIjoiMjAyNS0wOS0yOFQxMjozNDo1NloifQ==,v2,false,e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855 CSV && echo '--- quarantine.csv created ---' && wc -l /workspace/deadletter_policy/data/quarantine.csv && echo && head -n 2 /workspace/deadletter_policy/data/quarantine.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"--- quarantine.csv created --- 2 /workspace/deadletter_policy/data/quarantine.csv quarantined_at,message_id,source,partition,offset_or_sequence,handler,failure_count,last_error,quarantine_reason,content_type,raw_payload_base64,schema_version,pii_redacted,checksum_sha256 2025-09-28T12:45:03Z,5f2a3c1e-8d4b-4d3b-9e9a-7b1c5f8e2c11,orders-events,2,1048576,orders_enricher,3,JSONDecodeError: Expecting ',' delimiter at pos 42,threshold_exceeded,application/json,eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxMjMsImVtYWlsIjoiYWxpY2VAZXhhbXBsZS5jb20iICJldmVudCI6InNpZ251cCIsInRzIjoiMjAyNS0wOS0yOFQxMjozNDo1NloifQ==,v2,false,e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855 ","stderr":""} I'll add a tiny validator script that enforces the policy: quarantine.csv exists, has a header, at least one row, and that at least one row has failure_count > 2 (or reason=non_retryable if ≤ 2). Then run it to verify. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/deadletter_policy/scripts/validate_quarantine.sh << 'SH' #!/usr/bin/env bash set -euo pipefail FILE="/workspace/deadletter_policy/data/quarantine.csv" if [[ ! -s "$FILE" ]]; then echo "ERROR: quarantine.csv missing or empty" >&2 exit 1 fi # Ensure header exists and includes failure_count and quarantine_reason header=$(head -n1 "$FILE") if ! echo "$header" | grep -q "failure_count"; then echo "ERROR: Header missing failure_count" >&2; exit 1; fi if ! echo "$header" | grep -q "quarantine_reason"; then echo "ERROR: Header missing quarantine_reason" >&2; exit 1; fi # Determine the index of failure_count and quarantine_reason (1-based) fc_idx=$(awk -F',' 'NR==1{for(i=1;i<=NF;i++){if($i=="failure_count")print i}}' "$FILE") qr_idx=$(awk -F',' 'NR==1{for(i=1;i<=NF;i++){if($i=="quarantine_reason")print i}}' "$FILE") if [[ -z "$fc_idx" || -z "$qr_idx" ]]; then echo "ERROR: Could not locate required columns" >&2; exit 1; fi # Validate at least one row satisfies the policy (failure_count>2 or reason=non_retryable) awk -F',' -v fi="$fc_idx" -v qi="$qr_idx" 'NR>1{fc=$fi+0; qr=$qi; if(fc>2 || qr=="non_retryable") ok=1} END{if(ok!=1) exit 1}' "$FILE" \ && echo "Policy satisfied: found quarantined record meeting criteria" \ || { echo "ERROR: No record meets policy criteria" >&2; exit 1; } SH chmod +x /workspace/deadletter_policy/scripts/validate_quarantine.sh && /workspace/deadletter_policy/scripts/validate_quarantine.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Policy satisfied: found quarantined record meeting criteria ","stderr":""} Created the dead-letter policy and a mock quarantine file. What I delivered - Policy document: - /workspace/deadletter_policy/docs/deadletter_policy.md - Describes the rule “failures > 2 → quarantine” (third failure triggers quarantine), state transitions, retry/backoff, observability and alerts, data handling, CSV schema, pseudocode, runbook, and quality gates. - Mock quarantine CSV: - /workspace/deadletter_policy/data/quarantine.csv - Contains one quarantined record with failure_count=3 due to invalid JSON. - Header columns: quarantined_at, message_id, source, partition, offset_or_sequence, handler, failure_count, last_error, quarantine_reason, content_type, raw_payload_base64, schema_version, pii_redacted, checksum_sha256 - Optional validator (ran successfully): - /workspace/deadletter_policy/scripts/validate_quarantine.sh - Output: Policy satisfied: found quarantined record meeting criteria Example quarantined row (from quarantine.csv) 2025-09-28T12:45:03Z,5f2a3c1e-8d4b-4d3b-9e9a-7b1c5f8e2c11,orders-events,2,1048576,orders_enricher,3,JSONDecodeError: Expecting ',' delimiter at pos 42,threshold_exceeded,application/json,eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxMjMsImVtYWlsIjoiYWxpY2VAZXhhbXBsZS5jb20iICJldmVudCI6InNpZ251cCIsInRzIjoiMjAyNS0wOS0yOFQxMjozNDo1NloifQ==,v2,false,e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855 Notes - The CSV stores raw payloads in base64 to avoid CSV escaping issues and preserve fidelity. Use a secure workflow to decode and inspect. - The policy supports immediate quarantine on non-retryable errors even if failure_count ≤ 2. - Retention, access control, and encryption are outlined in the policy doc; adjust to your environment. If you want this adapted for a specific platform (e.g., Kafka DLQ topic, Azure Service Bus dead-letter subqueue, or AWS SQS DLQ), I can tailor the handler pseudocode and operational steps accordingly.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

|| { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, content.text: I'll quickly consult official sources on ADLS Gen2 medallion/layered architecture to ensure accurate distinctions between raw and curated zones before creating the files. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 raw zone curated zone medallion architecture bronze silver gold best practices schema enforcement quality checks partitioning", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Medallion architecture for lakehouses","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/databricks/lakehouse/medallion-architecture"},{"title":"Best practices for data lakes with ADLS Gen2","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/data-lake-storage-best-practices"},{"title":"Data Lake zones (raw, curated, trusted)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/architecture/data-guide/big-data/data-lake"},{"title":"Schema enforcement and evolution with Delta Lake","url":"https://docs.delta.io/latest/delta-batch.html"},{"title":"Data governance with Microsoft Purview","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/purview/concept-data-catalog-overview"}],"summary":"In ADLS Gen2, a layered approach is common: raw (landing/source-aligned, immutable, schema-on-read), curated (conformed/consumer-aligned, schema enforced, quality checks applied), and often a presentation/gold zone. Raw stores exactly-as-received data, partitioned by ingestion timestamps; curated uses standardized formats (Parquet/Delta), business-friendly partitioning, and enforces DQ, schema, and governance with tighter ACLs. Delta Lake features (schema enforcement, time travel) are commonly used in curated."}} Now I'll scaffold a project directory and create the requested files: layout_diagram.md and conformance_report.csv. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/adls_gen2/docs && mkdir -p /workspace/adls_gen2/reports && ls -R /workspace/adls_gen2"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/adls_gen2: docs reports /workspace/adls_gen2/docs: /workspace/adls_gen2/reports: ","stderr":""} Write a detailed layout_diagram.md explaining raw vs curated zones with structure examples, policies, and an ASCII/mermaid diagram. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/adls_gen2/docs/layout_diagram.md << 'MD' # ADLS Gen2 Layout Diagram — raw/ vs curated/ This document illustrates how data is organized and governed across the raw and curated zones in an Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 account. It also highlights key differences in purpose, structure, quality, and access controls. High-level flow - Sources (operational systems, SaaS, streaming) → raw/ (source-aligned, immutable landing) → curated/ (conformed, quality-enforced) → downstream consumers (warehouse, lakehouse, ML/BI) ASCII layout abfss://datalake@.dfs.core.windows.net/ │ ├─ raw/ # Source-aligned, as-received data (append-only) │ ├─ / │ │ ├─ / │ │ │ ├─ _meta/ # Optional ingestion manifests, checksums, source schema │ │ │ ├─ ingestion_date=YYYY-MM-DD/ │ │ │ │ ├─ batch_id=/ # Optional for replays/retries │ │ │ │ │ ├─ file1.json │ │ │ │ │ ├─ file2.csv │ │ │ │ │ └─ ... # No transformation; keep provenance intact │ │ │ └─ quarantine/ # Malformed files (optional) │ │ └─ ... │ └─ ... │ └─ curated/ # Consumer-aligned, standardized & governed ├─ / # e.g., sales, marketing, finance, hr, operations │ ├─ / │ │ ├─ format=delta/ # Standardized storage format (Delta/Parquet) │ │ ├─ _schema/ # Conformed schemas, contracts, constraints │ │ ├─ _dq/ # Data quality results & conformance reports │ │ ├─ year=YYYY/ │ │ │ ├─ month=MM/ │ │ │ │ ├─ day=DD/ │ │ │ │ │ ├─ part-000.parquet │ │ │ │ │ └─ ... │ │ └─ _change_data/ # Optional: CDC, SCD, or audit trails │ └─ ... └─ ... Key differences 1) Purpose - raw/: Landing area for exactly-as-received data. Optimized for ingestion throughput and lineage retention. - curated/: Conformed and consumer-aligned layer for analytics/BI/ML. Optimized for query performance and governance. 2) Transformations & Quality - raw/: No transformations beyond minimal normalization (optional). Schema-on-read; tolerate semi-structured formats. Data may be incomplete or duplicate. - curated/: Schema enforcement, constraint validation (nullability, types, PK/FK), deduplication, standardization, SCD handling, CDC application, and PII masking/redaction. Data quality and conformance checks recorded. 3) Formats & Partitioning - raw/: Mixed formats (CSV/JSON/Avro/Parquet), partitioned by ingestion time (ingestion_date) and optionally batch_id. - curated/: Standardized formats (Parquet/Delta), partitioned by business attributes (year/month/day, region, tenant) for performance. Delta Lake features (schema enforcement, time travel, optimize/vacuum) recommended. 4) Governance & Security - raw/: Restricted write access to ingestion identities; broad read is limited. Holds sensitive data with appropriate encryption; access tightly controlled. - curated/: Fine-grained ACLs, table- or column-level security, row-level filtering where applicable. Registered in catalog (e.g., Microsoft Purview) with business glossary and lineage. 5) Metadata & Catalog - raw/: Minimal technical metadata, ingestion manifests, and source schema snapshots in _meta/. - curated/: Rich metadata, versioned schemas in _schema/, DQ reports in _dq/, and data contracts documented for consumers. 6) SLA & Serve - raw/: Not consumer-serving. No freshness SLA; used as provenance layer. - curated/: Consumer-serving with documented SLAs (freshness, reliability), and non-breaking schema evolution policies. Mermaid diagram (optional rendering) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Systems] -->|Ingest (batch/stream)| R[raw/] R -->|ETL/ELT, DQ, Conformance| C[curated/] C --> W[Warehouse/Lakehouse] C --> BI[BI & Reporting] C --> ML[ML & Feature Store] ``` Naming & path conventions - raw///ingestion_date=YYYY-MM-DD/batch_id=/file.ext - curated///format=delta/year=YYYY/month=MM/day=DD/part-*.parquet Examples - raw/crm/sales_orders/ingestion_date=2025-09-27/file_001.json - curated/sales/fact_orders/format=delta/year=2025/month=09/day=27/part-000.parquet Operational policies (summary) - raw/: append-only; never mutate or delete except retention/quarantine policies. - curated/: managed with ACID (Delta), optimized/compacted regularly, vacuum per retention policy, schema evolution via governed change control. Data quality checkpoints (applied before/into curated/) - Schema conformance: field types, required columns - Constraints: primary key uniqueness, foreign key integrity - Nullability: critical fields non-null - Value ranges and reference lists - PII protection: redaction/tokenization where required - Freshness: within SLA thresholds - Deduplication & CDC handling MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now create the mock conformance_report.csv demonstrating passing checks for curated datasets. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/adls_gen2/reports/conformance_report.csv << 'CSV' zone,domain,dataset,table,partition,snapshot_at,row_count,null_check,schema_compliance,pk_uniqueness,fk_integrity,value_ranges,pii_protection,freshness_sla_hrs,freshness_actual_hrs,status,details curated,sales,orders,fact_orders,year=2025/month=09/day=27,2025-09-27T07:15:00Z,128345,PASS,PASS,PASS,PASS,PASS,PASS,4,1.2,PASS,All checks passed; No freshness SLA; used as provenance layer. - curated/: Consumer-serving with documented SLAs (freshness, reliability), and non-breaking schema evolution policies. body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I want to ground the sample message fields and consumer guidance in Azure Service Bus conventions (message properties like MessageId, CorrelationId, Subject, ContentType, ApplicationProperties, SessionId; delivery characteristics such as at-least-once, duplicates, PeekLock, dead-letter). I will do a quick web search of official docs to ensure correct property names and best-practice guidance to inform both the messages_sample.json and consumer_responsibilities.md. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Service Bus message properties MessageId CorrelationId Subject ContentType ApplicationProperties SessionId PeekLock at-least-once duplicate delivery dead-letter best practices Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus concepts and message structure","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/service-bus-messaging/service-bus-messages-payloads"},{"title":"Receive messages with Service Bus (PeekLock, settlement, dead-letter)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/service-bus-messaging/service-bus-queues-topics-subscriptions#receive-messages"},{"title":"Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus SDK reference (message properties)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/azure.messaging.servicebus.servicebusmessage"},{"title":"Sessions and message ordering in Service Bus","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/service-bus-messaging/message-sessions"},{"title":"Duplicate detection, at-least-once delivery semantics","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/service-bus-messaging/duplicate-detection"},{"title":"Troubleshoot and handle poison messages / DLQ","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/service-bus-messaging/service-bus-dead-letter-queues"}],"summary":"Azure Service Bus messages have user-defined body plus properties like MessageId, CorrelationId, Subject, ContentType, ApplicationProperties, and optional SessionId for ordered delivery. Delivery is at-least-once; duplicates can occur; implement idempotency. Receive mode PeekLock enables processing then settle (Complete/Abandon/Defer/DeadLetter). Sessions provide FIFO per SessionId. Duplicate detection can be enabled at entity level but consumers should still dedupe. Dead-letter queues store messages that can’t be processed; include a reason/description. The Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus SDK exposes these properties directly."} With property names and delivery semantics confirmed, I will scaffold a small workspace directory and create: - samples/messages_sample.json with two example messages (succeeded and failed ETL run), including a realistic Service Bus property block and a rich body that downstreams can act upon. - docs/consumer_responsibilities.md detailing required consumer behavior: idempotent writes, PeekLock settlement, retry/DLQ strategy, schema/version handling, ordering via sessions, observability, security, and SLAs. Then I’ll list the files created so the user can find them. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e mkdir -p /workspace/azure_service_bus_etl/{samples,docs} cat > "sales_daily|2025-09-27|us-east|attempt=1|succeeded", "traceparent": "00-4bf92f3577b34da6a3ce929d0e0e4736-00f067aa0ba902b7-01", "tracestate": "rojo=00f067aa0ba902b7" } }, "body": { "event_id": "duration_ms": 901209 }, "dataset": { "name": "sales_curated", "version": "v2025.09", "uri": "abfss://curated@datalake.dfs.core.windows.net/sales/dt=2025-09-27/region=us-east/", "partition_keys": {"dt": "2025-09-27", "region": "us-east"}, "row_count_input": 12456789, "row_count_output": 12455001, "bytes_output": 856234567, "checksum": "sha256:5e884898da28047151d0e56f8dc62927", "schema_uri":
