"null_rate_under_threshold": true, "schema_compatible": true }, "anomalies": [] }, "metrics": { "records_processed": 12456789, "records_output": 12455001, "throughput_rps": 2456.7, "error_count": 0, "warning_count": 12 }, "links": { "logs_url": "https://logger.example.com/runs/2025-09-27T23:50:00Z", "workflow_url": "https://orchestrator.example.com/flows/sales_daily/runs/2025-09-27", "data_catalog": "https://catalog.example.com/datasets/sales_curated" }, "idempotency": { "key": "sales_daily|2025-09-27|us-east|attempt=1|succeeded", "content_hash": "sha256:5e884898da28047151d0e56f8dc62927" } } }, { "service_bus": { "message_id": "e9805a4d-f8d0-4c0f-9f89-84a61ffb5e07", "correlation_id": "run-2025-09-28T01:23:45Z-abc123", "subject": "etl.run.completed", "content_type": "application/json", "session_id": "etl_jobs/sales_daily/2025-09-27/region=us-west", "application_properties": { "schema_version": "1.0.0", "environment": "prod", "region": "westus3", "etl_job_id": "sales_daily", "run_id": "2025-09-27T23:50:00Z", "attempt": 2, "status": "failed", "dataset": "sales_curated", "partition": "dt=2025-09-27/region=us-west", "idempotency_key": "sales_daily|2025-09-27|us-west|attempt=2|failed", "traceparent": "00-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa-bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbb-01" } }, "body": { "event_id": "b4f6f9e6-0e15-4c2b-8f7c-ea6f9de58c2b", "event_type": "etl.run.completed", "emitted_at": "2025-09-28T00:10:03.120Z", "producer": {"name": "etl-orchestrator", "version": "3.4.2"}, "etl_job": { "id": "sales_daily", "name": "sales_daily", "run_id": "2025-09-27T23:50:00Z", "attempt": 2, "status": "failed", "started_at": "2025-09-28T00:06:00.000Z", "completed_at": "2025-09-28T00:10:00.000Z", "duration_ms": 240000 }, "dataset": { "name": "sales_curated", "version": "v2025.09", "uri": null, "partition_keys": {"dt": "2025-09-27", "region": "us-west"}, "row_count_input": 0, "row_count_output": 0, "bytes_output": 0, "checksum": null, "schema_uri": "https://schemas.example.com/sales_curated/1-0-0.json" }, "lineage": { "source_system": "pos-ingest", "source_assets": [], "previous_run_id": "2025-09-26T23:50:00Z" }, "quality": { "validation_passed": false, "tests": { "row_count_nonzero": false, "schema_compatible": false }, "anomalies": [ {"name": "schema_mismatch", "severity": "high", "details": "Missing column: store_timezone"} ] }, "metrics": { "records_processed": 0, "records_output": 0, "throughput_rps": 0, "error_count": 3, "warning_count": 0 }, "links": { "logs_url": "https://logger.example.com/runs/2025-09-27T23:50:00Z/attempt-2", "workflow_url": "https://orchestrator.example.com/flows/sales_daily/runs/2025-09-27", "data_catalog": "https://catalog.example.com/datasets/sales_curated" }, "error": { "type": "SchemaValidationError", "message": "Output schema mismatch detected during write.", "code": "ETL_SCHEMA_MISMATCH", "retryable": false }, "idempotency": { "key": "sales_daily|2025-09-27|us-west|attempt=2|failed", "content_hash": null } } } ], "notes": [ "Properties reflect Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus naming (e.g., MessageId -> message_id, SessionId -> session_id).", "SessionId provides per-partition ordering; consumers must enable sessions to receive in order.", "Idempotency key is provided to support dedup on the consumer side.", "Subject and ContentType are populated for consistency; ApplicationProperties carry routing and metadata." ] } JSON cat > /workspace/azure_service_bus_etl/docs/consumer_responsibilities.md << 'MD' Title: Downstream Consumer Responsibilities for ETL Completion Topic (Azure Service Bus) Audience: Teams consuming the etl-completion topic to trigger downstream loads, materializations, caches, or audits. 1) Transport assumptions (what Service Bus guarantees) - Delivery is at-least-once. Duplicates can occur. Out-of-order delivery is possible unless Sessions are used and respected. - Default receive mode should be PeekLock. Process first, then settle (Complete) when durable work is finished. - Messages may expire (TTL) or be dead-lettered. Poison messages must be handled from the DLQ. - System properties like sequence number and enqueued time are assigned by the broker; do not rely on them for idempotency. 2) Versioning and validation (reject unknown contracts) - Validate content_type == application/json and application_properties.schema_version. For unknown MAJOR versions, dead-letter with reason SchemaVersionUnsupported. - Validate required body fields: event_type, etl_job.id, etl_job.run_id, etl_job.status, dataset.name, idempotency.key. If missing/invalid, dead-letter with reason SchemaValidationFailed. - Log correlation identifiers: message_id, correlation_id, session_id, body.event_id. 3) Idempotent processing (exactly-once effects at the sink) - Implement idempotency keyed by one of: - application_properties.idempotency_key, or - tuple (etl_job.id, etl_job.run_id, dataset.name, dataset.partition_keys, attempt) - Maintain a durable dedupe store (e.g., processed_events table) with columns: idempotency_key, event_id, checksum, processed_at, status, version. - On receipt: - If idempotency_key already processed with same checksum: treat as duplicate; Complete without re-applying side effects. - If key exists with different checksum: handle as conflicting update; escalate and dead-letter with reason IdempotencyConflict. - If not processed: begin a transaction to upsert sink state and record the idempotency_key atomically. Only Complete the message after commit succeeds. - Prefer an upsert or merge operation for sinks (databases, indices, caches). For files, write to a temp path and atomically move/rename. 4) Ordering (Sessions) - If the topic/subscription uses SessionId to enforce partition-ordering (e.g., per etl_job + partition), open a session receiver and process one session at a time or with controlled parallelism. - Do not assume global ordering. If sessions are not used, treat messages as potentially out-of-order and guard with idempotent writes. 5) Settlement and retries - Use PeekLock receive. After durable work completes, call Complete. - For transient errors (timeouts, throttling, retriable I/O): Abandon to requeue and retry with exponential backoff and jitter; consider deferring if immediate retry is harmful. - For non-retriable errors (schema incompatibility, missing required fields, unauthorized access to dataset URI): Dead-letter with DeadLetterReason and DeadLetterErrorDescription indicating the cause and remediation. - Configure and respect lock duration. If processing may exceed lock duration, enable auto lock renewal or renew locks manually. 6) Dead-letter queue (DLQ) handling - Monitor the DLQ. Create alerts for spikes. Implement a replay tool to inspect, fix, and re-publish when appropriate. - Stamp DLQ messages with clear reason codes: SchemaVersionUnsupported, SchemaValidationFailed, IdempotencyConflict, AuthorizationFailed, DownstreamPermanentFailure. 7) Security and access - Authenticate to Service Bus using Azure AD where possible. Use least privilege: Azure Service Bus Data Receiver role for consumers. - Data URIs (e.g., abfss://...) may require separate credentials/managed identity. Validate access before starting processing. 8) Observability and tracing - Propagate distributed tracing headers if present (traceparent, tracestate). Include message_id, correlation_id, session_id in logs. - Emit structured logs and metrics: processing_latency_ms, retries, completes, abandons, deadletters, duplicate_suppressed_count. - Add dashboards and alerts for backlog depth, DLQ depth, and processing SLAs. 9) Behavior by ETL status - status == succeeded: Read dataset.uri, validate presence and expected partition, then apply idempotent load to sink. If quality.validation_passed is false despite succeeded status, treat as policy violation; dead-letter or quarantine per team policy. - status == failed: Do not attempt data load. Persist failure metadata to your audit store, raise alerts, and Complete the message to avoid retries unless a retry-on-failure policy applies. 10) Time-to-live and lateness - If message TTL has expired or the message is outside your acceptable freshness window, follow policy: ignore + Complete, or dead-letter with reason StaleMessage. 11) Contract changes and forward compatibility - Treat schema_version minor/patch changes as compatible; log and proceed. - For major version increments, stop-and-ask: dead-letter or park until the consumer is upgraded. 12) Minimal processing algorithm (outline) - Receive (PeekLock) -> Validate schema + version -> Check dedupe store (by idempotency_key). - If duplicate: log and Complete. - Else: process data (read dataset.uri), upsert/load into sink atomically with recording dedupe entry -> on success Complete -> on transient failure Abandon -> on permanent failure DeadLetter. 13) Configuration recommendations - maxConcurrentCalls: set based on sink throughput; start conservative (e.g., 8) and tune. - PrefetchCount: set to reduce latency (e.g., 50–200) while watching memory. - AutoCompleteMessages: false (use manual Complete after durable write). - Enable auto lock renewal if processing duration is variable or long. 14) Testing checklist - Duplicate delivery: send the same message twice -> consumer completes once, second is suppressed. - Out-of-order delivery: late arrival of a prior attempt -> suppressed. - Transient sink failure: abandon triggers retry and success on next attempt. - Permanent error: consumer dead-letters with clear reason. - Major schema bump: consumer dead-letters with SchemaVersionUnsupported. - Session ordering: verify FIFO within a session under parallel load. Appendix A: Field mapping reference - service_bus.message_id -> Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus.ServiceBusMessage.MessageId - service_bus.correlation_id -> ServiceBusMessage.CorrelationId - service_bus.subject -> ServiceBusMessage.Subject - service_bus.content_type -> ServiceBusMessage.ContentType - service_bus.session_id -> ServiceBusMessage.SessionId - service_bus.application_properties -> ServiceBusMessage.ApplicationProperties (string->object map) Appendix B: Idempotency key recommendation - Default: application_properties.idempotency_key - Fallback composition: etl_job.id + '|' + etl_job.run_id + '|' + dataset.partition_keys serialized + '|attempt=' + etl_job.attempt + '|' + etl_job.status MD ls -R /workspace/azure_service_bus_etl "} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azure_service_bus_etl: docs samples /workspace/azure_service_bus_etl/docs: consumer_responsibilities.md /workspace/azure_service_bus_etl/samples: messages_sample.json ","stderr":""} Optionally verify JSON is valid to catch syntax errors. I will run jq on messages_sample.json to ensure it parses. {"type":"code","code_command":"jq . /workspace/azure_service_bus_etl/samples/messages_sample.json >/dev/null && echo "messages_sample.json is valid JSON""} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"messages_sample.json is valid JSON ","stderr":""} Everything is created and validated. I will now provide the final answer summarizing file locations and key guidance. Deliverables created - /workspace/azure_service_bus_etl/samples/messages_sample.json • Two sample messages for an ETL completion topic (one succeeded, one failed) • Includes a service_bus block with realistic properties (message_id, correlation_id, subject, content_type, session_id, application_properties) and a rich body (etl_job, dataset, metrics, lineage, quality, links, idempotency) • Valid JSON (verified) - /workspace/azure_service_bus_etl/docs/consumer_responsibilities.md • Clear guidance for downstream consumers: - Validate schema/version and content type - Implement idempotent processing with a durable dedupe store and transactional upsert - Use PeekLock; Complete only after durable writes; Abandon for retriable, Dead-letter for permanent failures - Handle duplicates, out-of-order delivery, and ordering via Sessions - DLQ practices, observability/trace propagation, security, TTL, and SLAs - Recommended configurations and a testing checklist - Field mapping to Azure.Messaging.ServiceBus properties - Suggested idempotency key strategy How to use - Use messages_sample.json as a contract example for publishing and for consumer unit tests (duplicates, failure handling). - Implement the responsibilities in consumer_responsibilities.md to ensure reliable, idempotent processing on Azure Service Bus. If you want, I can also provide a JSON Schema for the message body or a minimal publisher/consumer code sample (C# or Python) aligned with this contract.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll start by creating a dedicated project folder to hold the telemetry sample and the capture layout doc. Then I will add the telemetry_samples.json file. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry && ls -la /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 8 drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 . drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 .. ","stderr":""} Now I'll write telemetry_samples.json with 10 records (fields: host, cpu, ts) using an array of JSON objects. Timestamps will be ISO 8601 (UTC, Z-suffix); cpu will be numeric between 0 and 100. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/telemetry_samples.json << 'JSON' [ {"host":"web-01","cpu":23.4,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:00Z"}, {"host":"web-02","cpu":57.1,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:05Z"}, {"host":"web-01","cpu":42.0,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:10Z"}, {"host":"db-01","cpu":68.9,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:15Z"}, {"host":"web-02","cpu":35.2,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:20Z"}, {"host":"web-03","cpu":12.3,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:25Z"}, {"host":"db-01","cpu":77.5,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:30Z"}, {"host":"web-01","cpu":51.8,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:35Z"}, {"host":"web-02","cpu":29.7,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:40Z"}, {"host":"web-03","cpu":5.9,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:45Z"} ] JSON && wc -c /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/telemetry_samples.json && head -n 5 /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/telemetry_samples.json"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"635 /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/telemetry_samples.json [ {"host":"web-01","cpu":23.4,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:00Z"}, {"host":"web-02","cpu":57.1,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:05Z"}, {"host":"web-01","cpu":42.0,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:10Z"}, {"host":"db-01","cpu":68.9,"ts":"2025-09-28T14:00:15Z"}, ","stderr":""} I will validate that telemetry_samples.json is well-formed JSON using jq for sanity. {"type":"code","code_command":"jq type /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/telemetry_samples.json"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":""array" ","stderr":""} Next, I'll write capture_layout.md describing the tiny telemetry schema and the folder structure for captured data (year/month/day/hour/minute), plus an example tree and example file naming. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/capture_layout.md << 'MD' Title: Event Hubs Telemetry Schema and Capture Layout 1) Tiny Telemetry Schema - host: string (hostname or instance ID) - cpu: number (0.0–100.0, percentage of CPU utilization) - ts: string (ISO 8601 UTC timestamp, e.g., 2025-09-28T14:00:00Z) Example record: { "host": "web-01", "cpu": 42.0, "ts": "2025-09-28T14:00:10Z" } 2) Capture Folder Structure (partitioned by time) - Container/prefix: capture/telemetry/ - Partitioning keys: year, month, day, hour, minute - Purpose: efficient time-based queries and lifecycle management Path template: - capture/telemetry/year=YYYY/month=MM/day=DD/hour=HH/minute=mm/ Example tree (for 2025-09-28, hours 14–15): - capture/ - telemetry/ - year=2025/ - month=09/ - day=28/ - hour=14/ - minute=00/ - partition=0/ - 2025-09-28-14-00-00-part0.avro - partition=1/ - 2025-09-28-14-00-00-part1.avro - minute=05/ - partition=0/ - 2025-09-28-14-05-00-part0.avro - hour=15/ - minute=00/ - partition=0/ - 2025-09-28-15-00-00-part0.avro Notes - File format can be Avro, Parquet, or JSON depending on ingestion; Avro/Parquet recommended for capture. - partition=N represents the Event Hubs partition ID (0..N-1). - Additional metadata folders (e.g., eventhub=, namespace=) can be prefixed if desired. - Readers can infer time partitions from the folder names without parsing file names. 3) Sample Naming Conventions - File: YYYY-MM-DD-HH-mm-SS-part. - Example: 2025-09-28-14-05-00-part0.avro - Alternate with GUIDs if multiple rollovers occur within the same minute: - 2025-09-28-14-05-00-part0-.avro 4) Retention & Compaction (optional guidance) - Use storage lifecycle rules to expire older folders by partition key (e.g., year/month/day). - Downsample or compact minute-level files to hourly/daily Parquet for analytics workloads. 5) Validation Hints - Ensure each record contains host (string), cpu (number 0–100), ts (ISO 8601 UTC string). - Downstream consumers should reject records with missing fields or malformed timestamps. MD && wc -c /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/capture_layout.md && head -n 20 /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/capture_layout.md"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"1892 /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/capture_layout.md Title: Event Hubs Telemetry Schema and Capture Layout 1) Tiny Telemetry Schema - host: string (hostname or instance ID) - cpu: number (0.0–100.0, percentage of CPU utilization) - ts: string (ISO 8601 UTC timestamp, e.g., 2025-09-28T14:00:00Z) Example record: { "host": "web-01", "cpu": 42.0, "ts": "2025-09-28T14:00:10Z" } 2) Capture Folder Structure (partitioned by time) - Container/prefix: capture/telemetry/ - Partitioning keys: year, month, day, hour, minute - Purpose: efficient time-based queries and lifecycle management ","stderr":""} I'll list the final files to confirm their presence. {"type":"code","code_command":"ls -la /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry && echo && echo "telemetry_samples.json size:" && wc -c /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/telemetry_samples.json && echo "capture_layout.md size:" && wc -c /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/capture_layout.md"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 24 drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 . drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 .. -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1892 Sep 28 00:00 capture_layout.md -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 635 Sep 28 00:00 telemetry_samples.json telemetry_samples.json size: 635 /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/telemetry_samples.json capture_layout.md size: 1892 /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/capture_layout.md ","stderr":""} Everything is created and validated. I'll provide a concise summary with file paths and what they contain. Done. Created files - /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/telemetry_samples.json - Array of 10 records with fields: host (string), cpu (number 0–100), ts (ISO 8601 UTC, Z). - /workspace/eventhubs_telemetry/capture_layout.md - Describes the tiny telemetry schema and a recommended capture folder structure partitioned by year/month/day/hour/minute with an example tree and naming conventions. Notes - Telemetry timestamps are in UTC (e.g., 2025-09-28T14:00:00Z). - Capture layout is designed for efficient time-based querying and retention management.

This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Start by grounding on official Synapse Analytics COPY INTO syntax and options (CSV quoting, delimiters, FIRSTROW, DATEFORMAT, ENCODING, error handling) to ensure the README and sample files align with docs. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Synapse Analytics COPY INTO CSV FIELDQUOTE FIRSTROW DATEFORMAT ROWTERMINATOR documentation", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"COPY INTO (Azure Synapse Dedicated SQL pool)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-stored-procedures#copy-into"},{"title":"Load data using the COPY statement","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql-data-warehouse/load-data-from-azure-blob-storage-using-copy"},{"title":"Best practices for loading data","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql-data-warehouse/design-elt-data-loading"},{"title":"CSV format considerations in Synapse","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-tables-external-tables#csv-format"},{"title":"Quickstart: Load data with COPY into dedicated SQL pool","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/quickstart-sql-pool-import-csv"}],"summary":"Azure Synapse Dedicated SQL pool supports COPY INTO for high-throughput loads from Azure Storage. Common options for CSV include FILE_TYPE='CSV', FIELDTERMINATOR=',', ROWTERMINATOR='0x0A', FIRSTROW to skip header, ENCODING='UTF8', DATEFORMAT='yyyy-MM-dd', FIELDQUOTE='"', MAXERRORS, and ERRORFILE for rejects. Quoted fields must follow RFC 4180 (double quotes inside values are doubled). Managed identity or SAS can be used via CREDENTIAL=(IDENTITY='Managed Identity'). Distribution and table storage (HEAP vs COLUMNSTORE) are defined at CREATE TABLE, not in COPY."}} Now scaffold the project structure and generate a copy-ready CSV with 10 rows that exercise commas, quotes, unicode, dates, decimals, and booleans. Create directories under /workspace/synapse_copy with data and docs. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e mkdir -p /workspace/synapse_copy/{data,docs} cat > /workspace/synapse_copy/data/customers_10rows.csv << 'CSV' customer_id,full_name,email,signup_date,is_active,balance,notes 1,"Alice Johnson","alice@example.com",2023-01-15,1,1200.50,"VIP customer, prefers email" 2,"Bob Smith","bob.smith@example.com",2022-11-02,0,0.00,"No special notes" 3,"Carlos Méndez","carlos.mendez@example.com",2024-03-05,1,85.75,"Lives in Bogotá" 4,"D'Angelo Wright","dangelo.wright@example.com",2021-07-22,1,450.00,"Nickname ""Dee""; enjoys promotions" 5,"Ella Zhang","ella.zhang@example.com",2020-12-30,1,9999999.99,"High balance" 6,"François Dupont","francois.dupont@example.fr",2024-02-29,0,15.20,"Uses French locale" 7,"Göran Åkesson","goran.akesson@example.se",2023-06-10,1,320.00,"Requires invoice, address has comma, Stockholm, Sweden" 8,"Hannah Lee","h.lee@example.co.uk",2022-09-01,1,-12.34,"Negative balance edge-case" 9,"Ivan Petrov","ivan.petrov@example.ru",2025-01-05,0,100.00,"Recently moved; update address" 10,"Julia O'Connor","j.oconnor@example.com",2024-12-12,1,0.01,"Quote test: He said ""Hello""." CSV ls -R /workspace/synapse_copy"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/synapse_copy: data docs /workspace/synapse_copy/data: customers_10rows.csv /workspace/synapse_copy/docs: ","stderr":""} Create a Synapse-compatible table schema file (CREATE TABLE) matching the CSV columns and types, with sensible distribution and storage options. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/synapse_copy/docs/table_schema.sql << 'SQL' -- Target: Azure Synapse Dedicated SQL pool -- Create table matching customers_10rows.csv CREATE TABLE dbo.customers ( customer_id INT NOT NULL, full_name NVARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, email NVARCHAR(255) NULL, signup_date DATE NOT NULL, is_active BIT NOT NULL, balance DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL, notes NVARCHAR(4000) NULL ) WITH ( DISTRIBUTION = ROUND_ROBIN, HEAP ); -- Optional: add an index if you plan to query frequently by customer_id -- CREATE CLUSTERED INDEX CIX_customers_customer_id ON dbo.customers(customer_id); SQL sed -n '1,120p' /workspace/synapse_copy/docs/table_schema.sql"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"-- Target: Azure Synapse Dedicated SQL pool -- Create table matching customers_10rows.csv CREATE TABLE dbo.customers ( customer_id INT NOT NULL, full_name NVARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, email NVARCHAR(255) NULL, signup_date DATE NOT NULL, is_active BIT NOT NULL, balance DECIMAL(18,2) NOT NULL, notes NVARCHAR(4000) NULL ) WITH ( DISTRIBUTION = ROUND_ROBIN, HEAP ); -- Optional: add an index if you plan to query frequently by customer_id -- CREATE CLUSTERED INDEX CIX_customers_customer_id ON dbo.customers(customer_id); ","stderr":""} Author synapse_readme.md with practical instructions: quoting rules, encoding, delimiters, FIRSTROW, DATEFORMAT, sample COPY INTO command, identity/permissions, and error handling. Reference the web findings. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/synapse_copy/docs/synapse_readme.md << 'MD' Synapse COPY-ready sample (CSV + schema) This package contains: - data/customers_10rows.csv — 10-row CSV following RFC 4180 conventions - docs/table_schema.sql — T-SQL CREATE TABLE for Azure Synapse Dedicated SQL pool - docs/synapse_readme.md — this guide 1) Table schema Run in your Synapse Dedicated SQL pool (e.g., via Synapse Studio > Develop > SQL script): - Create table - See docs/table_schema.sql - Uses DISTRIBUTION = ROUND_ROBIN and HEAP (good for small staging tables). For large fact tables, consider CLUSTERED COLUMNSTORE and HASH distribution on a high-cardinality key. 2) CSV format and quoting rules - Delimiter: comma (,) - Row terminator: LF (0x0A) - Encoding: UTF-8 - Header row: present (FIRSTROW = 2 is used in COPY) - Quoting: double quotes (") used only when needed (values containing delimiter, quote, or leading/trailing spaces) - Escaping quotes inside a quoted value: double the double quote characters - Example in notes: Quote test: He said ""Hello"". - Date format: ISO 8601 yyyy-MM-dd (e.g., 2024-12-12) - Decimal format: dot for decimal separator (e.g., 1200.50) - Boolean: mapped to BIT (0 or 1) - NULLs: represent as empty field between delimiters with no quotes (,,). If you need to load empty strings instead, use "". These conventions align with Azure Synapse COPY INTO options for CSV, including FIELDQUOTE, FIELDTERMINATOR, ROWTERMINATOR, DATEFORMAT, FIRSTROW, and ENCODING. 3) Sample COPY INTO command Copy from Azure Storage (ADLS Gen2 or Blob). Grant your dedicated SQL pool access (managed identity recommended) and upload the CSV to a container path, e.g. customers/customers_10rows.csv. Example using workspace managed identity: COPY INTO dbo.customers FROM 'https://.blob.core.windows.net//customers/customers_10rows.csv' WITH ( FILE_TYPE = 'CSV', CREDENTIAL = (IDENTITY = 'Managed Identity'), FIELDTERMINATOR = ',', ROWTERMINATOR = '0x0A', FIRSTROW = 2, ENCODING = 'UTF8', FIELDQUOTE = '"', DATEFORMAT = 'yyyy-MM-dd', MAXERRORS = 0, ERRORFILE = 'https://.blob.core.windows.net//rejections/' ); Notes: - FIRSTROW = 2 skips the header. - FIELDQUOTE = '"' ensures proper handling of quoted values and doubled quotes inside. - ERRORFILE collects reject rows for troubleshooting; ensure the path exists and is writable. - If loading multiple files, point FROM to a folder (e.g., customers/) and optionally use a wildcard. - For SAS-based auth, use: CREDENTIAL = (IDENTITY='Shared Access Signature', SECRET=''). 4) Permissions and setup - Storage: The Synapse managed identity (or your chosen identity) needs Storage Blob Data Reader on the container with the CSVs, and write permissions to ERRORFILE path if specified. - In-database credential: Not required for 'Managed Identity' in Synapse Dedicated SQL pools. For SAS, use a database scoped credential. 5) Data type guidance and mapping - customer_id → INT - full_name → NVARCHAR(100) (UTF-8 CSV to NVARCHAR supports international characters) - email → NVARCHAR(255) - signup_date → DATE (yyyy-MM-dd) - is_active → BIT (0/1) - balance → DECIMAL(18,2) - notes → NVARCHAR(4000) 6) Quality checks before loading - Ensure file uses LF line endings (Unix). On Windows tools, convert if needed. - Confirm there are no stray unescaped quotes; within a quoted field, use doubled quotes. - Avoid embedded newlines in field values. If present, they must be within quoted fields; COPY INTO can handle them when properly quoted, but keeping rows to one line is simpler. 7) Useful references - COPY INTO overview and options (FILE_TYPE, FIELDTERMINATOR, ROWTERMINATOR, FIRSTROW, ENCODING, FIELDQUOTE, DATEFORMAT, MAXERRORS, ERRORFILE) - Synapse loading best practices (staging, distribution, columnstore) MD wc -l /workspace/synapse_copy/data/customers_10rows.csv head -n 5 /workspace/synapse_copy/data/customers_10rows.csv"}
