Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Search for or create /workspace/config.json, then rotate mockSecret version from v1 to v2, back up the file, and write rotation_log.md with old/new versions and UTC cutover time. Implement in one shell block using jq, then verify via a second shell block that shows the updated JSON and the log contents. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -euo pipefail shopt -s nullglob # 1) Locate or create config.json CONFIG_PATH=$(find /workspace -maxdepth 3 -type f -name config.json | head -n 1 || true) if [[ -z "${CONFIG_PATH:-}" ]]; then CONFIG_PATH="/workspace/config.json" mkdir -p "$(dirname "$CONFIG_PATH")" cat > "$CONFIG_PATH" << 'JSON' { "keyVault": { "secrets": { "mockSecret": { "name": "mock-secret", "version": "v1" } } } } JSON echo "Initialized new config at $CONFIG_PATH with mockSecret version v1" fi # 2) Validate JSON jq . "$CONFIG_PATH" >/dev/null # 3) Determine old version (default to v1 if absent) OLD_VERSION=$(jq -r '.keyVault.secrets.mockSecret.version // empty' "$CONFIG_PATH") if [[ -z "$OLD_VERSION" || "$OLD_VERSION" == "null" ]]; then OLD_VERSION="v1" fi # 4) Prepare backup and timestamp CUTOVER_TIME=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) BACKUP_PATH="${CONFIG_PATH}.bak-${CUTOVER_TIME}" cp "$CONFIG_PATH" "$BACKUP_PATH" # 5) Rotate version to v2 using jq (ensure nested structure exists) TMP_JSON="$(mktemp)" jq '.keyVault = (.keyVault // {}) | .keyVault.secrets = (.keyVault.secrets // {}) | .keyVault.secrets.mockSecret = (.keyVault.secrets.mockSecret // {}) | .keyVault.secrets.mockSecret.name = (.keyVault.secrets.mockSecret.name // "mock-secret") | .keyVault.secrets.mockSecret.version = "v2"' "$CONFIG_PATH" > "$TMP_JSON" mv "$TMP_JSON" "$CONFIG_PATH" # 6) Write/append rotation log LOG_PATH="/workspace/rotation_log.md" if [[ ! -f "$LOG_PATH" ]]; then cat > "$LOG_PATH" << 'HDR' # Secret Rotation Log This log records rotations of mock secrets in local config.json for development/testing. HDR fi cat >> "$LOG_PATH" << LOG ## Rotation: mockSecret - Secret path: keyVault.secrets.mockSecret.version - Old version: $OLD_VERSION - New version: v2 - Cutover time (UTC): $CUTOVER_TIME - Config path: $CONFIG_PATH - Backup created: $BACKUP_PATH LOG # 7) Print summary echo "Rotation complete" echo "Config: $CONFIG_PATH" echo "Backup: $BACKUP_PATH" echo "Log: $LOG_PATH" "}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, content.text: First, I need to ground the tier guidance in official Azure docs to ensure accurate recommendations (hot, cool, cold, archive tiers; access patterns; minimum storage durations; retrieval and transaction costs; rehydration). I will perform a web search for Azure Blob Storage access tiers and cold tier specifics. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Blob Storage access tiers hot cool cold archive minimum storage duration early deletion retrieval transaction costs cold access tier", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Hot, Cool, Cold, and Archive access tiers for Azure Blob data","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/access-tiers-overview"},{"title":"Use the archive tier to store blob data and rehydrate later","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/archive-rehydrate-overview"},{"title":"Azure Blob Storage pricing (Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive)","url":"https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/details/storage/blobs/"},{"title":"Blob storage lifecycle management to tier data","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/lifecycle-management-overview"},{"title":"Azure Storage redundancy and tiers basics","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/common/storage-redundancy"}],"summary":"Azure Blob Storage supports Hot (frequent access), Cool (infrequent access), Cold (very infrequent but still online), and Archive (offline) tiers. Storage cost decreases from Hot to Archive, while access (read) and transaction costs generally increase. Minimum storage duration charges apply: Cool ~30 days, Cold ~90 days, Archive ~180 days. Archive requires rehydration with hours of latency and additional rehydration charges. Cool and Cold have data retrieval and early deletion charges. Tiers can be applied per-blob; lifecycle policies can automate movement across tiers."}} Now that I have the key constraints (min durations and cost trade-offs), I will create the output directory and then author tier_suggestion.csv with five file types and recommended tiers, plus tier_notes.md explaining trade-offs and references. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azure_storage_tiers && ls -la /workspace/azure_storage_tiers"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 8 drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 . drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 .. ","stderr":""} I will write tier_suggestion.csv with columns: file_type,suggested_tier,access_pattern,reason. Then create tier_notes.md with detailed cost trade-offs, min storage duration, retrieval/rehydration, lifecycle tips, and links to docs. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azure_storage_tiers/tier_suggestion.csv << 'CSV' file_type,suggested_tier,access_pattern,reason Raw application logs (30–90 days),Cool,Infrequent read; occasional audits/queries,"Lower storage than Hot; acceptable retrieval/transaction costs; 30-day minimum aligns with log retention windows" Website/CDN static assets (images, CSS, JS),Hot,Frequent read; low latency required,"Hot optimizes for frequent access and low per-operation costs; avoids Cool/Cold retrieval charges" Database backups kept ~90 days,Cold,Rare read; must remain online for quick restore,"Cold lowers storage cost vs Cool; online access (no rehydration); 90-day minimum matches retention" Regulatory/compliance archives (7+ years),Archive,Very rare access; long-term retention,"Lowest storage cost; acceptable hours-long rehydration; 180-day minimum acceptable" ML training datasets used monthly,Cool,Periodic reads; not latency-critical,"Cheaper than Hot; periodic access tolerates higher per-read cost; 30-day minimum acceptable" CSV cat > /workspace/azure_storage_tiers/tier_notes.md << 'MD' Azure Blob Storage Tier Notes (Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive) Summary - Hot: For frequent access. Highest storage cost; lowest access/transaction costs. No minimum storage duration charge. - Cool: For infrequent access with occasional reads. Lower storage cost than Hot; higher read/transaction costs; early deletion/minimum storage duration charge ~30 days. - Cold: For very infrequent access but needs to remain online. Lower storage cost than Cool; higher read and write transaction costs than Cool; early deletion/minimum storage duration charge ~90 days. - Archive: For rarely accessed, long-term retention. Lowest storage cost; data is offline and must be rehydrated before read; minimum storage duration charge ~180 days; retrieval has hours of latency and rehydration charges. Cost trade-offs and operational implications 1) Storage cost vs. access cost - Storage $/GB-month decreases from Hot → Cool → Cold → Archive. - Read/transaction costs and data retrieval charges increase in the same direction (i.e., Hot has cheapest per-operation, Archive has the most expensive retrieval path including rehydration). 2) Minimum storage duration (early deletion) charges - Cool: 30 days. Deleting or moving a blob out of Cool before 30 days incurs a prorated early deletion charge. - Cold: 90 days. Same concept; intended for data that will live at least ~90 days. - Archive: 180 days. Very long minimum; best for true archival. - Hot: No minimum duration charge. 3) Retrieval behavior and latency - Hot/Cool/Cold are online: data is immediately readable (subject to higher per-read and transaction costs in Cool/Cold). - Archive is offline: must rehydrate to an online tier (Hot/Cool/Cold) before reading. Rehydration can take hours (Standard priority) or faster at higher cost (High priority), and incurs rehydration charges. 4) Typical mappings - Raw logs (30–90 days): Cool. Aligns well with 30-day minimum and infrequent access. - DB/DR backups (~90 days): Cold. Still online for quick restore; 90-day minimum fits backup retention. - Regulatory archives (years): Archive. Cost optimized for long-term, rare access; tolerate rehydration delay. - Frequently accessed website assets: Hot. Prioritizes low latency and lower per-operation cost. - ML datasets used monthly: Cool. Infrequent but online access OK; lifecycle policies can promote to Hot during training if needed. 5) Lifecycle management - Use blob lifecycle policies to automatically transition data based on age or last-modified time. Example: Hot → Cool at 30 days; Cool → Cold at 90 days; Cold → Archive at 180/365+ days. - Policies help avoid manual moves and reduce early deletion fees by aligning transitions to minimum duration windows. 6) Other considerations - Estimate total cost using both storage and expected access (read/write ops, data retrieval, rehydration). Small, frequent reads can make Cool/Cold more expensive than Hot. - Archive retrieval SLAs and operational workflows: plan for rehydration lead time before access (e.g., legal holds, audits). - Tiers are per-blob. You can mix tiers within the same storage account/container. References (Microsoft Learn and Pricing) - Access tiers overview (Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive): https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/access-tiers-overview - Archive tier and rehydration: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/archive-rehydrate-overview - Pricing (Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive): https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/details/storage/blobs/ - Lifecycle management: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/lifecycle-management-overview MD"}

First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: First, I should ground the pros/cons in official Azure Cosmos DB documentation to ensure accuracy (definitions, guarantees, trade-offs). I’ll do a web search for “Azure Cosmos DB consistency levels” and related trade-offs, then summarize. After that, I’ll scaffold files and write the requested notes and metadata. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Cosmos DB consistency levels strong session bounded staleness consistent prefix eventual trade-offs guarantees latency throughput availability RU cost official docs", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Consistency levels in Azure Cosmos DB","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/consistency-levels"},{"title":"Choose the right consistency level in Azure Cosmos DB","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/how-to-choose-consistency-level"},{"title":"Azure Cosmos DB consistency, latency, and throughput tradeoffs","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/consistency-tradeoffs"},{"title":"Session consistency details (read-your-writes, monotonic reads/writes)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/consistency-levels#session"},{"title":"Strong consistency guarantees and limitations","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/consistency-levels#strong"},{"title":"Bounded staleness semantics and parameters (K, T)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/consistency-levels#bounded-staleness"}],"summary":"Azure Cosmos DB offers five consistency levels: Strong, Bounded Staleness, Session, Consistent Prefix, and Eventual. Strong provides linearizability (read-after-write globally) but has the highest read latency and RU cost, and restrictions in multi-region configurations. Session provides per-client session guarantees (read-your-writes, monotonic reads/writes, write-follows-reads) with low latency and cost, ideal default for most apps, but cross-session reads may see lag and anomalies. Bounded Staleness caps staleness by operations K or time T. Consistent Prefix prevents out-of-order reads but allows lag. Eventual offers lowest latency/cost but no ordering guarantee. Trade-offs include latency, throughput (RU/s), availability under regional failover, and multi-region replication behavior."}} Now that I have references, I’ll create a project folder and placeholders for the two requested files: consistency_notes.md and metadata.json, plus a docs/ folder with a couple of sample docs to “tag” in the metadata. Then I’ll fill in the files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/cosmos_consistency/docs && touch /workspace/cosmos_consistency/consistency_notes.md /workspace/cosmos_consistency/metadata.json && echo "# Sample Doc 1\nExample content." > /workspace/cosmos_consistency/docs/sample1.md && echo "# Sample Doc 2\nAnother example." > /workspace/cosmos_consistency/docs/sample2.md && ls -R /workspace/cosmos_consistency"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/cosmos_consistency: consistency_notes.md docs metadata.json /workspace/cosmos_consistency/docs: sample1.md sample2.md ","stderr":""} I’ll write detailed pros/cons comparing Session vs Strong in consistency_notes.md, including definitions, guarantees, latency/throughput, RU cost, availability and multi-region implications, and typical use cases. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/cosmos_consistency/consistency_notes.md << 'MD' Cosmos DB Consistency Levels: Session vs Strong — Notes Context Azure Cosmos DB supports five consistency levels (Strong, Bounded Staleness, Session, Consistent Prefix, Eventual). This note focuses on a practical comparison between Session and Strong, the two levels most often contrasted for globally distributed applications requiring either client-centric guarantees (Session) or strict linearizability (Strong). Session Consistency Definition/Guarantees - Per-session guarantees with a session token issued to a client: - Read-your-writes: Subsequent reads by the same session observe their own writes. - Monotonic reads: Reads never move backward in time within the session. - Monotonic writes: Writes from the same session are serialized in order. - Write-follows-reads: A write following a read in the same session is guaranteed to be based on at least that read’s state. - Across different sessions/clients, some lag is possible; other clients may not immediately see your writes. Pros - Lower latency than Strong (both reads and writes), especially in multi-region deployments. - Lower RU consumption than Strong for equivalent workloads (reads typically cheaper because no global synchronous coordination). - Excellent default choice for most user-centric apps (web/mobile) where each user/session needs read-your-writes but strict global ordering isn’t required. - Scales well for global distribution and multi-region reads; preserves client-centric semantics with session tokens. - High availability under regional failover; generally no special limitations for multi-region topologies. Cons - Cross-session anomalies: Other clients (without your session token) may read slightly stale data. - Not suitable when business rules require strictly up-to-date reads globally, e.g., strongly consistent counters, double-spend prevention, or strict invariants that must be globally enforced on reads. - Additional care required in stateless/multi-service patterns to propagate session tokens to preserve guarantees between services. Typical Use Cases - User profile updates followed by immediate reads in the same session (web/mobile apps). - Shopping carts, personalized dashboards, collaborative apps where slight cross-user staleness is acceptable. - Microservices with per-request or per-user session token propagation. Operational Considerations - Ensure your client SDK captures and replays session tokens on subsequent reads to preserve guarantees. - When multiple services participate in a single logical session, propagate the session token through calls. Strong Consistency Definition/Guarantees - Linearizability (global strong consistency): Reads always return the most recent committed write globally. No staleness or reordering. - Equivalent to a single, strongly consistent copy from the perspective of clients. Pros - Simplest correctness model for consumers: no anomalies due to staleness. Ideal when you must enforce strict invariants at read time. - Predictable behavior across sessions/clients/regions: everyone sees the same latest committed state. Cons - Higher read latency due to synchronous replication/coordination to ensure global ordering. - Higher RU cost for reads (and potentially reduced effective throughput) relative to weaker levels. - Multi-region limitations for strong consistency may apply (see Azure docs for current constraints) and can reduce achievable geographic distances or region counts. - Under cross-region scenarios, read latency can be bounded by the slowest coordination path. Typical Use Cases - Financial transactions where any read must reflect the latest committed write to prevent double-spend or inventory oversell. - Global counters and state machines requiring strict ordering and no staleness across regions. - Workloads that cannot tolerate anomalies introduced by stale reads. Operational Considerations - Carefully assess user-perceived latency in globally distributed scenarios; consider colocation of clients and data. - Ensure the account’s regional configuration is compatible with strong consistency constraints and SLA. - Budget additional RUs for strongly consistent reads, and consider caching strategies for hot keys if appropriate. Summary Trade-offs - Latency & RU Cost: Session < Strong (Session is generally faster and cheaper). - Freshness: Strong > Session (Strong has zero staleness, Session is zero staleness per-session only). - Availability/Topology Flexibility: Session is more flexible in multi-region setups; Strong may impose constraints and higher coordination overhead. - Developer Complexity: Strong simplifies correctness reasoning globally; Session requires session token propagation but is often the best balance of performance and semantics for end-user apps. References (see official docs) - Consistency levels overview: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/consistency-levels - Choosing a consistency level: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/how-to-choose-consistency-level - Trade-offs: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/consistency-tradeoffs MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create metadata.json to “tag” the sample docs with a desired consistency level. I’ll choose Session as the desired level and include references to the two sample docs with a tag field. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/cosmos_consistency/metadata.json << 'JSON' { "desiredConsistencyLevel": "Session", "notes": "Defaulting to Session for user-centric workloads to balance latency, cost, and correctness via per-session guarantees.", "documents": [ { "id": "doc-001", "path": "docs/sample1.md", "title": "Sample Doc 1", "tags": ["consistency:Session"] }, { "id": "doc-002", "path": "docs/sample2.md", "title": "Sample Doc 2", "tags": ["consistency:Session"] } ] } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I should verify the files exist and show their contents for confirmation. {"type":"code","code_command":"ls -R /workspace/cosmos_consistency && echo '--- consistency_notes.md ---' && sed -n '1,200p' /workspace/cosmos_consistency/consistency_notes.md && echo '--- metadata.json ---' && sed -n '1,200p' /workspace/cosmos_consistency/metadata.json"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/cosmos_consistency: consistency_notes.md docs metadata.json /workspace/cosmos_consistency/docs: sample1.md sample2.md --- consistency_notes.md --- Cosmos DB Consistency Levels: Session vs Strong — Notes Context Azure Cosmos DB supports five consistency levels (Strong, Bounded Staleness, Session, Consistent Prefix, Eventual). This note focuses on a practical comparison between Session and Strong, the two levels most often contrasted for globally distributed applications requiring either client-centric guarantees (Session) or strict linearizability (Strong). Session Consistency Definition/Guarantees - Per-session guarantees with a session token issued to a client: - Read-your-writes: Subsequent reads by the same session observe their own writes. - Monotonic reads: Reads never move backward in time within the session. - Monotonic writes: Writes from the same session are serialized in order. - Write-follows-reads: A write following a read in the same session is guaranteed to be based on at least that read’s state. - Across different sessions/clients, some lag is possible; other clients may not immediately see your writes. Pros - Lower latency than Strong (both reads and writes), especially in multi-region deployments. - Lower RU consumption than Strong for equivalent workloads (reads typically cheaper because no global synchronous coordination). - Excellent default choice for most user-centric apps (web/mobile) where each user/session needs read-your-writes but strict global ordering isn’t required. - Scales well for global distribution and multi-region reads; preserves client-centric semantics with session tokens. - High availability under regional failover; generally no special limitations for multi-region topologies. Cons - Cross-session anomalies: Other clients (without your session token) may read slightly stale data. - Not suitable when business rules require strictly up-to-date reads globally, e.g., strongly consistent counters, double-spend prevention, or strict invariants that must be globally enforced on reads. - Additional care required in stateless/multi-service patterns to propagate session tokens to preserve guarantees between services. Typical Use Cases - User profile updates followed by immediate reads in the same session (web/mobile apps). - Shopping carts, personalized dashboards, collaborative apps where slight cross-user staleness is acceptable. - Microservices with per-request or per-user session token propagation. Operational Considerations - Ensure your client SDK captures and replays session tokens on subsequent reads to preserve guarantees. - When multiple services participate in a single logical session, propagate the session token through calls. Strong Consistency Definition/Guarantees - Linearizability (global strong consistency): Reads always return the most recent committed write globally. No staleness or reordering. - Equivalent to a single, strongly consistent copy from the perspective of clients. Pros - Simplest correctness model for consumers: no anomalies due to staleness. Ideal when you must enforce strict invariants at read time. - Predictable behavior across sessions/clients/regions: everyone sees the same latest committed state. Cons - Higher read latency due to synchronous replication/coordination to ensure global ordering. - Higher RU cost for reads (and potentially reduced effective throughput) relative to weaker levels. - Multi-region limitations for strong consistency may apply (see Azure docs for current constraints) and can reduce achievable geographic distances or region counts. - Under cross-region scenarios, read latency can be bounded by the slowest coordination path. Typical Use Cases - Financial transactions where any read must reflect the latest committed write to prevent double-spend or inventory oversell. - Global counters and state machines requiring strict ordering and no staleness across regions. - Workloads that cannot tolerate anomalies introduced by stale reads. Operational Considerations - Carefully assess user-perceived latency in globally distributed scenarios; consider colocation of clients and data. - Ensure the account’s regional configuration is compatible with strong consistency constraints and SLA. - Budget additional RUs for strongly consistent reads, and consider caching strategies for hot keys if appropriate. Summary Trade-offs - Latency & RU Cost: Session < Strong (Session is generally faster and cheaper). - Freshness: Strong > Session (Strong has zero staleness, Session is zero staleness per-session only). - Availability/Topology Flexibility: Session is more flexible in multi-region setups; Strong may impose constraints and higher coordination overhead. - Developer Complexity: Strong simplifies correctness reasoning globally; Session requires session token propagation but is often the best balance of performance and semantics for end-user apps. References (see official docs) - Consistency levels overview: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/consistency-levels - Choosing a consistency level: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/how-to-choose-consistency-level - Trade-offs: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/consistency-tradeoffs --- metadata.json --- { "desiredConsistencyLevel": "Session", "notes": "Defaulting to Session for user-centric workloads to balance latency, cost, and correctness via per-session guarantees.", "documents": [ { "id": "doc-001", "path": "docs/sample1.md", "title": "Sample Doc 1", "tags": ["consistency:Session"] }, { "id": "doc-002", "path": "docs/sample2.md", "title": "Sample Doc 2", "tags": ["consistency:Session"] } ] } ","stderr":""} All requested artifacts have been created and verified. I’ll now present the final summary and paths. Completed What I did - Researched Cosmos DB consistency levels via official Microsoft documentation to ensure accurate trade-offs. - Created two deliverables in /workspace/cosmos_consistency: 1) consistency_notes.md — concise comparison of Session vs Strong, including definitions, guarantees, pros/cons, use cases, and operational considerations. 2) metadata.json — tagged sample docs with a desired consistency level (Session). Artifacts - /workspace/cosmos_consistency/consistency_notes.md Contents: - Session consistency • Guarantees: read-your-writes, monotonic reads/writes, write-follows-reads (per session) • Pros: lower latency and RU cost, great default for user-centric apps, scales well globally • Cons: cross-session staleness possible; need session token propagation • Use cases: profile updates, carts, dashboards - Strong consistency • Guarantees: linearizability (global read-after-write, zero staleness) • Pros: simplest correctness, strict invariants • Cons: higher latency/RU, multi-region constraints • Use cases: financial transactions, global counters - Summary trade-offs and links to official docs - /workspace/cosmos_consistency/metadata.json { "desiredConsistencyLevel": "Session", "notes": "Defaulting to Session for user-centric workloads...", "documents": [ {"id": "doc-001", "path": "docs/sample1.md", "title": "Sample Doc 1", "tags": ["consistency:Session"]}, {"id": "doc-002", "path": "docs/sample2.md", "title": "Sample Doc 2", "tags": ["consistency:Session"]} ] } Where to find files - Notes: /workspace/cosmos_consistency/consistency_notes.md - Metadata: /workspace/cosmos_consistency/metadata.json - Sample docs: /workspace/cosmos_consistency/docs/sample1.md, sample2.md Next steps (optional) - If you prefer Strong, change desiredConsistencyLevel to "Strong" and update tags accordingly. - For broader context, you can add brief summaries for Bounded Staleness, Consistent Prefix, and Eventual to the notes. - Ensure client apps propagate session tokens when using Session consistency to preserve guarantees.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.
