body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: First, I need to confirm the latest, correct syntax and options for Azure Container Apps secrets: how to define secrets, reference them as environment variables, mount them as files via volumes, and reference Key Vault secrets. I'll use to pull the official Microsoft Learn docs so the YAML/CLI I include in secrets_layout.md is accurate. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Container Apps secrets environment variable secretRef volumes mount secrets Key Vault reference YAML az containerapp secret set", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Use secrets in Azure Container Apps","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/container-apps/secrets"},{"title":"Use Key Vault references with Azure Container Apps","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/container-apps/key-vault-secrets"},{"title":"Azure Container Apps YAML schema reference","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/container-apps/container-apps-yaml"},{"title":"Manage secrets with Azure CLI for Container Apps","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/container-apps/secrets#azure-cli"},{"title":"Mount secrets as volumes in Azure Container Apps","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/container-apps/secrets#mount-secrets-as-volumes"},{"title":"Container Apps environment variables and secrets (secretRef)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/container-apps/secrets#use-secrets-as-environment-variables"}],"summary":"Azure Container Apps supports app-level secrets defined in configuration.secrets. You can inject secrets into containers via environment variables by using env.name and env.secretRef. You can also mount secrets as files using template.volumes with entries referencing secrets via secretRef and a path, then a corresponding containers.volumeMounts mountPath. Key Vault secrets can be referenced in configuration.secrets via keyVaultUrl; the app's managed identity must have get permission. Azure CLI supports az containerapp secret set/list/show to manage secrets. Updating a secret value typically requires restarting the container to pick up the new value; changing the template can create a new revision."} Good. Now I'll scaffold a local workspace for this task and create placeholders for secrets_layout.md and config_security_map.csv. I'll make a dedicated project directory under /workspace/aca_secrets with docs and examples to keep things organized. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/aca_secrets/{docs,examples,templates} && touch /workspace/aca_secrets/docs/secrets_layout.md /workspace/aca_secrets/config_security_map.csv && ls -R /workspace/aca_secrets"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/aca_secrets: config_security_map.csv docs examples templates /workspace/aca_secrets/docs: secrets_layout.md /workspace/aca_secrets/examples: /workspace/aca_secrets/templates: ","stderr":""} Before writing, I want to double-check the exact YAML keys for mounting secrets as volumes and the Key Vault reference property name. I'll consult the YAML schema and secrets docs for examples: configuration.secrets with value vs keyVaultUrl, template.volumes with secrets.secretRef/path, and containers.env secretRef usage. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Container Apps YAML volumes secrets.secretRef path example keyVaultUrl configuration.secrets env secretRef example", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Container Apps YAML reference: configuration.secrets and template.volumes","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/container-apps/container-apps-yaml#secrets"},{"title":"Azure Container Apps: Mount secrets as files (YAML example)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/container-apps/secrets#mount-secrets-as-volumes"},{"title":"Azure Container Apps: Use secrets as environment variables (YAML)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/container-apps/secrets#use-secrets-as-environment-variables"},{"title":"Azure Container Apps Key Vault references: keyVaultUrl","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/container-apps/key-vault-secrets#use-key-vault-references-in-configurationsecrets"}],"summary":"The YAML format uses configuration.secrets entries with either value: "..." or keyVaultUrl: "https://.vault.azure.net/secrets//". To expose via environment variable use template.containers[*].env with name: VAR and secretRef: secret-name. To mount secrets as files, define template.volumes with - name: volname and secrets: - secretRef: secret-name path: filename; then mount with template.containers[*].volumeMounts with volumeName and mountPath."}} Great, I have the accurate keys: configuration.secrets with value or keyVaultUrl; containers.env.secretRef; volumes.secrets[].secretRef and path; containers.volumeMounts mountPath. Now I’ll write secrets_layout.md with clear sections: overview, security model, environment variable injection, volume mount, Key Vault references, CLI/YAML examples, rotation notes, and verification tips. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/aca_secrets/docs/secrets_layout.md << 'MD' Title: Azure Container Apps — Secrets Layout and Mount Patterns Overview This document shows how application secrets are defined and mounted in Azure Container Apps (ACA) as either environment variables or files via secret volumes, including guidance for Azure Key Vault references, rotation, and verification. Security Model Summary - Secrets are defined at the Container App resource level under configuration.secrets. - Secrets can be sourced from: - Inline secret values (not recommended beyond development). - Azure Key Vault references via keyVaultUrl (recommended for production). - Secrets are consumed by containers as: - Environment variables (env[].secretRef), or - Files, by mounting a secret volume (template.volumes + volumeMounts). - Access to Key Vault requires a managed identity (system- or user-assigned) with get permission to the secret. - Secret value changes generally require a container restart to be effective; template changes produce new revisions. A) Use a secret as an environment variable 1) Define the secret Option 1 — Inline (dev/test): configuration: secrets: - name: db-password value: Option 2 — Key Vault reference (prod): identity: type: SystemAssigned configuration: secrets: - name: db-password keyVaultUrl: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/db-password/ 2) Reference the secret as an environment variable in the container template: containers: - name: api image: myregistry.azurecr.io/api:1.0 env: - name: DB_PASSWORD secretRef: db-password Notes - DB_PASSWORD will be available to the process as a normal environment variable. Avoid printing or logging it. - Changing db-password requires a restart to refresh environment variables. B) Mount secrets as files using a volume Use this for file-based secrets such as certificates, private keys, SSH keys, or any secret your application expects from the filesystem. 1) Define the secret in configuration.secrets (inline or Key Vault reference as above) configuration: secrets: - name: jwt-private-key keyVaultUrl: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/jwt-private-key/ 2) Create a secret-backed volume and mount it in the container template: volumes: - name: jwt-secrets secrets: - secretRef: jwt-private-key path: signing.key # file name created inside the mount containers: - name: api image: myregistry.azurecr.io/api:1.0 volumeMounts: - volumeName: jwt-secrets mountPath: /var/run/secrets/jwt Resulting filesystem - The secret value is written to /var/run/secrets/jwt/signing.key at container runtime. - File permissions are read-only to the container user context. C) Azure CLI management examples (idempotent) # Set or update a secret value (inline) az containerapp secret set \ --name myapp \ --resource-group myrg \ --secrets db-password=\"\" # List secrets (names only; values are not returned) az containerapp secret list \ --name myapp \ --resource-group myrg # Reference a Key Vault secret (by URL) az containerapp update \ --name myapp \ --resource-group myrg \ --set configuration.secrets=[{name=db-password,keyVaultUrl=https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/db-password/}] # Restart the app to apply secret updates (if not creating a new revision) az containerapp restart --name myapp --resource-group myrg D) Full YAML example combining both patterns name: myapp type: Microsoft.App/containerApps location: identity: type: SystemAssigned properties: environmentId: /subscriptions//resourceGroups//providers/Microsoft.App/managedEnvironments/ configuration: secrets: - name: db-password keyVaultUrl: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/db-password/ - name: jwt-private-key keyVaultUrl: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/jwt-private-key/ template: containers: - name: api image: myregistry.azurecr.io/api:1.0 env: - name: DB_PASSWORD secretRef: db-password volumeMounts: - volumeName: jwt-secrets mountPath: /var/run/secrets/jwt volumes: - name: jwt-secrets secrets: - secretRef: jwt-private-key path: signing.key E) When to use which approach - Environment variable: - Best for short scalar values (passwords, tokens, connection strings). - Simple to consume in 12-factor applications. - Mounted file: - Best for certificates, keys, or any secret the app expects from a file path. - Avoids exposing large blobs as environment variables. F) Rotation and lifecycle - Key Vault rotation: rotate the backing secret version in Key Vault, then update keyVaultUrl to the new version or use a versionless URL if supported per your policy. - Inline secrets: use az containerapp secret set to update; then restart the app. - New revision vs restart: changing template fields creates a new revision; pure secret value updates may require restart to refresh values. G) Verification tips - Exec into a sidecar debug shell and verify either: - printenv | grep DB_PASSWORD (for env-based; do not print value in production), or - ls -l /var/run/secrets/jwt && test -s /var/run/secrets/jwt/signing.key (for file-based). - Use az containerapp show --name myapp --resource-group myrg --query properties.template.containers[0].env to validate the secretRef wiring (values are not shown). H) Security best practices - Prefer Key Vault references; grant least-privilege (get) to the app’s managed identity. - Never bake secrets into images or source control. - Avoid logging or echoing environment variables. - Scope secret volumes to the minimal mount path; do not mount to broad directories like /. - Use user-assigned identity when multiple apps share the same permissions boundary. MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I will draft config_security_map.csv to capture a mapping of app configuration keys to ACA secret names, injection method (env vs volume), env var name, volume mount path/file, and rotation notes. I'll include common secrets like database creds, JWT keys, TLS certs, storage connection strings, OAuth client secrets, SMTP creds. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/aca_secrets/config_security_map.csv << 'CSV' config_key,description,secret_name,source,inject_method,env_var,volume_name,mount_path,secret_file,rotation_policy,notes app.database.password,DB password for primary database,db-password,KeyVaultRef,env,DB_PASSWORD,,,restart-on-change,Managed identity requires get on secret app.database.connectionString,Full DB connection string,db-conn,KeyVaultRef,env,DB_CONNECTION_STRING,,,restart-on-change,Prefer DSN-style if supported app.jwt.signingKey,JWT private key (PEM),jwt-private-key,KeyVaultRef,volume,,jwt-secrets,/var/run/secrets/jwt,signing.key,rolling-rotate,Use versioned KV URL; rotate by updating keyVaultUrl app.jwt.publicKey,JWT public cert (PEM),jwt-public-cert,KeyVaultRef,volume,,jwt-secrets,/var/run/secrets/jwt,public.pem,rolling-rotate,Mount alongside signing key app.smtp.username,SMTP username,smtp-username,Inline/KeyVaultRef,env,SMTP_USERNAME,,,restart-on-change,Non-sensitive but still keep secret app.smtp.password,SMTP password,smtp-password,KeyVaultRef,env,SMTP_PASSWORD,,,restart-on-change, app.oauth.github.clientId,GitHub OAuth client ID,github-client-id,Inline/KeyVaultRef,env,GITHUB_CLIENT_ID,,,restart-on-change,Client ID not secret but keep consistent app.oauth.github.clientSecret,GitHub OAuth client secret,github-client-secret,KeyVaultRef,env,GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET,,,restart-on-change, app.storage.connectionString,Azure Storage connection string,storage-conn,KeyVaultRef,env,AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING,,,restart-on-change,Consider using MSI instead of key app.redis.password,Redis password,redis-password,KeyVaultRef,env,REDIS_PASSWORD,,,restart-on-change,Use TLS when connecting app.tls.certificate,Server certificate (PFX/PEM),tls-cert,KeyVaultRef,volume,,tls-vol,/var/run/secrets/tls,server.crt,rolling-rotate,Pair with key app.tls.privateKey,Server private key (PEM),tls-key,KeyVaultRef,volume,,tls-vol,/var/run/secrets/tls,server.key,rolling-rotate,File permissions restrictive app.tls.pfxPassword,Password for PFX bundle,tls-pfx-pass,KeyVaultRef,env,TLS_PFX_PASSWORD,,,restart-on-change,Do not log app.webhooks.sharedSecret,Webhook HMAC shared secret,webhook-secret,KeyVaultRef,env,WEBHOOK_SECRET,,,restart-on-change, app.sentry.dsn,Sentry DSN,sentry-dsn,Inline/KeyVaultRef,env,SENTRY_DSN,,,restart-on-change,Non-secret but config value app.apiKeys.thirdParty,Generic API key third-party,thirdparty-api-key,KeyVaultRef,env,THIRDPARTY_API_KEY,,,restart-on-change, CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I'll add a small example YAML into the templates folder that matches the mapping for quick reference. This is optional but helpful to users: one env var secret and one volume secret. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/aca_secrets/templates/containerapp-secrets-example.yaml << 'YAML' name: myapp type: Microsoft.App/containerApps location: westeurope identity: type: SystemAssigned properties: environmentId: /subscriptions//resourceGroups//providers/Microsoft.App/managedEnvironments/ configuration: secrets: - name: db-password keyVaultUrl: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/db-password/ - name: jwt-private-key keyVaultUrl: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/jwt-private-key/ template: containers: - name: api image: mcr.microsoft.com/azuredocs/containerapps-helloworld:latest env: - name: DB_PASSWORD secretRef: db-password volumeMounts: - volumeName: jwt-secrets mountPath: /var/run/secrets/jwt volumes: - name: jwt-secrets secrets: - secretRef: jwt-private-key path: signing.key YAML"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I’ll include a small README snippet in examples to demonstrate CLI-based secret set and restart, since some users prefer CLI over YAML. Then I'll verify file presence and preview the created files. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/aca_secrets/examples/cli-secrets-quickstart.sh << 'SH' #!/usr/bin/env bash set -euo pipefail RG=myrg APP=myapp # Set inline secret (development/testing only) az containerapp secret set --resource-group "$RG" --name "$APP" --secrets db-password="" # List secrets (names only) az containerapp secret list --resource-group "$RG" --name "$APP" # Restart to apply secret updates az containerapp restart --resource-group "$RG" --name "$APP" SH chmod +x /workspace/aca_secrets/examples/cli-secrets-quickstart.sh # Verify file tree and preview ls -R /workspace/aca_secrets && echo '--- secrets_layout.md (head) ---' && head -n 40 /workspace/aca_secrets/docs/secrets_layout.md && echo '--- config_security_map.csv ---' && cat /workspace/aca_secrets/config_security_map.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/aca_secrets: config_security_map.csv docs examples templates /workspace/aca_secrets/docs: secrets_layout.md /workspace/aca_secrets/examples: cli-secrets-quickstart.sh /workspace/aca_secrets/templates: containerapp-secrets-example.yaml --- secrets_layout.md (head) --- Title: Azure Container Apps — Secrets Layout and Mount Patterns Overview This document shows how application secrets are defined and mounted in Azure Container Apps (ACA) as either environment variables or files via secret volumes, including guidance for Azure Key Vault references, rotation, and verification. Security Model Summary - Secrets are defined at the Container App resource level under configuration.secrets. - Secrets can be sourced from: - Inline secret values (not recommended beyond development). - Azure Key Vault references via keyVaultUrl (recommended for production). - Secrets are consumed by containers as: - Environment variables (env[].secretRef), or - Files, by mounting a secret volume (template.volumes + volumeMounts). - Access to Key Vault requires a managed identity (system- or user-assigned) with get permission to the secret. - Secret value changes generally require a container restart to be effective; template changes produce new revisions. A) Use a secret as an environment variable 1) Define the secret Option 1 — Inline (dev/test): configuration: secrets: - name: db-password value: Option 2 — Key Vault reference (prod): identity: type: SystemAssigned configuration: secrets: - name: db-password keyVaultUrl: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/db-password/ 2) Reference the secret as an environment variable in the container template: containers: - name: api image: myregistry.azurecr.io/api:1.0 env: - name: DB_PASSWORD secretRef: db-password Notes - DB_PASSWORD will be available to the process as a normal environment variable. Avoid printing or logging it. - Changing db-password requires a restart to refresh environment variables. --- config_security_map.csv --- config_key,description,secret_name,source,inject_method,env_var,volume_name,mount_path,secret_file,rotation_policy,notes app.database.password,DB password for primary database,db-password,KeyVaultRef,env,DB_PASSWORD,,,restart-on-change,Managed identity requires get on secret app.database.connectionString,Full DB connection string,db-conn,KeyVaultRef,env,DB_CONNECTION_STRING,,,restart-on-change,Prefer DSN-style if supported app.jwt.signingKey,JWT private key (PEM),jwt-private-key,KeyVaultRef,volume,,jwt-secrets,/var/run/secrets/jwt,signing.key,rolling-rotate,Use versioned KV URL; rotate by updating keyVaultUrl app.jwt.publicKey,JWT public cert (PEM),jwt-public-cert,KeyVaultRef,volume,,jwt-secrets,/var/run/secrets/jwt,public.pem,rolling-rotate,Mount alongside signing key app.smtp.username,SMTP username,smtp-username,Inline/KeyVaultRef,env,SMTP_USERNAME,,,restart-on-change,Non-sensitive but still keep secret app.smtp.password,SMTP password,smtp-password,KeyVaultRef,env,SMTP_PASSWORD,,,restart-on-change, app.oauth.github.clientId,GitHub OAuth client ID,github-client-id,Inline/KeyVaultRef,env,GITHUB_CLIENT_ID,,,restart-on-change,Client ID not secret but keep consistent app.oauth.github.clientSecret,GitHub OAuth client secret,github-client-secret,KeyVaultRef,env,GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET,,,restart-on-change, app.storage.connectionString,Azure Storage connection string,storage-conn,KeyVaultRef,env,AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING,,,restart-on-change,Consider using MSI instead of key app.redis.password,Redis password,redis-password,KeyVaultRef,env,REDIS_PASSWORD,,,restart-on-change,Use TLS when connecting app.tls.certificate,Server certificate (PFX/PEM),tls-cert,KeyVaultRef,volume,,tls-vol,/var/run/secrets/tls,server.crt,rolling-rotate,Pair with key app.tls.privateKey,Server private key (PEM),tls-key,KeyVaultRef,volume,,tls-vol,/var/run/secrets/tls,server.key,rolling-rotate,File permissions restrictive app.tls.pfxPassword,Password for PFX bundle,tls-pfx-pass,KeyVaultRef,env,TLS_PFX_PASSWORD,,,restart-on-change,Do not log app.webhooks.sharedSecret,Webhook HMAC shared secret,webhook-secret,KeyVaultRef,env,WEBHOOK_SECRET,,,restart-on-change, app.sentry.dsn,Sentry DSN,sentry-dsn,Inline/KeyVaultRef,env,SENTRY_DSN,,,restart-on-change,Non-secret but config value app.apiKeys.thirdParty,Generic API key third-party,thirdparty-api-key,KeyVaultRef,env,THIRDPARTY_API_KEY,,,restart-on-change, ","stderr":""} Everything is in place: secrets_layout.md with clear examples and the updated config_security_map.csv. I’ll now provide the final summary with paths and key snippets for mounting as env and as a volume. Deliverables created 1) docs/secrets_layout.md - Location: /workspace/aca_secrets/docs/secrets_layout.md - Contents: - How to define Azure Container Apps secrets (inline and Key Vault reference). - How to mount a secret as an environment variable via env.secretRef. - How to mount a secret as a file via template.volumes + volumeMounts. - Full YAML example including identity and Key Vault references. - Azure CLI examples for setting/listing secrets and restarting the app. - Rotation guidance and verification tips. - Security best practices. Key snippets Environment variable injection (recommended for scalar secrets): configuration: secrets: - name: db-password keyVaultUrl: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/db-password/ template: containers: - name: api image: myregistry.azurecr.io/api:1.0 env: - name: DB_PASSWORD secretRef: db-password Mounted file (recommended for file-based secrets like keys/certs): configuration: secrets: - name: jwt-private-key keyVaultUrl: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/jwt-private-key/ template: volumes: - name: jwt-secrets secrets: - secretRef: jwt-private-key path: signing.key containers: - name: api image: myregistry.azurecr.io/api:1.0 volumeMounts: - volumeName: jwt-secrets mountPath: /var/run/secrets/jwt 2) config_security_map.csv - Location: /workspace/aca_secrets/config_security_map.csv - Updated mapping of app configuration keys to ACA secrets: - Columns: config_key, description, secret_name, source, inject_method, env_var, volume_name, mount_path, secret_file, rotation_policy, notes - Includes entries for database credentials, JWT signing keys, TLS certs/keys, OAuth secrets, SMTP creds, storage connection strings, and more. - Examples: - app.database.password → secret db-password via env var DB_PASSWORD (KeyVaultRef) - app.jwt.signingKey → secret jwt-private-key via volume jwt-secrets mounted to /var/run/secrets/jwt/signing.key (KeyVaultRef) - app.tls.privateKey → secret tls-key via volume tls-vol mounted to /var/run/secrets/tls/server.key Extras - templates/containerapp-secrets-example.yaml: A ready-to-adapt YAML container app template showing both env and volume secret patterns. - examples/cli-secrets-quickstart.sh: CLI workflow to set a secret and restart the app. Next steps you can take - Replace placeholder values (, , , , , image name) with your environment details. - Ensure your app’s managed identity has get permission in Key Vault for the referenced secrets. - Deploy via: az containerapp create --resource-group --name --yaml templates/containerapp-secrets-example.yaml - Rotate secrets by updating the Key Vault reference version or running az containerapp secret set, then restart the app. If you want, I can also generate a validation checklist tailored to your specific app keys and produce a smoke test container to verify env and file mounts at runtime (without exposing secret values).

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: We need to create a small reproducible workspace, generate a 10-row toy dataset, then compute top-3 categories by frequency and save outputs: top_categories.csv and selection_notes.md. Start by creating a working directory. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/top_n_categories && ls -R /workspace/top_n_categories"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/top_n_categories: ","stderr":""} Create the toy dataset with exactly 10 rows under a single column 'category' with a distribution that will yield a clear top-3: apple(3), banana(3), cherry(2), date(1), elderberry(1). {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/top_n_categories/toy_data.csv << 'CSV' category apple banana cherry apple banana apple banana cherry date elderberry CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Verify the dataset: line count and content look correct (11 lines including header, 10 data rows). {"type":"code","code_command":"wc -l /workspace/top_n_categories/toy_data.csv && echo "----" && cat /workspace/top_n_categories/toy_data.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"11 /workspace/top_n_categories/toy_data.csv ---- category apple banana cherry apple banana apple banana cherry date elderberry ","stderr":""} Compute top-3 categories by count. Use a deterministic tie-break: sort by count desc, then category asc. Save to top_categories.csv with header category,count. {"type":"code","code_command":"tail -n +2 /workspace/top_n_categories/toy_data.csv | sort | uniq -c | sort -k1,1nr -k2,2 | head -n 3 | awk '{print $2","$1}' | sed '1s/^/category,count\n/' > /workspace/top_n_categories/top_categories.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Show the computed top_categories.csv to confirm the result. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat /workspace/top_n_categories/top_categories.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"category,count apple,3 banana,3 cherry,2 ","stderr":""} Create selection_notes.md describing method, tie-break rules, inputs, counts, and final selection. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/top_n_categories/selection_notes.md << 'MD' Top-N Categories Selection Notes - Input data: /workspace/top_n_categories/toy_data.csv - Rows: 10 (plus header) - Column used: category - N (top-N): 3 Method 1) Count frequency of each category value. 2) Sort by count descending. 3) Tie-breaker: alphabetical (ascending) by category name. 4) Select top 3. Shell pipeline used tail -n +2 toy_data.csv | sort | uniq -c | sort -k1,1nr -k2,2 | head -n 3 | awk '{print $2","$1}' | sed '1s/^/category,count\\n/' > top_categories.csv Category counts (full) - apple: 3 - banana: 3 - cherry: 2 - date: 1 - elderberry: 1 Top 3 (after tie-break): 1) apple (3) 2) banana (3) 3) cherry (2) Outputs - /workspace/top_n_categories/top_categories.csv - /workspace/top_n_categories/selection_notes.md Assumptions - Input contained a single categorical column named "category". - Case-sensitive comparison; no trimming beyond CSV parsing. - Deterministic tie-break by alphabetical order. MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} List the directory to confirm both required artifacts exist. {"type":"code","code_command":"ls -1 /workspace/top_n_categories"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"selection_notes.md top_categories.csv toy_data.csv ","stderr":""} Preview selection_notes.md to confirm content. {"type":"code","code_command":"sed -n '1,200p' /workspace/top_n_categories/selection_notes.md"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Top-N Categories Selection Notes - Input data: /workspace/top_n_categories/toy_data.csv - Rows: 10 (plus header) - Column used: category - N (top-N): 3 Method 1) Count frequency of each category value. 2) Sort by count descending. 3) Tie-breaker: alphabetical (ascending) by category name. 4) Select top 3. Shell pipeline used tail -n +2 toy_data.csv | sort | uniq -c | sort -k1,1nr -k2,2 | head -n 3 | awk '{print $2","$1}' | sed '1s/^/category,count\\n/' > top_categories.csv Category counts (full) - apple: 3 - banana: 3 - cherry: 2 - date: 1 - elderberry: 1 Top 3 (after tie-break): 1) apple (3) 2) banana (3) 3) cherry (2) Outputs - /workspace/top_n_categories/top_categories.csv - /workspace/top_n_categories/selection_notes.md Assumptions - Input contained a single categorical column named "category". - Case-sensitive comparison; no trimming beyond CSV parsing. - Deterministic tie-break by alphabetical order. ","stderr":""} Completed: Computed top-3 categories by count on a 10-row toy dataset and saved the requested artifacts. Artifacts - top_categories.csv: /workspace/top_n_categories/top_categories.csv Contents: category,count apple,3 banana,3 cherry,2 - selection_notes.md: /workspace/top_n_categories/selection_notes.md Includes dataset description, method, tie-break rule, full counts, and the exact shell pipeline used to reproduce. Toy data used - Path: /workspace/top_n_categories/toy_data.csv - 10 rows under column "category" with values: apple, banana, cherry, apple, banana, apple, banana, cherry, date, elderberry. - Counts: apple(3), banana(3), cherry(2), date(1), elderberry(1) Method summary - Count frequencies, sort by count descending, break ties alphabetically ascending, take top N=3. If you want me to run this on a specific dataset you have, share the path or file and the same pipeline can be applied.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll generate synthetic data for 3 variables, compute a 3x3 Pearson correlation matrix, save it to /workspace/corr_matrix.csv, then verify by reading the file and identifying the top correlated pair. {"type":"code","code_command":"python - << 'PY' import random, math, csv # Reproducible synthetic data random.seed(42) N = 200 A = [random.gauss(0, 1) for _ in range(N)] B = [0.8*a + 0.2*random.gauss(0,1) for a in A] # highly correlated with A C = [random.gauss(0, 1) for _ in range(N)] # mostly independent def mean(xs): return sum(xs)/len(xs) def std(xs): m = mean(xs) return math.sqrt(sum((x - m)**2 for x in xs) / (len(xs) - 1)) def corr(x, y): mx, my = mean(x), mean(y) sx, sy = std(x), std(y) if sx == 0 or sy == 0: return 0.0 num = sum((xi - mx)*(yi - my) for xi, yi in zip(x, y)) return num / ((len(x) - 1) * sx * sy) vars_ = ['A','B','C'] data = {'A': A, 'B': B, 'C': C} # Compute correlation matrix matrix = [[corr(data[i], data[j]) for j in vars_] for i in vars_] # Save to CSV out_path = '/workspace/corr_matrix.csv' with open(out_path, 'w', newline='') as f: w = csv.writer(f) w.writerow([''] + vars_) for i, row in enumerate(matrix): w.writerow([vars_[i]] + [round(v, 6) for v in row]) print('Saved correlation matrix to', out_path) print('Matrix:') print([''] + vars_) for i, row in enumerate(matrix): print([vars_[i]] + [round(v, 6) for v in row]) PY"}
