This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. content.text: I'll create a new folder, generate a simple funnel.csv with three synthetic steps and their conversion rates, and write conversion_notes.md documenting the formulas and worked example. Then I'll verify the CSV values programmatically. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/funnel_conversion && cat > /workspace/funnel_conversion/funnel.csv << 'CSV' step,users,conv_from_prev_pct,conv_cumulative_pct Visited,1200,100.0,100.0 Signed Up,360,30.0,30.0 Purchased,90,25.0,7.5 CSV cat > /workspace/funnel_conversion/conversion_notes.md << 'MD' Title: Funnel Conversion Calculation Notes - Steps: Visited -> Signed Up -> Purchased - Counts (unique users): Visited=1200, Signed Up=360, Purchased=90 Formulas: 1) Step-to-step conversion (%): conv_from_prev_pct(step_i) = (users_i / users_{i-1}) * 100 For the first step, conv_from_prev_pct = 100 by convention. 2) Cumulative conversion (%): conv_cumulative_pct(step_i) = (users_i / users_first) * 100 Worked values: - Visited: conv_from_prev=100.0%, cumulative=100.0% - Signed Up: conv_from_prev=(360/1200)*100 = 30.0%, cumulative=(360/1200)*100 = 30.0% - Purchased: conv_from_prev=(90/360)*100 = 25.0%, cumulative=(90/1200)*100 = 7.5% Notes: - Use unique user counts at each step. - Small rounding differences may occur; values here are rounded to one decimal place. MD"}

First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Plan: Create a working directory, generate 24 synthetic UTC timestamps (one per hour across a single date) for reproducibility, compute an events-by-hour histogram including empty bins, save events_by_hour.csv at project root, and write binning_notes.md describing binning assumptions and mapping. Use code tool to create files and verify outputs. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e # 1) Setup project structure mkdir -p /workspace/time_binned_histogram/{data,scripts} cd /workspace/time_binned_histogram echo "Project structure initialized at: $(pwd)" ls -la # 2) Create a Python script to generate 24 synthetic timestamps and build histogram cat > scripts/generate_and_build.py << 'PY' import sys from datetime import datetime, timezone from pathlib import Path base_dir = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1] data_dir = base_dir / 'data' timestamps_path = data_dir / 'timestamps.txt' output_csv = base_dir / 'events_by_hour.csv' notes_md = base_dir / 'binning_notes.md' # Ensure data directory exists data_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) # Generate 24 deterministic synthetic timestamps: one per hour across a single UTC day # Date chosen for determinism; minutes/seconds vary deterministically by hour # This ensures exactly 24 events total synthetic = [ datetime(2025, 9, 27, h, (h * 3) % 60, (h * 7) % 60, tzinfo=timezone.utc) for h in range(24) ] # Write timestamps to file in ISO 8601 Zulu format with timestamps_path.open('w', encoding='utf-8') as f: for dt in synthetic: f.write(dt.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ') + '\n') # Read timestamps back and build histogram by UTC hour-of-day [0..23] counts = {h: 0 for h in range(24)} assigned = [] # list of (timestamp_str, hour) with timestamps_path.open('r', encoding='utf-8') as f: for line in f: ts = line.strip() if not ts: continue try: dt = datetime.strptime(ts, '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ') except ValueError as e: sys.stderr.write(f'Invalid timestamp format: {ts} -> {e}\n') sys.exit(1) # Treat timestamps as UTC (Z) hour = dt.hour counts[hour] += 1 assigned.append((ts, hour)) # Validate total event count total = sum(counts.values()) if total != 24: sys.stderr.write(f'Expected 24 events, got {total}\n') sys.exit(1) # Write CSV with all 24 hours present in ascending order with output_csv.open('w', encoding='utf-8') as f: f.write('hour,count\n') for h in range(24): f.write(f'{h},{counts[h]}\n') # Write binning notes with notes_md.open('w', encoding='utf-8') as f: f.write('Binning Notes for Events by Hour\n') f.write('\n') f.write('- Timezone: UTC (Zulu). Input timestamps use a trailing Z and are parsed as UTC.\n') f.write('- Binning logic: Hour-of-day bins are half-open intervals [HH:00:00, HH+1:00:00), inclusive of the lower bound and exclusive of the upper bound.\n') f.write('- Bin keys: Integers 0..23 representing the UTC hour-of-day.\n') f.write('- Empty bins: Included with a count of 0 (not applicable here since we used one event per hour).\n') f.write('- Input file: data/timestamps.txt (one ISO-8601 timestamp per line, with Z).\n') f.write('- Total events: 24 (one per hour).\n') f.write('- Date component: Present but not used for grouping; grouping is purely by UTC hour-of-day.\n') f.write('- Determinism: Synthetic timestamps are generated deterministically for reproducibility.\n') f.write('\n') f.write('Per-event hour assignment (timestamp -> hour):\n') for ts, h in assigned: f.write(f'- {ts} -> {h}\n') print('Generated files:') print(f' - {timestamps_path}') print(f' - {output_csv}') print(f' - {notes_md}') print('Total events:', total) PY # 3) Run the script to create outputs python3 scripts/generate_and_build.py # 4) Verify outputs exist and show their contents ls -la echo "\n== events_by_hour.csv ==" cat events_by_hour.csv echo "\n== binning_notes.md (first 40 lines) ==" nl -ba binning_notes.md | sed -n '1,40p' "}

J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. content.text: We need to generate a synthetic CSV with 10 rows including some negative amounts, then enforce the constraint amount >= 0 by splitting into cleaned.csv and violations.csv. I’ll first create a working folder and the synthetic.csv with a mix of positive, zero, and negative amounts. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/data_constraint && cat > /workspace/data_constraint/synthetic.csv << 'CSV' id,date,customer,amount,description 1,2025-09-28,Alice,120.50,Payment received 2,2025-09-28,Bob,-5.00,Refund error 3,2025-09-28,Carol,0,Zero amount edge case 4,2025-09-28,Dan,250,Invoice payment 5,2025-09-28,Eve,-0.01,Rounding issue 6,2025-09-28,Frank,99.99,Purchase 7,2025-09-28,Grace,-100.25,Chargeback 8,2025-09-28,Heidi,5000,Large payment 9,2025-09-28,Ivan,13.37,Microtransaction 10,2025-09-28,Judy,-42,Adjustment CSV ls -l /workspace/data_constraint && echo '--- synthetic.csv ---' && cat /workspace/data_constraint/synthetic.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 8 -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 519 Sep 28 00:00 synthetic.csv --- synthetic.csv --- id,date,customer,amount,description 1,2025-09-28,Alice,120.50,Payment received 2,2025-09-28,Bob,-5.00,Refund error 3,2025-09-28,Carol,0,Zero amount edge case 4,2025-09-28,Dan,250,Invoice payment 5,2025-09-28,Eve,-0.01,Rounding issue 6,2025-09-28,Frank,99.99,Purchase 7,2025-09-28,Grace,-100.25,Chargeback 8,2025-09-28,Heidi,5000,Large payment 9,2025-09-28,Ivan,13.37,Microtransaction 10,2025-09-28,Judy,-42,Adjustment ","stderr":""} Now I’ll enforce the constraint amount >= 0 by reading synthetic.csv and writing rows to cleaned.csv if amount is numeric and >= 0, otherwise to violations.csv. I’ll implement this with a short Python script to ensure correct CSV handling. {"type":"code","code_command":"python - << 'PY' import csv, pathlib p = pathlib.Path('/workspace/data_constraint') src = p/'synthetic.csv' cleaned = p/'cleaned.csv' viol = p/'violations.csv' with src.open(newline='') as f_in, cleaned.open('w', newline='') as f_ok, viol.open('w', newline='') as f_bad: rdr = csv.DictReader(f_in) fieldnames = rdr.fieldnames ok = csv.DictWriter(f_ok, fieldnames=fieldnames) bad = csv.DictWriter(f_bad, fieldnames=fieldnames) ok.writeheader(); bad.writeheader() for row in rdr: amt_raw = row.get('amount', '') try: amt = float(amt_raw) if amt >= 0: ok.writerow(row) else: bad.writerow(row) except Exception: # Non-numeric or missing count as violation bad.writerow(row) print('Done') PY ls -l /workspace/data_constraint && echo '--- cleaned.csv ---' && cat /workspace/data_constraint/cleaned.csv && echo '\n--- violations.csv ---' && cat /workspace/data_constraint/violations.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Done total 20 -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 263 Sep 28 00:00 cleaned.csv -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 303 Sep 28 00:00 synthetic.csv -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 270 Sep 28 00:00 violations.csv --- cleaned.csv --- id,date,customer,amount,description 1,2025-09-28,Alice,120.50,Payment received 3,2025-09-28,Carol,0,Zero amount edge case 4,2025-09-28,Dan,250,Invoice payment 6,2025-09-28,Frank,99.99,Purchase 8,2025-09-28,Heidi,5000,Large payment 9,2025-09-28,Ivan,13.37,Microtransaction --- violations.csv --- id,date,customer,amount,description 2,2025-09-28,Bob,-5.00,Refund error 5,2025-09-28,Eve,-0.01,Rounding issue 7,2025-09-28,Grace,-100.25,Chargeback 10,2025-09-28,Judy,-42,Adjustment ","stderr":""} I’ll add a quick verification of row counts to confirm we have 10 total rows split into 6 cleaned and 4 violations, and produce a small summary file. {"type":"code","code_command":"python - << 'PY' from pathlib import Path import csv base = Path('/workspace/data_constraint') counts = {} for name in ['synthetic.csv','cleaned.csv','violations.csv']: with (base/name).open() as f: rows = sum(1 for _ in f) - 1 # exclude header counts[name] = rows summary = f"Total rows: {counts['synthetic.csv']}\nPassed (amount >= 0): {counts['cleaned.csv']}\nFailed (amount < 0 or invalid): {counts['violations.csv']}\n" (base/'summary.txt').write_text(summary) print(summary) PY cat /workspace/data_constraint/summary.txt"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Total rows: 10 Passed (amount >= 0): 6 Failed (amount < 0 or invalid): 4 Total rows: 10 Passed (amount >= 0): 6 Failed (amount < 0 or invalid): 4 ","stderr":""} All files are created and verified. I’ll wrap up with the final summary and locations of the outputs. Completed constraint enforcement on synthetic data. What I did - Generated synthetic.csv (10 rows) with a column amount including positive, zero, and negative values. - Enforced the row-level constraint amount >= 0. - Split data into: • cleaned.csv: rows that satisfy the constraint (amount >= 0) • violations.csv: rows that fail (amount < 0; non-numeric or missing would also be routed here) - Wrote a summary.txt with counts. Outputs (saved under /workspace/data_constraint) - synthetic.csv — source data (10 rows + header) - cleaned.csv — 6 passing rows - violations.csv — 4 failing rows - summary.txt — counts of passed/failed rows Quick preview - cleaned.csv (amount >= 0): 1,Alice,120.50 3,Carol,0 4,Dan,250 6,Frank,99.99 8,Heidi,5000 9,Ivan,13.37 - violations.csv (amount < 0): 2,Bob,-5.00 5,Eve,-0.01 7,Grace,-100.25 10,Judy,-42 Next steps (optional) - Adjust the constraint or columns as needed (e.g., treat nulls). - Extend script to validate schema and data types before enforcement.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate content.text: I'll generate two toy distributions, run a pure-Python two-sample KS test (to avoid external deps), write ks_result.txt and drift_summary.md under /workspace, then verify the files exist and show a preview. {"type":"code","code_command":"python3 - << 'PY' import random, math, statistics from pathlib import Path # Deterministic toy data random.seed(42) n1, n2 = 500, 500 X = [random.gauss(0.0, 1.0) for _ in range(n1)] Y = [random.gauss(0.5, 1.0) for _ in range(n2)] # Two-sample KS statistic and asymptotic p-value def ks_2samp(a, b): a = sorted(a) b = sorted(b) i = j = 0 na, nb = len(a), len(b) cdf_a = cdf_b = 0.0 d = 0.0 while i < na and j < nb: if a[i] <= b[j]: i += 1 cdf_a = i / na else: j += 1 cdf_b = j / nb diff = abs(cdf_a - cdf_b) if diff > d: d = diff # Tail corrections if i < na: d = max(d, abs(1.0 - cdf_b)) if j < nb: d = max(d, abs(cdf_a - 1.0)) ne = na * nb / (na + nb) lam = (math.sqrt(ne) + 0.12 + 0.11 / math.sqrt(ne)) * d def qks(l): if l < 1e-12: return 1.0 # Kolmogorov distribution tail, converges fast for l>1 s = 0.0 for k in range(1, 101): s += (-1) ** (k - 1) * math.exp(-2 * (k * k) * (l * l)) return 2 * s p = max(0.0, min(1.0, qks(lam))) return d, p D, p = ks_2samp(X, Y) # Write results Path('/workspace').mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) with open('/workspace/ks_result.txt', 'w') as f: f.write(f'p-value={p:.6f}\n') with open('/workspace/drift_summary.md', 'w') as f: f.write('# Drift Summary\n') f.write('We ran a two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test between two toy distributions.\n\n') f.write(f'- Sample sizes: n1={n1}, n2={n2}\n') f.write('- Distributions: X ~ N(0, 1), Y ~ N(0.5, 1) (drawn with Python random.gauss)\n') f.write(f'- KS statistic D = {D:.4f}\n') f.write(f'- p-value = {p:.6f}\n\n') f.write('Interpretation:\n') if p < 0.05: f.write('The p-value is below 0.05, indicating a statistically significant difference between the two distributions (evidence of drift).\n') else: f.write('The p-value is above 0.05, so we do not reject the null hypothesis; no significant drift detected.\n') f.write('\nAdditional context:\n') f.write(f'- Sample means: mean(X)={statistics.mean(X):.3f}, mean(Y)={statistics.mean(Y):.3f}\n') f.write(f'- Sample stds: std(X)={statistics.pstdev(X):.3f}, std(Y)={statistics.pstdev(Y):.3f}\n') print('Wrote /workspace/ks_result.txt and /workspace/drift_summary.md') PY"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"Wrote /workspace/ks_result.txt and /workspace/drift_summary.md ","stderr":""} Now verify the files and show a quick preview of their contents. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e ls -l /workspace/ks_result.txt /workspace/drift_summary.md printf "--- ks_result.txt ---\n" cat /workspace/ks_result.txt printf "--- drift_summary.md (first 20 lines) ---\n" head -n 20 /workspace/drift_summary.md"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 17 Sep 28 00:00 /workspace/drift_summary.md -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 17 Sep 28 00:00 /workspace/ks_result.txt --- ks_result.txt --- p-value=0.000000 --- drift_summary.md (first 20 lines) --- # Drift Summary We ran a two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test between two toy distributions. - Sample sizes: n1=500, n2=500 - Distributions: X ~ N(0, 1), Y ~ N(0.5, 1) (drawn with Python random.gauss) - KS statistic D = 0.2140 - p-value = 0.000000 Interpretation: The p-value is below 0.05, indicating a statistically significant difference between the two distributions (evidence of drift). Additional context: - Sample means: mean(X)=-0.020, mean(Y)=0.507 - Sample stds: std(X)=0.990, std(Y)=1.030 ","stderr":""} Created and saved: - /workspace/ks_result.txt - /workspace/drift_summary.md Summary of results: - KS statistic D ≈ 0.2140 - p-value ≈ 0.000000 - Interpretation: With p < 0.05, we detect statistically significant distribution shift (drift) between the two toy distributions (X ~ N(0,1) vs Y ~ N(0.5,1)). You can view them with: - cat /workspace/ks_result.txt - less /workspace/drift_summary.md If you prefer a specific p-value format (e.g., fewer decimals), tell me and I’ll adjust the file formatting.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: We need to design precise CSV-to-SQL mappings in Azure Synapse (serverless and dedicated), especially around NULLs and date/time parsing. Rather than guess, I’ll consult Microsoft docs for COPY INTO and OPENROWSET CSV options (DATEFORMAT, TIMEFORMAT, NULL handling, FIELDQUOTE, FIELDTERMINATOR, ENCODING, PARSER_VERSION) to ensure correct guidance and examples. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Synapse Analytics COPY INTO CSV options DATEFORMAT TIMEFORMAT FIELDQUOTE FIELDTERMINATOR ENCODING MAXERRORS ERRORFILE NULL handling", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"COPY INTO (Transact-SQL) for Azure Synapse dedicated SQL pool","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-tsql-copy-into"},{"title":"Bulk load data using the COPY statement","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-copy-into-overview"},{"title":"OPENROWSET in serverless SQL pool (CSV)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/query-single-csv-file"},{"title":"OPENROWSET with CSV and FORMAT options","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-openrowset#csv-support"},{"title":"CREATE EXTERNAL FILE FORMAT (Transact-SQL) for delimited text","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/sql/t-sql/statements/create-external-file-format-transact-sql?view=azure-sqldw-latest#delimitedtext"},{"title":"Error handling with COPY INTO (MAXERRORS and ERRORFILE)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-copy-into#load-failures"}],"summary":"COPY INTO for dedicated SQL supports CSV FILE_TYPE with options: FIELDTERMINATOR, FIELDQUOTE, ROWTERMINATOR, FIRSTROW, ENCODING (UTF8, UTF16LE, etc.), DATEFORMAT, TIMEFORMAT, MAXERRORS, and ERRORFILE. Serverless SQL OPENROWSET CSV supports FORMAT='CSV', PARSER_VERSION='2.0', HEADER_ROW=TRUE, FIELDTERMINATOR, FIELDQUOTE, ROWTERMINATOR, and encoding. Neither engine auto-maps textual markers like 'NULL' to SQL NULL; you must normalize in SELECT using NULLIF, LTRIM/RTRIM, CASE, and TRY_CONVERT/TRY_CAST for types. ISO-8601 timestamps parse with style 126/127; datetimeoffset should use 127. Empty fields become empty strings; to preserve as NULL you must transform explicitly."}} Confirm specifics for serverless OPENROWSET CSV options: HEADER_ROW, FIELDQUOTE, support for embedded newlines, PARSER_VERSION=2.0, and any NULL/DATEFORMAT options. Then confirm details for detecting invalid dates with TRY_CONVERT and style codes (126/127/23/101 etc.). {"type":"web","q":"Synapse serverless OPENROWSET CSV HEADER_ROW PARSER_VERSION 2.0 FIELDQUOTE embedded newline DATEFORMAT NULL handling", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Serverless SQL pool query CSV files","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/query-single-csv-file#format-parameters-for-csv"},{"title":"OPENROWSET function (Transact-SQL) in serverless","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-openrowset"},{"title":"CSV parsing behavior and quoted field newlines","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-openrowset#quoted-fields-and-new-lines"},{"title":"Date and time styles in CAST and CONVERT (Transact-SQL)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/sql/t-sql/functions/cast-and-convert-transact-sql?view=sql-server-ver16#date-and-time-styles"},{"title":"ISJSON, TRY_CONVERT references","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/sql/t-sql/functions/isjson-transact-sql?view=sql-server-ver16"}],"summary":"Serverless OPENROWSET supports FORMAT='CSV', FIELDTERMINATOR, FIELDQUOTE (default '"'), ROWTERMINATOR, ESCAPECHAR, HEADER_ROW=TRUE, and PARSER_VERSION='2.0'. Quoted fields can include delimiter characters and newlines. There is no NULL_VALUES parameter; empty fields are read as empty strings. Normalize nulls in T-SQL via LTRIM/RTRIM/NULLIF and CASE. Use TRY_CONVERT with datetime styles: 126 ISO8601, 127 ISO8601 with time zone. ISJSON validates JSON content."}} Check dedicated PolyBase/external tables options for delimited text file format (STRING_DELIMITER, FIELD_TERMINATOR) and notes on date parsing (which is during SELECT, not file format), plus encoding options. This informs mapping guidance. {"type":"web","q":"Synapse dedicated SQL external table delimited text CREATE EXTERNAL FILE FORMAT STRING_DELIMITER FIELD_TERMINATOR encoding UTF8 NULL handling", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"CREATE EXTERNAL FILE FORMAT (Transact-SQL) - DelimitedText","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/sql/t-sql/statements/create-external-file-format-transact-sql?view=azure-sqldw-latest#delimitedtext"},{"title":"CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE (Transact-SQL) - Synapse dedicated SQL pool","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/sql/t-sql/statements/create-external-table-transact-sql?view=azure-sqldw-latest"},{"title":"Best practices for loading data into dedicated SQL pool","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/best-practices-dedicated-sql-pool#data-loading"}],"summary":"DelimitedText external file formats support FIELD_TERMINATOR, STRING_DELIMITER, and encoding. They don't define NULL semantics; conversions happen in SELECT/INSERT. For robust ingestion, land to a staging table of NVARCHAR columns via COPY INTO or external table, then transform with TRY_CONVERT/TRY_CAST into final typed table."}} Confirm COPY INTO details for DATEFORMAT and TIMEFORMAT, as these directly affect parsing for dedicated SQL pool. Also confirm ENCODING values and ERRORFILE patterns. Then we'll proceed to implement mapping docs and test CSV. {"type":"web","q":"COPY INTO DATEFORMAT TIMEFORMAT CSV Azure Synapse example DATEFORMAT='yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss' ENCODING UTF8 ERRORFILE MAXERRORS", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"COPY INTO reference and examples (Azure Synapse)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-copy-into#examples"},{"title":"COPY INTO supported file formats and options","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-copy-into#csv"},{"title":"Troubleshoot COPY INTO","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/synapse-analytics/sql/develop-copy-into-troubleshoot"}],"summary":"COPY INTO CSV supports DATEFORMAT and TIMEFORMAT patterns (for example, DATEFORMAT='yyyy-MM-dd', TIMEFORMAT='HH:mm:ss.fff zzz'). It also supports FIELDQUOTE, FIELDTERMINATOR, ROWTERMINATOR, FIRSTROW, ENCODING ('UTF8','UTF16','UTF16LE'), MAXERRORS, and ERRORFILE to capture row-level failures. COPY INTO does not automatically treat string 'NULL' as SQL NULL; you must pre-process or load into staging then transform."}} We have enough authoritative grounding to write a thorough mapping guide and craft comprehensive test rows. Next, I’ll scaffold the workspace structure and create mapping.md and a test_case_rows.csv with many edge cases (NULL-like tokens, whitespace, quotes, embedded commas/newlines, various date/time formats, offsets, overflows, booleans variants, numeric with thousands separators and currency, guid, varbinary hex, JSON content, Unicode). Not reliable; prefer TRY_CONVERT IS NOT NULL). - Error capture with COPY INTO ERRORFILE. - Character encoding notes (UTF-8 vs UTF-16, BOM, Unicode). - Testing instructions referencing test_case_rows.csv. Authoritative references - COPY INTO (dedicated SQL pool): supports CSV FILE_TYPE with FIELDTERMINATOR, FIELDQUOTE, ROWTERMINATOR, FIRSTROW, ENCODING, DATEFORMAT, TIMEFORMAT, MAXERRORS, ERRORFILE. - OPENROWSET (serverless SQL pool): supports FORMAT='CSV', PARSER_VERSION='2.0', HEADER_ROW=TRUE, FIELDTERMINATOR, FIELDQUOTE, ROWTERMINATOR, ESCAPECHAR, and encoding; quoted fields can include commas/newlines. - T-SQL conversion: TRY_CONVERT, TRY_CAST, styles 23/120/126/127 for dates/times; ISJSON; conversion behaviors for DATETIME2, DATETIMEOFFSET, TIME, DECIMAL, MONEY, BIT, VARBINARY. Key principles 1) Land raw text, then type: Ingest CSV into a staging projection of NVARCHAR columns. Only after landing, normalize nulls and convert types using TRY_* to avoid load failures. 2) Normalize null-ish inputs: Treat '', 'NULL', 'null', 'N/A', 'NaN', 'Infinity', and whitespace-only as NULL by policy unless business rules dictate otherwise. 3) Be explicit with date/time styles: Favor ISO-8601. Use TRY_CONVERT(datetime2, s, 126) for ISO; TRY_CONVERT(datetimeoffset, s, 127) if offset/Z present. Fall back carefully to regional formats with explicit styles. 4) Strip formatting from numerics: Remove thousands separators and currency symbols prior to TRY_CONVERT to DECIMAL/MONEY. 5) Map booleans via CASE: true/false, yes/no, y/n, 1/0 → BIT. 6) Validate semantics: Assert ranges (e.g., 0<=percentage<=100), precision fits (DECIMAL(p,s)), and time/date validity via TRY_CONVERT IS NOT NULL. CSV parser configuration - Use FIELDQUOTE '"' and PARSER_VERSION '2.0' to safely parse quoted fields containing delimiters or newlines. - Choose FIELDTERMINATOR=',' (or another) and ROWTERMINATOR='0x0A' (LF) consistently. - Encoding: Prefer UTF-8. For UTF-16LE files, specify ENCODING accordingly (COPY INTO) or ensure correct interpretation in OPENROWSET. failed type conversion (e.g., date/text issues): SELECT * FROM dbo.stg_csv_raw WHERE TRY_CONVERT(int, int_col_raw) IS NULL AND NULLIF(TRIM(int_col_raw), '') IS NOT NULL; - Range checks: SELECT * FROM ISJSON(json_col_raw) = 0; Edge cases and guidance - Empty vs NULL: CSV empty fields load as ''. Use NULLIF(TRIM(x),'') to map to NULL. '""' yields empty string as well. - 'NULL' tokens: Normalize via CASE/TRIM/LOWER to NULL. - Whitespace: Apply TRIM before checks. Non-breaking spaces may require REPLACE(x, NCHAR(160), ' '). - Dates: - Prefer ISO: 'yyyy-MM-dd' (style 23) and 'yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss[.fffffff][Z|±hh:mm]' (styles 126/127). - Ambiguous '12/11/2021': Decide culture; use 101 (MDY) or 103 (DMY) explicitly. - Invalid dates (2021-02-29, 2021-04-31) → TRY_CONVERT returns NULL. - DATETIME vs DATETIME2: use DATETIME2(7) for fidelity; SMALLDATETIME rounds to nearest minute. - TIME '24:00:00' invalid; use '00:00:00' next day instead. - Numerics: - Thousands separators: strip commas (and dots in some locales) before conversion. - Currency: strip '$','€','£'; consider negative with parentheses e.g. '(1,234.56)' → map by REPLACE and CASE: multiply by -1. - Scientific notation: TRY_CONVERT(float, '1.2E3') succeeds; DECIMAL may fail—convert float then cast to DECIMAL if range fits. - Booleans: normalize various tokens to BIT via CASE. - GUIDs: TRY_CONVERT(uniqueidentifier, s) handles standard hyphenated form. - VARBINARY: - Hex: TRY_CONVERT(varbinary(max), '0xDEADBEEF', 1) succeeds. - Base64: SELECT CAST(N'' as xml).value('xs:base64Binary(sql:variable("@s"))','varbinary(max)') as workaround. - Unicode: Use NVARCHAR for text; ensure UTF-8 or UTF-16 encoding declared properly. - Embedded delimiters/newlines: Use FIELDQUOTE '"' and PARSER_VERSION '2.0' (serverless) or FIELDQUOTE in COPY INTO; ensure authoring tools quote such fields. - Error capture: In dedicated, use COPY INTO ERRORFILE to capture bad rows for triage; MAXERRORS to tolerate a threshold. Reusable helper inline patterns - normalize_nullish(x) → CASE WHEN x IS NULL OR NULLIF(TRIM(x),'') IS NULL OR TRIM(LOWER(x)) IN ('null','n/a','nan','infinity') THEN NULL ELSE TRIM(x) END - to_decimal_clean(x) → TRY_CONVERT(decimal(18,4), REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(normalize_nullish(x), ',', ''), '$', ''), ' ', '')) - to_bit(x) → CASE WHEN TRIM(LOWER(x)) IN ('1','true','t','yes','y') THEN 1 WHEN TRIM(LOWER(x)) IN ('0','false','f','no','n') THEN 0 WHEN NULLIF(TRIM(x),'') IS NULL THEN NULL ELSE NULL END - to_date_iso_or_locale(x) → COALESCE(TRY_CONVERT(date, TRIM(x), 23), TRY_CONVERT(date, TRIM(x), 101), TRY_CONVERT(date, TRIM(x), 103)) - to_datetime2_iso(x) → COALESCE(TRY_CONVERT(datetime2(7), TRIM(x), 127), TRY_CONVERT(datetime2(7), TRIM(x), 126), TRY_CONVERT(datetime2(7), TRIM(x), 120)) - to_datetimeoffset_iso(x) → COALESCE(TRY_CONVERT(datetimeoffset(7), TRIM(x), 127), TRY_CONVERT(datetimeoffset(7), TRIM(x))) Testing with provided CSV - Upload test_case_rows.csv to your storage and run the serverless or dedicated patterns above. - Validate each column’s conversion and review rows that result in NULL after TRY_CONVERT when the raw was non-nullish.
