brief README snippet to mapping.md for file locations and quick-start verification, then reprint tail of mapping.md to ensure content formatting. But mapping.md is already thorough. Next, finalize by summarizing outputs and usage steps in the solution. Created: - /workspace/synapse_csv_mapping/mapping.md - /workspace/synapse_csv_mapping/test_case_rows.csv What’s inside mapping.md: - A comprehensive Synapse CSV-to-SQL mapping guide for serverless and dedicated SQL pools. - Practical, doc-grounded guidance on: - Parser options: FIELDQUOTE, FIELDTERMINATOR, ROWTERMINATOR, ENCODING, PARSER_VERSION=2.0, HEADER_ROW. - Robust NULL handling: normalize '', whitespace, 'NULL', 'null', 'N/A', 'NaN', 'Infinity' to SQL NULL via TRIM/LOWER/CASE/NULLIF. - Typed conversion patterns using TRY_CONVERT/TRY_CAST and explicit styles: - Dates: style 23 (yyyy-MM-dd), 101 (mm/dd/yyyy), 103 (dd/mm/yyyy). - Datetimes: style 126/127 (ISO-8601), 120 (yyyy-mm-dd hh:mi:ss). - Datetimeoffset: style 127 for ISO-8601 with Z/offset. - Numbers: strip formatting symbols (commas, spaces, currency, parentheses) before conversion; scientific notation considerations. - Booleans to BIT mapping: true/false/yes/no/y/n/1/0. - GUIDs, VARBINARY hex (style 1), JSON validation with ISJSON. - Unicode and encoding notes (UTF-8, UTF-16LE). - Error capture (COPY INTO ERRORFILE) and QA queries to detect failed conversions. - Full examples: - Serverless OPENROWSET landing NVARCHARs then projecting typed columns. - Dedicated COPY INTO to staging, then INSERT…SELECT transforms into final schema. What’s inside test_case_rows.csv: - Header with representative columns: row_id, int_col, bigint_col, decimal_col, float_col, money_col, bit_col, bool_text_col, date_col, datetime2_col, time_col, datetimeoffset_col, guid_col, varchar_col, nvarchar_col, varbinary_col, json_col, nullable_col - 20 rows that stress edge cases: - Blank fields, whitespace-only, and tokens like NULL/null/N/A/NaN/Infinity. - Numeric variants: thousands separators, currency ($), parentheses for negatives, scientific notation, extreme/overflow values. - Boolean variants: 1/0, true/false/t/f, yes/no/y/n, unexpected tokens. - Dates/times: ISO-8601, US and DMY formats, invalid dates (2021-02-29, 2021-04-31), min dates, times with milliseconds, invalid '24:00:00', datetime with Z and offsets. - GUIDs (valid/invalid), VARBINARY hex (valid/invalid), JSON (valid/invalid). - Strings with commas, embedded quotes (CSV-escaped), embedded newline (quoted), Unicode across scripts and emoji. How to use - Serverless SQL pool: - Upload CSV to your storage. - Run the provided OPENROWSET WITH (...) landing schema (all NVARCHARs), then the SELECT projection that normalizes nulls and converts to final types. - Dedicated SQL pool: - Create staging (NVARCHAR) and final (typed) tables from mapping.md. - COPY INTO staging using CSV options (FIELDQUOTE='"', FIELDTERMINATOR=',', ROWTERMINATOR='0x0A', ENCODING='UTF8', FIRSTROW=2, MAXERRORS, ERRORFILE). - INSERT…SELECT with normalization and TRY_CONVERT logic into the final table. Artifacts on disk - mapping doc: /workspace/synapse_csv_mapping/mapping.md - test CSV: /workspace/synapse_csv_mapping/test_case_rows.csv Next steps - Replace / placeholders with your paths. - Optionally extend boolean/null token lists to match your domain. - Add unit tests (T-SQL assertions) around TRY_CONVERT IS NOT NULL for each column to gate ingestion quality.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Confirm Azure ML evaluation artifact conventions and standard metrics via official docs before drafting files, to ensure the contract is aligned with common practices. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation artifacts metrics outputs MLflow predictions.jsonl metrics.json evaluation job outputs structure", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure Machine Learning","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Track experiments with MLflow in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"Evaluate models - metrics for classification and regression","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"Benchmark inference - latency and throughput","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and named outputs","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-model#view-and-download-job-outputs"}],"summary":"Docs indicate standard evaluation outputs include per-row predictions (often JSONL or Parquet), aggregate metrics (metrics.json), optional plots/diagnostics, and MLflow artifact logging. Common classification metrics: accuracy, precision/recall/F1 macro, AUROC, PR-AUC. Regression: MAE, RMSE, R2, MAPE. Inference benchmarking often logs p95 latency and throughput. Artifacts are typically logged under a named output folder (e.g., eval/)."}}] Scaffold a working directory in /workspace to hold the contract and the CSV. may include per-class (classification) and per-slice metrics. - run_metadata.json: Reproducibility metadata: model identifiers, dataset identifiers, seeds, environment info. Recommended but optional artifacts: - labels.jsonl: Required only when labels are not embedded in predictions.jsonl. - plots/: confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png (classification), residuals.png (regression). - diagnostics/: calibration.json, fairness.json, error_analysis.json. - slices.json: Cohort definitions used for per-slice metrics. 3) File Specifications 3.1 predictions.jsonl (required) - Format: JSON Lines (one JSON object per line) Fields: - id (string): Unique identifier for the example. - y_true (string|number|null): Ground-truth label/target if available; may be null if labels stored separately. - y_pred (string|number): Predicted label/target. - proba (object|null): For classification, a mapping from class label to probability in [0,1]. Should sum to ~1.0 if present. - aux (object|null): Optional auxiliary info (features hash, cohort tags, etc.). Example lines: {"id":"row-0001","y_true":"cat","y_pred":"cat","proba":{"cat":0.92,"dog":0.06,"other":0.02}} {"id":"row-0002","y_true":12.4,"y_pred":13.1} 3.2 metrics.json (required) - Top-level keys: - tafake_key_type: classification | regression - overall: Aggregate metrics for the whole dataset - per_class: Classification only; dictionary keyed by class label - slices: Optional; dictionary keyed by slice name/cohort - inference_benchmark: Optional; latency and throughput if measured Classification metrics (overall): - accuracy - precision_macro, recall_macro, f1_macro - auroc_macro (binary: AUROC; multi-class: macro-averaged one-vs-rest) - pr_auc_macro (macro-averaged PR-AUC) Regression metrics (overall): - mae, rmse, r2, mape Optional benchmarking: - latency_p50_ms, latency_p95_ms, latency_p99_ms - throughput_qps Schema example: { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.957, "precision_macro": 0.949, "recall_macro": 0.944, "f1_macro": 0.946, "auroc_macro": 0.981, "pr_auc_macro": 0.973 }, "per_class": { "cat": {"precision": 0.95, "recall": 0.96, "f1": 0.955}, "dog": {"precision": 0.94, "recall": 0.92, "f1": 0.93} }, "slices": { "small_images": {"f1_macro": 0.92}, "large_images": {"f1_macro": 0.95} }, "inference_benchmark": { "latency_p95_ms": 42.3, "throughput_qps": 275.4 } } 3.3 run_metadata.json (required) - Keys: - model_name, model_version or model_uri - data_asset or dataset_version - seed(s) - azureml: { run_id, job_name, image, conda_env, git_commit } - timestamp Example: { "model_name": "my-classifier", "model_version": "1.2.0", "data_asset": "azureml://datastores/workspaceblobstore/paths/datasets/v5/", "seed": 1337, "azureml": { "run_id": "ml-evl-abc123", "job_name": "eval-my-classifier-1.2.0", "image": "mcr.microsoft.com/azureml/openmpi4.1.0-ubuntu20.04:latest", "conda_env": "conda.yaml", "git_commit": "abcdef1234567890" }, "timestamp": "2025-09-28T00:00:00Z" } 3.4 labels.jsonl (conditional) - Only required if y_true is not included in predictions.jsonl. - Format: {"id": "...", "y_true": } 4) Metric Definitions Classification: - accuracy = correct / total - precision_macro = mean_i precision_i, recall_macro = mean_i recall_i - f1_macro = mean_i (2·precision_i·recall_i / (precision_i+recall_i)) - auroc_macro = macro-average over one-vs-rest ROC AUC - pr_auc_macro = macro-average PR AUC Regression: - mae = mean(|y_true - y_pred|) - rmse = sqrt(mean((y_true - y_pred)^2)) - r2 = 1 - SS_res/SS_tot - mape = mean(|(y_true - y_pred)/y_true|), with safe handling for zeros Latency/Throughput (if benchmarked): - latency_p95_ms = 95th percentile model inference time per example - throughput_qps = sustained queries per second 5) Quality Gates (example defaults; update per project requirements) - Classification: f1_macro >= 0.70 (fail if below) - Regression: rmse <= baseline_rmse * 1.05 (warn if > 1.05x) - Data leakage checks: id uniqueness in predictions.jsonl (fail if duplicates) - Artifact completeness: required files present and valid JSON (fail if not) 6) Reproducibility Requirements - Log model/data versions and random seeds in run_metadata.json. - Fix seeds in evaluation code for any stochastic steps. - Record environment (image, conda, Python, CUDA/cuDNN) in run_metadata.json. 7) Azure ML and MLflow Integration - Write all artifacts to a single folder (e.g., outputs/). In AzureML jobs, surface this folder as a named output (eval/). - With MLflow: python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") - Download via Azure CLI: az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval 8) Validation Checklist - predictions.jsonl present, newline-delimited JSON, unique ids - metrics.json present, valid JSON with required keys for tafake_key_type - run_metadata.json present with model/data versions and environment - If labels not embedded: labels.jsonl present - Quality gates executed; results recorded (pass/warn/fail) 9) Example Directory Structure - outputs/ - predictions.jsonl - metrics.json - run_metadata.json - labels.jsonl (conditional) - plots/ (optional) - diagnostics/ (optional) 10) Versioning and Backward Compatibility - Increment contract version on breaking changes and record in run_metadata.json under contract_version. - Avoid renaming required keys without deprecation period. MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Fill bundle_index.csv with the list of expected files, their purpose, required status, and MIME type to guide producers and CI checks. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions with optional labels and probabilities,required,application/jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate evaluation metrics (overall and optional per-class/slice),required,application/json outputs/run_metadata.json,Reproducibility and traceability metadata,required,application/json outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels when not embedded in predictions,conditional,application/jsonl outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix plot (classification),recommended,image/png outputs/plots/roc_curve.png,ROC curve plot (classification),optional,image/png outputs/plots/pr_curve.png,Precision-Recall curve plot (classification),optional,image/png outputs/plots/residuals.png,Residuals plot (regression),optional,image/png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics (ECE, bins),optional,application/json outputs/diagnostics/fairness.json,Fairness metrics per sensitive attribute,optional,application/json outputs/slices.json,Definitions for evaluation cohorts/slices,optional,application/json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Verify that both files exist and show a short preview to confirm content was written correctly. {"type":"code","code_command":"echo '=== eval_contract.md (first 60 lines) ===' && sed -n '1,60p' /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md && echo '\n=== bundle_index.csv ===' && cat /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"=== eval_contract.md (first 60 lines) === Azure ML Evaluation Contract 1) Purpose and Scope - body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Confirm Azure Key Vault secret naming constraints (allowed characters, max length, case sensitivity) to ensure the proposed convention is valid. Use web search to reference official Microsoft docs. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Key Vault secret naming rules allowed characters length case sensitivity hyphen underscore", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Azure Key Vault secrets - About, concepts, and operations","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/key-vault/secrets/about-secrets"},{"title":"Azure Key Vault naming rules and restrictions","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/key-vault/general/overview#key-vault-objects"},{"title":"Secret client library for .NET - Azure Key Vault","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/key-vault/secrets/secret-client"},{"title":"Best practices to use Azure Key Vault","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/key-vault/general/best-practices"},{"title":"Azure Key Vault keys, secrets, and certificates naming requirements","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/key-vault/general/about-keys-secrets-certificates#name-and-version"}],"summary":"Microsoft docs indicate Key Vault secret names must be 1-127 characters, may contain only alphanumeric characters and dashes (-), and are case-insensitive. Underscores are not allowed in secret names. Secret versions are 32-character GUIDs assigned automatically. Use contentType and tags for additional metadata. Avoid PII in names."} Now that constraints are confirmed (no underscores, hyphen allowed, 1–127 chars, case-insensitive), design a robust naming convention schema with tokens like env, app, component, purpose, provider, scope, region, tier, and variant. Include validation regex, length budgeting, abbreviations, and examples. Then scaffold directories and files: /workspace/keyvault_naming/docs/secret_naming.md plus three CSV samples for dev/test/prod under /workspace/keyvault_naming/samples/. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/keyvault_naming/{docs,samples} && ls -R /workspace/keyvault_naming"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/keyvault_naming: docs samples /workspace/keyvault_naming/docs: /workspace/keyvault_naming/samples: ","stderr":""} Write the comprehensive secret_naming.md with: principles, constraints, canonical schema, token glossary with allowed values, regex, examples, do/don’t, migration mapping from underscore style, tagging strategy, rotation and ownership, and validation guidance. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/keyvault_naming/docs/secret_naming.md << 'MD' Azure Key Vault Secret Naming Convention Purpose This document defines a practical, enforceable convention for Azure Key Vault secret names that supports discoverability, automation, and compliance across environments. Authoritative constraints (from Microsoft docs) - Allowed characters: A–Z, a–z, 0–9, and dash (-) only. Underscores (_) and other symbols are not allowed. - Length: 1–127 characters. - Case-insensitive: names are treated case-insensitively; prefer lower-case for consistency. - Versions: managed automatically by Key Vault (a 32-char GUID). Do not embed version in the name. - Metadata: use secret tags (key–value) and contentType for auxiliary metadata instead of encoding everything in the name. Design goals - Machine-parseable with stable, fixed-order tokens - Portable across subscriptions/tenants - Short, readable, and within length limits - Consistent across environments and apps Canonical schema Use hyphen-separated tokens in the order below. Keep tokens short with standard abbreviations. -------- - Required tokens: env, app, purpose - Optional tokens: component, provider, scope, region, tier, variant - Omit trailing empty tokens entirely. Do not use consecutive dashes or placeholders like "na". Token glossary - env (required): dev | tst | stg | prd | sbx | uat - app (required): lowercase a–z0–9, short code (max 12 chars). Example: checkout → chkout, identity → id - component (optional): sub-system or microservice. Example: web, api, worker, dlx - purpose (required): concise description of secret type/purpose. Examples: - connstr, endpoint, api-key, client-id, client-secret, password, sas, token, cert, thumbprint, webhook, private-key, public-key - provider (optional): originating service/provider. Examples: sql, pg, mysql, redis, cosmos, storage, kv, appins, appcfg, aad, b2c, gh, databricks, eventhub, sb, keycloak - scope (optional): audience or usage scope. Examples: internal, external, admin, readonly, readwrite, ingest, egress, analytics - region (optional): Azure region short code (pick one standard mapping per org), e.g., eus2, weu, westeurope → weu, eastus2 → eus2 - tier (optional): svc tier or criticality. Examples: free, basic, std, prem, crt1..crt3 - variant (optional): alternative/rotated/blue-green variant. Examples: a | b, v2, canary, backup Examples - prd-bill-api-connstr-sql-internal-eus2-std - stg-chkout-web-api-key-adb-external-weu - dev-id-auth-client-secret-aad-internal - prd-id-auth-thumbprint-cert - tst-chkout-worker-password-redis-readonly-eus2 - prd-ops-runbook-webhook-gh - dev-ml-features-connstr-storage-analytics Validation - Enforce regex: ^[a-z0-9]+(?:-[a-z0-9]+){1,8}$ - At least two tokens (env + app + ...) - All tokens must be lowercase alphanumeric - Separated by single hyphen - Recommended maximum token lengths (budget guideline): - env: 3, app: <=12, component: <=12, purpose: <=15, provider: <=12, scope: <=12, region: <=6, tier: <=6, variant: <=6 - Overall target: <= 80 characters for operational comfort (127 is hard limit) Do and Don’t Do - Use lower-case - Use single hyphen as delimiter - Keep app/component short and stable - Prefer tags for mutable metadata (owner, rotation, data sensitivity) - Reuse purpose/provider vocab across teams Don’t - Don’t use underscores or spaces (invalid in Key Vault secret names) - Don’t include PII or secrets in the name - Don’t include dates, versions, or rotation cycle in the name (use tags) - Don’t create multiple secrets with only casing differences Mapping from legacy underscore style If you previously used environment_application_name (with underscores), map as: - Replace underscores with hyphens - Ensure all tokens are lower-case and alphanumeric - Insert purpose/provider tokens to increase clarity Examples: - dev_checkout_connection_string → dev-chkout-connstr-sql - prod_identity_client_secret → prd-id-auth-client-secret-aad - staging_notifications_webhook_github → stg-notif-webhook-gh Recommended tagging strategy Use tags to carry operational metadata: - owner: team or email (e.g., team-identity) - system: system/app name (redundant to app but useful for filters) - rotation: 30d | 60d | 90d | on-demand - sensitivity: public | internal | confidential | restricted - ticket: work item or change request ID - schema: kv-naming-v1 - created-by: pipeline | user UPN - last-reviewed: YYYY-MM-DD - data-class: pci | phi | pii | none Content type - Set contentType for helpful context: application/json, text/plain, application/x-pem-file, application/x-pkcs12 Organizational variants If your platform mandates subset schemas, define profiles: - Minimal: -- - Service integration: --- - Region-aware: ----- Decision tree - Integration secret (to external SaaS)? include provider and scope - Service credential per region? include region and tier - Shared app secret across services? include component or scope to avoid collisions Collision avoidance - Avoid same app + purpose without further qualifiers. Use component or scope tokens to distinguish multiple secrets of same type within one app. Review checklist - Name passes regex and character rules - Tokens are meaningful and from controlled vocabulary - Length < 80 chars (preferred) - No PII or temporal/version info in the name - Appropriate tags set (owner, rotation, sensitivity) Automation hooks - Lint secret names in CI with the regex and token vocab - Block creation when rules fail, allow override with approval tag - Inventory via Azure CLI or SDK and report drift Appendix A: Extended examples - prd-ledger-api-connstr-sql-internal-weu-prem - prd-ledger-api-client-secret-aad-internal - stg-ledger-worker-endpoint-eventhub-ingest-eus2 - dev-ml-train-token-gh-internal - prd-ml-infer-sas-storage-egress-weu - sbx-rpt-batch-connstr-pg-analytics - uat-catalog-web-client-id-aad-external - prd-comms-api-key-sendgrid-external - prd-search-indexer-api-key-cognitive-external-weu - stg-data-pipeline-password-redis-readwrite-eus2 Appendix B: Token vocab (starter) - purpose: connstr, endpoint, api-key, client-id, client-secret, password, sas, token, cert, thumbprint, webhook, private-key, public-key - provider: sql, pg, mysql, redis, cosmos, storage, kv, appins, appcfg, aad, b2c, gh, databricks, eventhub, sb, keycloak, sendgrid, cognitive - scope: internal, external, admin, readonly, readwrite, ingest, egress, analytics References - Azure Key Vault secrets: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/key-vault/secrets/about-secrets - Best practices: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/key-vault/general/best-practices - Name requirements: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/key-vault/general/about-keys-secrets-certificates#name-and-version MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Create three CSV files with sample names for dev, tst, and prd, each including columns: secret_name,purpose,provider,scope,region,tier,variant,notes. Populate 12 representative rows per file to illustrate patterns. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/keyvault_naming/samples/sample_names_dev.csv << 'CSV' secret_name,purpose,provider,scope,region,tier,variant,notes tst-chkout-web-api-key-gh-external,api-key,gh,external,,,testing PAT tst-chkout-api-connstr-sql-internal-weu,connstr,sql,internal,weu,,,- See also - Sample CSVs: /workspace/keyvault_naming/samples/sample_names_dev.csv, /workspace/keyvault_naming/samples/sample_names_tst.csv, /workspace/keyvault_naming/samples/sample_names_prd.csv MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Everything requested is created. Important note about “environment_application_name” - Azure Key Vault secret names do not allow underscores (_). The convention uses hyphens (-) as separators. - Example mapping: environment_application_name → env-app-purpose[-provider[-scope[-region[-tier[-variant]]]]] - dev_checkout_connection_string → dev-chkout-connstr-sql - prod_identity_client_secret → prd-id-auth-client-secret-aad Next steps (optional) - Adopt the provided regex in your CI to lint new secret names. - Define your team’s fixed vocab for purpose/provider/scope to keep names consistent. - Use tags to store metadata like owner, rotation cadence, and sensitivity. body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. content.text: I should confirm Azure AI Search filter syntax and operators (eq, gt, ge, and/or/not, search.in, collection any/all) from official docs before crafting examples and mock responses. Then I’ll create the files in /workspace and verify JSON validity. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Cognitive Search OData $filter syntax eq gt and or not search.in any all collection operators REST API", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Filter and order-by in Azure Cognitive Search","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-query-odata-filter-orderby-syntax"},{"title":"OData expression syntax reference for Azure Cognitive Search","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-query-odata-syntax-reference"},{"title":"search.in function in filters","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-query-odata-collection-operators#searchin"},{"title":"Collection filters using any/all","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-query-odata-collection-operators#any-and-all-operators"},{"title":"Query REST API for Azure Cognitive Search","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-query-rest-api"}],"summary":"Azure Cognitive Search uses OData filter syntax with operators eq, ne, gt, ge, lt, le, and, or, not. Functions like search.in are supported in filters, and collection operators any/all can filter arrays (e.g., tags/any(t: t eq 'sale')). Strings are single-quoted. Booleans are true/false. Filters are passed via the $filter parameter in the REST API request."}} Now I’ll create the folder and write filter_examples.md plus two mock JSON result files that resemble Azure Search REST responses. I’ll include realistic fields and filters using gt/eq/and and search.in/any. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azure_ai_search_filters && cat > /workspace/azure_ai_search_filters/filter_examples.md << 'MD' Azure AI Search — Filter Query Examples Context - Index: products - Sample fields: id (Edm.String), name (Edm.String), price (Edm.Double), brand (Edm.String), category (Edm.String), rating (Edm.Double), inStock (Edm.Boolean), tags (Collection(Edm.String)) - API version: 2024-07-01 (example) Example 1: Numeric + string + boolean - Filter logic: price > 100 AND brand = 'Contoso' AND inStock = true - OData filter: price gt 100 and brand eq 'Contoso' and inStock eq true - REST example: GET https://.search.windows.net/indexes('products')/docs?api-version=2024-07-01&search=*&$filter=price%20gt%20100%20and%20brand%20eq%20'Contoso'%20and%20inStock%20eq%20true - Notes: Use gt for numeric comparison, eq for equality, and true/false for booleans. Strings must be single-quoted. Example 2: Category + rating + brand in set + exclude tag via any/not - Filter logic: category = 'Electronics' AND rating >= 4 AND brand IN {'Contoso','Fabrikam'} AND NOT tags contains 'clearance' - OData filter: category eq 'Electronics' and rating ge 4 and search.in(brand, 'Contoso,Fabrikam', ',') and not tags/any(t: t eq 'clearance') - REST example: GET https://.search.windows.net/indexes('products')/docs?api-version=2024-07-01&search=*&$filter=category%20eq%20'Electronics'%20and%20rating%20ge%204%20and%20search.in(brand,%20'Contoso,Fabrikam',%20',')%20and%20not%20tags/any(t:%20t%20eq%20'clearance') - Notes: search.in provides a set-membership check; any/all are used for collections like tags. MD && cat > /workspace/azure_ai_search_filters/results_filter1.json << 'JSON' { "@odata.context": "https://mysearch.search.windows.net/indexes('products')/$metadata#docs(*)", "value": [ { "@search.score": 1.0, "id": "P-1001", "name": "Contoso Noise-Canceling Headphones", "price": 199.99, "brand": "Contoso", "category": "Electronics", "rating": 4.6, "inStock": true, "tags": ["audio", "wireless", "new"] }, { "@search.score": 0.98, "id": "P-1002", "name": "Contoso 27-inch 4K Monitor", "price": 329.00, "brand": "Contoso", "category": "Electronics", "rating": 4.3, "inStock": true, "tags": ["display", "4k", "productivity"] }, { "@search.score": 0.95, "id": "P-1003", "name": "Contoso Smartwatch Series 5", "price": 249.50, "brand": "Contoso", "category": "Wearables", "rating": 4.1, "inStock": true, "tags": ["wearable", "fitness", "bluetooth"] } ] } JSON && cat > /workspace/azure_ai_search_filters/results_filter2.json << 'JSON' { "@odata.context": "https://mysearch.search.windows.net/indexes('products')/$metadata#docs(*)", "value": [ { "@search.score": 1.0, "id": "P-2001", "name": "Fabrikam Bluetooth Speaker", "price": 89.99, "brand": "Fabrikam", "category": "Electronics", "rating": 4.2, "inStock": true, "tags": ["audio", "portable"] }, { "@search.score": 0.99, "id": "P-2002", "name": "Contoso Wireless Mouse", "price": 29.99, 49.99, "brand": "Contoso", "category": "Electronics", "rating": 4.0, "inStock": true, "tags": ["accessory", "usb-c", "dock"] } ] } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Verify 1966 Sep 28 00:00 filter_examples.md -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1210 Sep 28 00:00 results_filter1.json -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1207 Sep 28 00:00 results_filter2.json Validating JSON...
